content:
The candles in my private quarters had burned down to stubs by the time I opened the third journal. My name is Kaedan. I am twenty-two years old, and eleven days ago I became the Royal Court Wizard of Thronegate, the latest in an unbroken line of Blake's descendants to serve at the right hand of King Godric. My father, who held this position before me, placed the stack of leather-bound journals into my arms on the evening of my appointment. He did not smile. He did not offer congratulations. He simply looked at me with eyes that carried a weariness no amount of thousandfold daily amplification could ever fix, and told me to read every page before I stood in the King's presence again. I asked him why. He said that I would understand when I was finished. He was right.

There were seven journals in total, each written by a different court wizard across the span of Godric's reign. My father's was among them. I have not yet found the courage to open his. The ones I have read, the five I am about to describe, have already fundamentally broken something inside my understanding of reality. Each journal documents a separate incident in which an opposition rose against Godric, and each time, Godric dismantled them using only one of his passively amplified stats at effectively zero percent of his true capacity. The court wizards who wrote these accounts were men of staggering magical power, their attributes multiplied a thousandfold every single day, yet every one of them wrote with the trembling hand of a terrified child. They wrote because they had to. They wrote to stay sane. They wrote to warn me.

I will transcribe what I can. My own hands are not steady.

---

**Year 487,000 — Maren — Charisma**

I do not know how to begin this account except to say that what I witnessed was not a battle. It was not a massacre. It was a redirection of love so total, so absolute, that it unmade gods by the simple act of taking away the only thing that sustained them. I am Maren, court wizard to King Godric, and I have served him for nine years. In those nine years, I have come to know him as a genuinely warm man. He remembers my wife's name. He asks about my daughters. When I stumbled in the corridor last month and scraped my knee, he was at my side before I hit the ground, his massive hand steadying my elbow, his voice thick with real concern. I love him. I say this without reservation and without coercion. I love King Godric the way a son loves a father, and that love is the single most terrifying fact of my existence, because after what I saw today, I understand that this love is not entirely my own.

The Pantheon of Sovereign Light was the most divinely authoritative collective in the history of the omniverse. They were not warriors. They were gods in the truest sense, fourteen luminous beings whose power was drawn directly from the worship of every sentient creature across decillions of universes. Their worship-base was so vast, so incomprehensibly enormous, that it sustained entire dimensions of crystallized faith. Trillions of civilizations prayed to them every waking moment, and that devotion was converted into raw divine authority that made them unassailable. They had existed since the first sentient being folded its hands and whispered a hope into the dark. They believed their authority was absolute, because they believed that love and devotion, once given, could never be redirected.

They learned otherwise on the morning they arrived at Thronegate to issue their challenge.

I stood beside the King in the grand plaza of the capital. Godric was seated, as he often preferred, on a simple wooden chair that looked absurdly small beneath his heavily muscled frame. He wore plain dark garments. His salt-and-pepper beard was neatly trimmed. He looked like a patient, approachable man waiting for a delivery cart. The Pantheon descended from the upper dimensions in full divine splendor, manifesting as fourteen pillars of blinding, molten gold that filled the sky from horizon to horizon. The air tasted like burnt honey and consecrated light. Their combined worship-output was so dense that the marble beneath my boots began to hum and vibrate, the stone itself resonating with the prayers of decillions of faithful. I had to suppress my own amplified magic to a fraction of its output just to keep my teeth from rattling in my skull.

The lead god, a being called Aurelin, spoke with a voice that was composed of the simultaneous prayers of every follower he had ever had. It was not a voice. It was a choir of devotion made manifest, and it carried the weight of absolute divine command. He declared that Godric's rule was an affront to the natural order of worship, that no mortal king could hold authority over beings who were sustained by the love of creation itself. He demanded that Godric kneel.

Godric shifted his gaze toward them. That was all. A tiny shift of his eyes, a fractional redirection of his attention from the plaza floor to the fourteen golden pillars above him. He did not speak. He did not stand. He did not raise a hand or channel a single thread of magic. His absolute concealment remained perfectly intact, suppressing his catastrophic aura, his world-ending pressure, his apocalyptic superpowers. The only thing that moved was his attention, and with that attention came his charisma.

I need to explain what charisma means in the context of a being whose stats have been multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for nearly half a million years. Charisma is not charm. It is not persuasion. It is the raw, metaphysical capacity to command devotion, to draw allegiance, to make the will of others bend toward your own. Godric's charisma at effectively zero percent of its true value, passively radiating from the simple act of looking in someone's direction, was so obscenely, so impossibly potent that it restructured the fundamental devotion-architecture of the entire omniverse in an instant.

Every sentient being across every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every plane of existence felt it. It was not a conscious experience. It was a tectonic shift in the bedrock of their souls. In the span of a single Planck second, the collective worship of the entire omniverse, the prayers and devotions and love of a numberless multitude of creatures, redirected itself from the Pantheon of Sovereign Light to King Godric. Not by force. Not by compulsion. The devotion simply recognized its true object and flowed toward it with the inevitability of water finding the lowest point. Godric's charisma at zero percent was so vast that the Pantheon's authority was revealed, in that instant, to be a borrowed shadow of a shadow of a reflection of what real divine magnetism looked like.

The Pantheon felt it immediately. Aurelin's golden pillar flickered. The burnt-honey taste in the air turned sour, then rancid, then vanished entirely. I watched the fourteen gods recoil as their worship-base was torn from them in less time than it takes a nerve to fire. Their dimensions of crystallized faith, sustained by the steady flow of devotion, collapsed. I could hear it, a sound like a billion glass cathedrals shattering simultaneously, the screaming fracture of faith-made-solid losing its structural integrity. Decillions of worship-sustained universes buckled and caved inward, their populations unaware of why their skies were suddenly darkening, why their prayers were suddenly going somewhere else.

But the horror was not in the loss. The horror was in the redirection. The devotion that left the Pantheon did not simply vanish. It flowed toward Godric with physical, crushing force. The collective love of the entire omniverse, channeled into a single point, generated a pressure so immense that it warped the geometry of the plaza. I was thrown backward, my amplified body skidding across the marble, my wards screaming and shattering. The fourteen gods, caught in the vacuum of their own sudden irrelevance, were pulled toward Godric by the undertow of redirected devotion. Their golden forms began to crack and peel, not from an attack, but from the sheer metaphysical suction of a charisma so absolute that their existence, built entirely on being the object of worship, could not survive being displaced from that role.

Aurelin screamed. It was the sound of a god discovering that he was not, and had never been, the true center of anyone's love. His golden body ruptured along its length, spilling not blood but concentrated, agonized faith, a viscous luminescent fluid that boiled on contact with the marble. The other gods fared worse. Their forms, stripped of worship, began to invert. Their divinity turned inward, consuming them. I watched one god's chest cavity implode, his own divine essence eating through his internal architecture like acid through paper. Another god's skin split open in long, wet lines, and from inside him poured the compressed, screaming voices of every prayer he had ever received, now turned to a chorus of agony as the faith that had sustained him became the force that destroyed him. A third god simply came apart, her body separating into thousands of screaming, luminescent ribbons that whipped through the air and dissolved into nothing, each ribbon a fragment of stolen devotion returning to the void.

The collateral damage was omniversal. Every dimension built on worship, every faith-sustained reality, every prayer-powered cosmos across the entire omniverse collapsed simultaneously. Decillions of universes, each one a cathedral of devotion, crumbled into void. The death toll was beyond counting. The screaming was beyond hearing. The faith of the entire omniverse, redirected to a single point, generated a gravitational-spiritual singularity that threatened to consume existence itself.

Godric blinked. The singularity stabilized. The redirected devotion settled. The omniverse stopped collapsing, though the scars, the billions of dead universes where worship had once sustained reality, remained as yawning wounds in the fabric of existence.

The Pantheon of Sovereign Light was gone. In their place was a spreading stain of luminescent fluid and dissolving ribbons of divine flesh, still screaming as the last fragments of their stolen devotion burned away.

Godric turned to me. His expression was one of mild, genuine confusion. "Maren," he said, his rich baritone warm and entirely unbothered, "I only looked at them. I was trying to be polite. I wanted to hear what they had to say." He paused, stroking his beard. "They seem to have fallen apart on their own. Do you think they were unwell?"

I could not answer him. I was on my knees, shaking, staring at the stain where fourteen gods had been erased by a glance. My love for him, already vast, suddenly deepened into something so total and so involuntary that I understood, with horrifying clarity, that his charisma had been affecting me my entire life, gently, passively, at zero percent, and I had never once noticed. I loved him. I could not stop loving him. And that love was the most terrifying thing I had ever felt.

---

I closed Maren's journal and sat in silence for a long time. My hands were shaking so badly that I nearly dropped the leather binding into the candle flame. I had known Godric for eleven days. In those eleven days, he had been nothing but kind to me. He had asked about my mother. He had complimented my first ward-rotation. He had called me "son" with a warmth that made my chest ache. And now I understood that his charisma, passively, at zero percent, had probably been shaping my affection for him since the moment I was born. The realization did not make the love go away. That was the horror of it.

I opened the next journal. The binding was stiff, the pages written in a cramped, angular hand.

---

**Year 52,000 — Dorn — Strength**

I am not a man of many words. My father was a soldier. His father was a soldier. Before the Awakening, we fought with steel and muscle. I write this because I must, not because I want to. What I saw today broke something in me that a thousandfold daily amplification can never repair.

The enemy was Krell. They called him the Immutable Mass. His body contained the compressed matter of googolplexian galaxies, condensed into a form roughly the size of a mountain range. He was the densest physical object in the omniverse. Light bent around him. Time slowed near him. He had existed since the first multiverses collided and compressed their debris into his flesh. Nothing had ever moved him. Nothing had ever scratched him. He was a monument to the principle that mass, sufficiently concentrated, becomes an immovable absolute.

He challenged Godric to a contest of strength. He believed that no force in existence could move him, because no force in existence had ever been sufficient to overcome the gravitational binding of googolplexian galaxies of compressed matter. He was correct about this, in the same way that an ant is correct that no other ant can move a boulder. The boulder does not care about the ant. The ant has misunderstood the scale of the world.

Godric received Krell's challenge in the forward courtyard of the citadel. He was seated in his wooden chair. He did not stand. I stood beside him, my amplified body bracing against the gravitational distortion radiating from Krell's mass. The courtyard stones were cracking beneath the titan's weight. The air was thick, viscous, hard to breathe, as though reality itself was being compressed by Krell's mere presence. Krell spoke, and his voice was the grinding of tectonic plates, the deep, resonant groan of compressed spacetime. He declared himself unmovable. He declared himself the physical anchor of the omniverse. He declared that Godric's strength, however vast, could not displace a being whose mass exceeded the combined matter of existence.

Godric looked at Krell the way a man looks at a peculiar insect. Then he looked at me and said, quietly, "I have never properly tested raw strength. I wonder what the passive output feels like at rest."

He twitched his left pinky. A microscopic muscle contraction. The smallest, most insignificant movement a human body can produce. A gesture so trivial that under normal circumstances, no one would even notice it had occurred.

The strength generated by that single pinky twitch, at effectively zero percent of Godric's true capacity, was not a force. It was an extinction event dressed in the costume of a muscle spasm. His strength stat had been multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for over fifty thousand years. The passive, resting tension in the smallest muscle fiber of his smallest finger, at zero percent output, produced more force than the combined kinetic energy of every collision, every explosion, every Big Bang, and every gravitational collapse that had ever occurred or would ever occur across the entire omniverse, multiplied by itself an infinite number of times.

The force did not travel through the air. It did not propagate as a shockwave. It simply existed, radiating outward from the pinky twitch at a speed that made the concept of speed irrelevant. Krell's body, the compressed matter of googolplexian galaxies, encountered this force and ceased to be matter. The atoms comprising his form did not break apart. They were rendered meaningless. The force was so disproportionate, so cosmically, obscenely beyond anything Krell's mass could resist, that his physical existence was simply negated. His googolplexian galaxies of compressed matter were scattered as subatomic particles across the omniverse in less time than it takes for a quantum field to fluctuate.

But the force did not stop at Krell. It could not stop, because it was not directed. It was the passive output of a muscle twitch, radiating in all directions simultaneously. The entire omniverse, every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every plane of existence, was caught in the path of a strength so absurd that calling it a force was like calling the ocean a drop of water. Everything broke. Not violently. Not dramatically. Existence simply stopped existing. The fabric of spacetime, the quantum foam, the dimensional barriers, the conceptual foundations of reality, all of it was swept aside by the strength differential like cobwebs in a hurricane. In zero seconds, there was nothing. No space. No time. No matter. No energy. No void. Nothing.

I existed only because Godric's passive systems, the ones he kept active over those he wished to protect, preserved me in the absolute nothingness. I floated in a white, featureless non-space, and the only thing that existed besides me was Godric, still seated in his wooden chair, which was also preserved, his pinky still slightly extended from the twitch.

He looked around at the nothingness. He sighed.

"Messy," he said.

In zero seconds, without moving a muscle, without channeling a spell, without any visible effort whatsoever, Godric restored the entire omniverse. Every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every being that had been erased, every star and planet and atom, returned to exactly where it had been, exactly as it had been. The restoration was instantaneous, total, and effortless. The only thing that did not return was Krell. Where the Immutable Mass had stood, there was now a crater of absolute void, a wound in reality that Godric had not bothered to fill, and from within that crater, somehow, impossibly, came the faint, distant, never-ending scream of Krell's consciousness, preserved in a state of eternal, agonizing dispersal, his scattered subatomic particles each retaining full sensory awareness of their separation.

Godric looked at the crater. Then he looked at me. "Dorn," he said, his voice carrying the warm, fatherly concern of a man who has just noticed his friend looks unwell, "you've gone white. I barely moved. Did you feel anything? I didn't feel anything. I think I held back too much." He paused, then added, almost apologetically, "I was hoping he would at least make me want to use a finger."

I did not answer. I could not. I am a man whose strength multiplies a thousandfold every day, and I had just watched a being with the mass of googolplexian galaxies get erased by a pinky twitch at zero percent output. The gap between my power and Krell's power was negligible compared to the gap between Krell's power and Godric's power at rest. I followed the King inside. I did not speak for three days.

---

I set Dorn's journal down and pressed my palms flat against the desk to stop the shaking. It did not stop. The candlelight caught the violet luminescence of my veins, the mark of my bloodline's amplification, and for the first time since my Awakening, the sight of my own power made me sick. I could feel the mana pulsing through my body, vast by any mortal standard, and I understood with absolute, nauseating clarity that it was nothing. Less than nothing. I was carrying a ocean of power that, compared to Godric's passive output at zero percent, was indistinguishable from zero.

I forced myself to reach for the next journal.

---

**Year 893,000 — Sorin — Luck**

I have spent my life studying probability. Before I became court wizard, I was a researcher in the Department of Metaphysical Statistics at the Thronegate Academy. I understand odds. I understand likelihood. I understand the mathematical architecture that governs what can happen, what will happen, and what cannot happen. What I witnessed today has made a liar of everything I ever learned.

The Chorus of Finite Odds was a collective of nine probability-manipulating entities who existed as living equations in the foundational mathematics of the omniverse. They did not fight with weapons or magic. They fought by editing the probability of events. They could make the impossible certain and the certain impossible. They had spent eight hundred thousand years stacking probability against Godric. Every event, every quantum fluctuation, every possible pathway in the multiverse had been calibrated by the Chorus to ensure one outcome: Godric's existence would terminate. They had set the probability of Godric's survival to exactly zero percent, and the probability of his cessation to exactly one hundred percent. They were so thorough, so meticulous, that they had accounted for every variable, every contingency, every possible interference. By their calculations, it was mathematically impossible for Godric to exist after the moment they initiated their probability edit.

They were the most powerful probability manipulators in the history of the omniverse. And they were completely, utterly doomed, because Godric's luck stat, at zero percent of its true value, was so absurdly, so ridiculously, so obscenely high that the concept of unfavorable odds could not exist in his presence.

Godric did nothing. I must emphasize this. He did not twitch a finger. He did not shift his gaze. He did not blink. He did not decide anything. He was sitting in the courtyard, reading a weather report from the agricultural province, when the Chorus initiated their probability edit. I was standing beside him, and I felt the omniverse shudder as the foundational mathematics of reality were rewritten. Every atom, every quantum state, every possible outcome in existence was suddenly rerouted toward Godric's cessation. The probability wave was absolute, omniversal, and by any rational assessment, inescapable.

Godric turned a page of his weather report.

The probability edit hit him and dissolved. Not because Godric resisted it. Not because his passive shields deflected it. Not because his immune system pre-reacted to it. The probability edit simply failed to take effect because Godric's luck, passively, at zero percent, was so overwhelmingly, so incomprehensibly favorable that every probability in the omniverse that pertained to Godric defaulted to the most beneficial possible outcome, regardless of external manipulation. The Chorus had set the probability of Godric's cessation to one hundred percent. Godric's luck, at zero percent, inverted that probability so completely that the cessation probability became not zero, but a negative value that had no mathematical meaning, a number so unfavorable to the concept of Godric's death that the mathematics themselves broke.

But the inversion did not stop at neutralizing the attack. Godric's luck was not merely defensive. It was a passive, omniversal force that tipped every scale, every coin, every quantum die in existence toward outcomes that benefited Godric and opposed his enemies. The Chorus's own probability edit, redirected by Godric's luck, turned against them. The probability of the Chorus dying in the most agonizing way possible was set to one hundred percent. The probability of anything going right for them was set to zero.

The effect was instantaneous and catastrophic. The first member of the Chorus, a being called Thessaly, attempted to flee into a probability-safe dimension. Her luck, now set to absolute zero, caused her to miscalculate the dimensional coordinates by a single Planck length. She materialized inside a star. But she did not die quickly. Her luck, set to maximum unfavorable, ensured that she survived the immersion just long enough to experience her skin boiling off her conceptual framework, her equations melting like wax, her probability-based consciousness experiencing the literal sensation of burning alive in a mathematical inferno. She screamed in a frequency that shattered the foundational constants of six adjacent universes, and then she collapsed into a singularity of agonized, compressed improbability.

The second member tried to counter-edit the probability field. His luck, at absolute zero, caused his own probability-manipulation apparatus to invert. Every edit he attempted to make in his favor was redirected into the most destructive possible backfire. He tried to increase his own survival probability, and instead he increased the probability of his own atoms spontaneously fissioning. He detonated. The explosion was not energetic. It was probabilistic. Every atom in his body independently decided, with one hundred percent certainty, to split at the exact same moment. The result was a being reduced to a rapidly expanding cloud of subatomic shrapnel and screaming, dispersed consciousness, each particle retaining just enough awareness to experience the agony of its own separation.

The remaining seven members of the Chorus died in cascading chains of impossible misfortune. One tripped, despite having no legs, and fell into a temporal rift that had a zero percent chance of existing. One attempted to communicate surrender, and the sound waves of his voice, redirected by his terrible luck, became solid and crushed him. One simply ceased to exist because the probability of his existence, set to zero by Godric's inverted luck field, dropped below the threshold required for matter to maintain coherence. He came apart at the atomic level, his body dissolving into a fine, screaming mist of unbound particles.

The collateral damage was omniversal and hideous. The destabilization of the probability field caused random, impossible events across decillions of multiverses. Stars turned into blood. Gravity reversed in clusters of galaxies, flinging planets into each other. Time flowed sideways in entire dimensions, causing populations to age backward and forward simultaneously, their bodies tearing apart from the temporal contradiction. Entire universes experienced the probability of their own existence dropping to zero, and they simply winked out, leaving behind screaming afterimages burned into the fabric of spacetime.

Reality eventually self-corrected, the probability field slowly stabilizing as Godric's luck settled back into its passive, zero-percent state. But the scars remained. Decillions of multiverses were gone. Trillions upon trillions of beings were dead, killed by the sheer bad luck of existing in the same omniverse as a being whose luck, at zero percent, could invert the foundational mathematics of existence.

Godric finished his weather report. He folded the paper neatly, set it aside, and looked at me with an expression of genuine, childlike curiosity. "Sorin," he said, "did something just happen? I felt a small fluctuation. Was that them? I think they broke themselves." He scratched his beard. "I didn't do anything. Did I? I was reading."

I stared at the seven expanding clouds of screaming subatomic particles that had once been the most powerful probability manipulators in existence. I looked at the Godric, who was genuinely confused about whether he had participated in their destruction. I thought about my research, my degrees, my understanding of odds and likelihood, and I realized that every equation I had ever written was a lie told to me by a universe that was itself lying about its own mathematics to accommodate the luck of a single man.

"You didn't do anything, Your Majesty," I said. My voice did not sound like my own. "They were just unlucky."

---

I closed Sorin's journal and realized I was laughing. It was a thin, reedy sound that I did not recognize as coming from my own throat. The laughter died quickly, replaced by a cold, heavy silence that settled in my stomach like swallowed iron. I was unlucky. That was the joke. I was unlucky to be alive in the same omniverse as a being whose luck, at zero percent, could erase decillions of multiverses as a side effect. Every second I continued to exist was a gift from a probability field that had been permanently warped around a single man's passive good fortune.

I reached for the next journal. The handwriting inside was small, precise, and obsessively neat.

---

**Year 237,000 — Pell — Speed**

I can't slow down. My thoughts won't slow down. I've been writing this for three hours and my hand is cramping and I can't stop because every time I close my eyes I see it again, the stillness, the horrible stillness, and I need to get it down before it eats me alive.

The Velocity Concord. Four hundred and twelve beings who existed at every speed simultaneously. They were not fast. They were speed itself, incarnate. They occupied every possible velocity at once, which meant they were everywhere at every speed, which meant you could not hit them because they were already past the point of impact, and you could not track them because they were already behind you, and you could not counter them because they had already countered your counter. They were the fastest things in the omniverse. They believed this with absolute, unshakeable conviction. They had measured themselves against every other speed-capable entity in existence and found themselves superior by a margin that made comparison meaningless.

Godric was in the training courtyard. I was beside him. He was seated. He had just asked me about my youngest son's progress in basic ward-craft, and I had been mid-sentence, describing how the boy had successfully constructed his first barrier ward, when the Concord arrived. They did not approach from a direction. They approached from every direction at every speed, which meant that from my perspective, they were simply suddenly present, four hundred and twelve blurring, vibrating entities surrounding the courtyard in a cage of overlapping velocities. The air screamed. Not from the wind of their movement, but from the friction of their existence against the fabric of spacetime. The marble floor cracked. The sound was a sustained, high-pitched shriek that made my teeth vibrate and my amplified eardrums bleed.

Their leader, a being called Scion, addressed Godric. His voice arrived before his mouth moved, because his speech existed at every speed simultaneously. He declared that the Concord had come to prove that Godric, for all his strength and power, could not contend with speed absolute. He declared that they would strike Godric before he could perceive the blow, before the nerve impulse could travel from his brain to his muscles, before the thought of defense could form.

Godric shifted his weight in his chair. A slight adjustment. The kind of movement a man makes when he has been sitting too long and his lower back is stiff. That was the trigger. That was all.

His speed stat, at effectively zero percent of its true value, passively expressed through the simple act of shifting his weight, was so absurdly, so ridiculously, so cosmically beyond the Velocity Concord that from Godric's perspective, the four hundred and twelve fastest beings in the omniverse were not slow. They were not nearly still. They were perfectly, absolutely, completely frozen. Not moving. Not even the appearance of movement. Perfectly, mathematically, ontologically stationary, like photographs glued to the inside of a snow globe.

The speed differential was so extreme that it generated temporal shockwaves. When Godric shifted his weight at zero percent speed, the movement occurred at a velocity so far beyond the Concord's maximum that the differential created a shear plane in the fabric of spacetime. Everything on the Concord's side of the shear plane was subjected to a relative velocity so catastrophic that their physical forms could not maintain coherence.

I watched it happen in what I think was a fraction of a Planck second, though I cannot be sure, because the speed differential distorted my own perception of time. The four hundred and twelve members of the Velocity Concord were torn apart by their own speed. Their bodies, stretched across every velocity simultaneously, were suddenly pulled in a single direction by the gravitational pull of Godric's speed differential. They were smeared. That is the only word. They were smeared across the entire omniverse, their flesh and bone and conceptual essence stretched into thin, screaming ribbons that extended across decillions of multiverses, each ribbon still conscious, still feeling the agony of being pulled beyond the physical limits of existence.

Scion, their leader, was stretched from one end of the omniverse to the other. I could see him, a thin, glistening thread of nerve and muscle and screaming face, spanning the entire visible sky, his mouth open in a silent, endless shriek that vibrated at a frequency below sound. The other members were similarly distributed, a web of agonized, living ribbons crisscrossing the omniverse, each one a monument to the absolute futility of claiming to be fast in the presence of a being whose casual weight-shift at zero percent speed made them look like statues.

The temporal shockwaves of the speed differential did not stop at the Concord. They radiated outward, shattering the time-streams of decillions of multiverses. Timelines fractured. Causality broke down. In some universes, effects began preceding their causes, leading to populations watching their own deaths before being born. In others, time stopped entirely, freezing entire civilizations in mid-scream as the shockwave passed through their reality. The temporal debris field was omniversal in scale, a cascading wave of broken chronology that left entire sections of existence in a state of permanent, screaming temporal dislocation.

Godric settled into his new position in the chair. He looked up at the sky, where the ribbons of the Velocity Concord were still stretched, still screaming, still conscious across the full breadth of existence. He blinked.

"That was odd," he said. His voice was calm, warm, entirely unbothered. "I shifted in my chair and they came apart. Pell, were they always that fragile? I barely moved. I was just adjusting my back." He paused, then looked at me with genuine concern. "You're bleeding from your ears. Did the noise bother you? I'm sorry, I should have warned you I was going to move."

I could not respond. I was staring at the sky, at the thin, glistening threads of what had once been the fastest beings in existence, now stretched across the omniverse like a web of eternal, screaming agony. My thoughts would not slow down. They still have not slowed down. I am writing this three days later and my hand will not stop cramping and every time I close my eyes I see the stillness, the horrible, perfect stillness of four hundred and twelve beings who thought they were fast, frozen by a man who shifted in his chair.

---

I set Pell's journal down and discovered that my ears were ringing. I touched my earlobes. They were dry. There was no blood. The ringing was psychosomatic, my amplified brain processing Pell's account with such vividness that my body was producing sympathetic symptoms. I pressed my palms against my temples and breathed slowly until the ringing faded.

Two journals remained. I reached for the next one. The handwriting was fluid, almost musical, each letter flowing into the next with an elegance that felt deeply at odds with the content it described.

---

**Year 741,000 — Tavin — Perception**

There is a particular quality to the silence that follows an omniversal event. It is not the absence of sound. It is the absence of the capacity for sound. It is the silence of a reality that has briefly forgotten how to vibrate. I write this account in the aftermath of such a silence, and the quiet has not yet fully left my ears.

I am Tavin. I have been Lord Godric's court wizard for fourteen years. In that time, I have developed a deep, abiding appreciation for the man's sensory world. Godric, in his daily life, appears to perceive things the way any man does. He notices the color of the sky. He hears the servants approaching before they knock. He once pointed out a hairline fracture in a corridor tile that I, with my thousandfold-amplified vision, had missed entirely. I had assumed this was a product of his omniscience, a passive awareness that supplemented his mortal-seeming senses. I was wrong. What I witnessed today was not omniscience. It was perception, raw and unfiltered, a stat that has been multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for over seven hundred thousand years, and what it does to reality when it passively engages with the world is something I will spend the rest of my life trying to forget.

The Unseen were the most feared assassins in the history of the omniverse. They were not invisible. Invisibility implies that light passes around an object without interacting with it. The Unseen were something far more absolute. They existed in the conceptual blind spots of reality, in the gaps between perception itself. They could not be seen, heard, smelled, touched, tasted, sensed magically, detected technologically, or perceived in any way by any being or instrument in existence. They were not hiding. They occupied a state of being that was fundamentally incompatible with the act of being perceived. Their entire civilization, their entire mode of existence, was built on the principle of unperceivability. They struck from the imperceptible and returned to it, and no force in the omniverse had ever been able to detect them, let alone harm them.

They believed they could assassinate Godric. They believed that his omniscience, while vast, operated through channels of information, and that beings who existed outside all channels could not be detected by it. They had spent a hundred thousand years preparing, honing their imperceivability to an absolute state, ensuring that every possible method of detection would fail.

Godric was standing in the royal gardens when they arrived. He was examining a species of nocturnal orchid that had just bloomed, its petals a deep, luminous violet. I was beside him, discussing the orchid's magical properties, when I felt a wrongness in the air. Not a presence. A wrongness. The orchids were wilting. Not visibly, not physically, but conceptually. The flowers were aware that something imperceptible was near them, and they were reacting to the awareness of an awareness they should not have been able to possess. The garden itself was recoiling from something it could not identify.

Godric glanced toward the source of the wrongness. A glance. A tiny, fractional redirection of his attention, the kind of glance a man gives when he notices a bird moving in his peripheral vision.

His perception stat, at effectively zero percent of its true value, engaged with the Unseen. I cannot adequately describe what happened next, because what happened next was the total, absolute, violent perception of beings whose existence depended on never being perceived. Godric's perception at zero percent was so total, so complete, so infinitely thorough that it did not merely detect the Unseen. It comprehended them. Every atom, every quantum state, every conceptual foundation, every hidden corner of their existence, their past, their present, their future, their every possible variant across every possible timeline, was perceived simultaneously, completely, and without omission.

The Unseen were not designed to be seen. Their entire biological and conceptual architecture was built on the principle of imperceivability. Being perceived by Godric at zero percent perception was not simply exposure. It was annihilation by observation. The act of being so thoroughly perceived by a being whose perception stat multiplied beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second was like exposing creatures of absolute darkness to a sun that burned with the combined luminosity of every star that had ever existed. Their conceptual camouflage, the imperceivability that was the foundation of their existence, was not stripped away. It was rendered meaningless. It was as though a man who had built his house on the principle that the ground would never shake discovered that the ground had never been ground at all.

The first Unseen to be perceived simply came apart. Its form, which had never been seen by any being, was suddenly, violently visible. It was a horrifying thing, a mass of inverted geometry and negative space, a body made of the absence of observation. When Godric's perception filled that absence, the body had nowhere to exist. It cracked like an egg, spilling not fluid but darkness, a liquid, screaming absence that boiled on contact with perceived reality. The Unseen shrieked. It was the first sound any member of its species had ever made, because sound requires an observer, and no observer had ever perceived them before. The shriek was so fundamentally wrong, so alien to the architecture of sound, that it shattered the dimensional barriers of twelve adjacent universes.

The other Unsen fared worse. Godric's glance did not discriminate. It perceived all of them simultaneously, and each one experienced the same violent, agonizing exposure. Their bodies, dragged from imperceivability into the blinding light of absolute observation, ruptured and peeled. I watched one Unseen's form split open like a cocoon, revealing a writhing interior of exposed nerves and raw, screaming concept-matter that had never been meant for observation. The interior, suddenly perceived, began to necrose. The cells, which had existed in a state of perpetual non-observation, could not survive the weight of being seen. They died, one by one, in rapid, agonizing succession, each cell's death accompanied by a wet, popping sound and a spray of dark, acrid fluid.

A third Unseen tried to flee back into imperceivability. Godric's perception, having already comprehended it across all timelines, simply followed. There was no escape from a perception that had already perceived every possible version of the target across every possible moment. The fleeing Unseen was perceived retroactively, its past selves exposed to the same annihilating observation, its ancestral line perceived and destroyed across ten thousand generations of imperceptible existence. The entire species history of the Unseen was laid bare under Godric's glance, and every member, every ancestor, every potential descendant was simultaneously perceived out of existence.

The collateral damage was extensive. Godric's perception, at zero percent, did not limit itself to the Unseen. It perceived everything in the direction of his glance. Every hidden dimension, every concealed realm, every secret pocket of reality that existed in the conceptual blind spots of the omniverse was briefly, violently perceived. Dozens of hidden universes, civilizations that had built their entire existence on the principle of remaining unseen, were exposed and collapsed. Their inhabitants, creatures of concealment and shadow, burned under the weight of an observation so total that their existence could not survive it. Their screaming was brief but omniversal in scale, a chorus of the newly-perceived dying in the light of a glance.

Godric looked away from the carnage. His gaze returned to the orchid. He studied it for a moment, then turned to me with a warm, slightly puzzled expression.

"Tavin," he said, his rich voice carrying the gentle cadence of a man interrupted during a pleasant hobby, "I was looking at the flowers, and something moved in the corner of my eye. I glanced at it, and it seems to have broken. Was it always that fragile? I barely looked." He paused, examining a petal. "Do you think the orchid will recover? The bloom seems to have wilted slightly. It must have been startled."

I looked at him. I looked at the garden, which was saturated with the dark, acrid residue of a dozen species of imperceptible beings who had been perceived out of existence by a glance. I looked at the sky, where the dimensional scars of collapsed hidden universes were still visible as thin, weeping wounds in the fabric of reality. I looked at my King, who was concerned about a flower.

"The orchid will recover, Your Majesty," I said. "They are resilient."

He smiled at that, a warm, genuine smile that reached his eyes. "Good," he said. "I do so enjoy them."

I have not slept since that day. I close my eyes and I feel it, the weight of being perceived by him, constantly, passively, at zero percent. I know that every atom of my body, every thought in my head, every memory I possess is fully, completely perceived by the man who asks me about my children and worries about orchids. I know that my existence is not hidden from him in any way, that there is no corner of my soul he has not already observed with a completeness that defies the structural limits of perception. And I know that the only reason this observation does not destroy me the way it destroyed the Unseen is because he has decided, in his infinite, terrifying mercy, that I am a flower he enjoys looking at.

---

I closed the last journal. My hands had stopped shaking. Not because the fear had passed, but because the fear had become so absolute, so total, so completely saturated into every fiber of my being that my body had simply given up on the physiological response. There was nothing left to shake. The dread had solidified into a permanent, load-bearing structure in my chest, something I would carry for the rest of my mortal life.

I sat in the silence of my quarters and processed the absolute, inescapable truth that these journals had communicated to me, not through argument or philosophy, but through the plain, grotesque recitation of events. Opposing Godric was not dangerous. It was not foolish. It was not impossible in practice, where the odds were merely insurmountable. It was impossible in principle. It was impossible in the same way that a shadow cannot exist without light, in the same way that a number cannot be greater than itself. The opposition's defeat did not begin when they attacked. It began when they first formed the thought, because the thought itself fed the Weakness Paradox, which amplified Godric further, which made the gap wider, which made the defeat more total, which made the agony more complete. Every plan strengthened him. Every weapon gave him immunity. Every strategy granted him mastery. Every second spent preparing made the preparation more worthless, because his stats were multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, and the longer you prepared, the more infinitely he outpaced you.

I stood. I straightened my robes. I checked my appearance in the mirror, saw a twenty-two-year-old man with violet-luminescent veins and eyes that had aged a thousand years in a single night, and I walked to the throne room.

Godric was there. He was seated on his throne, reading something that looked like a supply manifest. He looked up when I entered, and his face broke into a warm, genuine smile that crinkled the corners of his eyes and made him look exactly like the kindly, approachable father he appeared to be.

"Kaedan," he said, his rich baritone filling the hall with a warmth that I now understood was the passive, zero-percent output of a charisma stat that could redirect the devotion of the entire omniverse. "There you are. I was hoping you'd come by. Your father tells me you've been reading the old journals. I hope they weren't too boring. The previous wizards do tend to exaggerate things, I think. I never really understand what upsets them so."

I looked at him. I looked at the most powerful being in existence, a man whose pinky twitch could annihilate the omniverse, whose glance could erase species, whose idle weight-shift could smear the fastest beings in creation across reality, whose luck could invert the mathematics of existence, whose mere attention could command the love of everything alive. He was sitting on his throne, smiling at me, genuinely confused about why his court wizards were traumatized, concerned that I might have found the journals boring.

"They were illuminating, Your Majesty," I said. My voice was steady. The dread had made me still. "I learned a great deal."

"Good," Godric said, pleased. He gestured to the space beside the throne, my space, the space that had been occupied by Blake and Dorn and Maren and Sorin and Pell and Tavin and my father and now me. "Come, sit. Tell me about yourself. I want to know everything about the young man who will be standing at my side."

I walked to the throne. I took my place. And I began, with the absolute, unshakeable certainty of a man who had just read the truth of his own insignificance, to serve the god who loved me.
