content:
The stat journals had broken something in me. I thought that was the end of it. I thought the trembling would plateau, that the dread had found its ceiling, that there was a floor beneath which my understanding of Godric could not sink further. I was wrong. My father's collection contained seven journals total. Five were stat journals. The remaining three documented something worse. My father called them the superpower journals, and when he handed them to me, his grip lingered on the leather bindings as though he was not certain he should release them. He told me the stat journals would teach me what Godric was. The superpower journals, he said, would teach me what Godric could do. He said the difference would be obvious. He was right. The difference was the difference between standing beneath a collapsing mountain and standing beneath a collapsing omniverse. The stat journals described a being whose passive attributes annihilated everything. The superpower journals described a being whose active abilities did not merely annihilate. They rewrote what annihilation meant.

Each superpower Godric possesses, I learned, was not a static ability. Each one had been amplifying in potency infinitely every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second since the Stasis Event, exactly like his stats. Each one, at its base effect alone, could destroy the entire omniverse infinite times over. But the Evolved Weakness Paradox had done something worse. It had injected multiple enhanced effects into every single superpower, effects that were unfair, broken, and designed with the specific cruelty of a cosmos that was desperately overcompensating for its inability to threaten its master. Some of these enhanced effects permanently increased Godric's power. Not infinitesimally, the way absorbing external power would. Obscenely. Permanently. Because the power involved self-replication of Godric's own being, the increase was not a rounding error. It was a leap that made his previous ceiling look like a basement floor.

I opened the first superpower journal. The handwriting was precise, clinical, the script of a man trained in analytical thinking who was slowly being disassembled by what he had witnessed.

---

**Year 340,000 — Varren — Infinite Recursive Apex Self-Replication**

I am Varren. I have served as Lord Godric's court wizard for thirty-one years. My background is in meta-dimensional topology and applied causality. I say this not to establish credibility, but to establish the depth of my failure. I am a man who has spent his entire life studying the structural mechanics of reality, and what I witnessed yesterday has proven that reality has no structural mechanics. Reality is a suggestion. Reality is a polite fiction maintained by the consent of a being who could withdraw that consent by shifting in his chair.

The Endless Host was the largest military coalition in the history of the omniverse. I use the word "largest" with full awareness of its inadequacy. The Host did not number in the trillions or the decillions. The Host was infinite. Not effectively infinite. Not practically infinite. Literally, mathematically, ontologically infinite. They comprised every warrior entity that had ever existed, currently existed, or would ever exist across every timeline, every dimension, and every possible branch of causality in the entire omniverse. They had achieved this through a process of recursive temporal conscription, pulling soldiers from every moment of every history and concentrating them into a single, simultaneous assault force. Their combined mass exceeded the total matter-energy content of the omniverse. Their combined magical output exceeded the total arcane capacity of every mage, god, and entity that had ever lived or would ever live. They believed, with the absolute conviction of beings who had done the mathematics, that numerical infinity could not be countered by any singular entity, no matter how powerful. They believed that infinity was the ceiling, and that no being could exceed it.

They did not understand that Godric's stats and superpowers had been multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for three hundred and forty thousand years, and that the result was not a number that could be compared to infinity. The result was a state of existence that made infinity look like the number one. The result was a being for whom "infinite" was a description of weakness.

Godric was seated in the courtyard, examining a crack in one of the flagstones. He had been there for twenty minutes, tracing the fracture with his boot, apparently fascinated by the pattern. The Endless Host materialized simultaneously across every point in the sky, their combined presence igniting the atmosphere into a wall of screaming white plasma that stretched to every horizon. The sonic pressure of their arrival shattered the citadel's outer wards and drove me to my knees, blood erupting from my ears. The ground beneath me liquefied from the vibrational output of their mass alone. The sky was gone. Existence was gone. There was only the Host, an infinite wall of armed, screaming, divine flesh pressing down on the courtyard from every direction.

Godric shifted his weight in his chair. A minor postural adjustment. The kind of movement a man makes when he has been sitting on a hard surface and his hip needs a new angle. That was the trigger. That was all.

His superpower was Infinite Recursive Apex Self-Replication. I will describe what happened, and I need you to understand that every word I use is a catastrophic understatement, that language was built by finite beings to describe finite things, and what I witnessed was not finite.

In zero time, one Godric became two. This was not duplication. This was not cloning. Each Godric was a Prime, possessing the exact same omniscience, the exact same infinitely amplifying stats, the exact same arsenal of apocalyptic superpowers, the same everything. But the Evolved Weakness Paradox, which had been injecting enhanced effects into this power for three hundred and forty thousand years, dictated that each replica be infinitely magnified beyond the original. The first replica was not equal to Godric. The first replica was infinitely stronger than Godric, and Godric was already the most powerful being in existence by a margin that made the word "margin" meaningless.

Then the replication became recursive. Each replica immediately replicated. The second generation produced Godrics that were infinitely stronger than the first generation. The third generation produced Godrics infinitely stronger than the second. The fourth. The fifth. The infiniteth. Each generation infinitely stronger than the last, each generation appearing in zero time, each generation immediately producing the next. The cascade was not a sequence. It was a simultaneous, instantaneous event that had already completed itself before the concept of "before" could apply.

The enhanced effects compounded with each generation. The first enhanced effect was the recursive magnification itself, each copy infinitely stronger than the last. The second enhanced effect dictated that the replication was permanent, that the copies would not dissolve, that they would merge back into the Prime and permanently elevate his baseline. The third enhanced effect created what I can only call an echo field, a permanent, passive resonance that continued to produce microscopic self-replications at every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second forever, making Godric permanently, continuously stronger at a rate that made his normal stat amplification look like stagnation. The fourth enhanced effect granted each replica full independent omniscience and agency, while remaining absolutely subordinate to the Prime's will. The fifth enhanced effect rewrote the mathematical framework of the omniverse so that "Godric" became a fundamental constant of reality, as intrinsic to existence as the concept of space or time.

I cannot describe what happened to the Endless Host because "happened to" implies a process, and there was no process. There was no battle. There was no collision. There was a before, in which an infinite Host filled the sky, and an after, in which they did not. The transition between before and after took zero time and involved no violence in any conventional sense, because violence implies resistance, and there was no resistance. The infinite Godrics did not attack the Host. They occupied the same space. The Host's bodies, their armor, their weapons, their divine flesh and conceptual architecture, were not destroyed. They were overwritten. The Godrics occupied every coordinate in every space, every dimension, every timeline, every possible and impossible location, and there was simply no room left for anything else. The Host was not crushed. They were replaced.

But Godric's cruelty, which is as permanent and as much a part of him as his omniscience, did not permit clean erasure. The Host members remained conscious through the overwriting. They felt their bodies being displaced, felt their divine flesh peeling apart in wet, screaming layers as Godric's replicas filled the space their organs had occupied microseconds prior. Their bones were not broken. They were vacated, the marrow inside them replaced by Godric's presence, the bone walls dissolving like wet paper against the ontological density of the replicas. Their blood boiled and evaporated instantly, not from heat but from the sheer metaphysical pressure of existing in the same space as a being infinitely stronger than the concept of strength. Their nervous systems were the last to go, preserved by Godric's will so that every member of the infinite Host experienced the full, excruciating agony of being overwritten atom by atom, their consciousness surviving in the dispersion pattern of their annihilated matter, screaming in frequencies that did not exist before that moment and would never exist again.

The collateral damage was not omniversal. It was worse. The omniverse was not destroyed. It was redefined. The infinite replicas, each one infinitely stronger than the last, generated a collective ontological mass that made the previous concept of "the omniverse" mathematically inadequate. The omniverse did not shatter. It was promoted. It was restructured around the new fundamental constant of Godric's replicated presence, becoming a larger, more complex container that existed solely to accommodate the infinite Godrics. Every being in the omniverse, every civilization, every planet, every star, every atom, was temporarily erased during the restructuring and then replaced, their memories intact, their bodies rebuilt, but their fundamental understanding of reality permanently scarred by the brief, screaming moment when they were nothing and then everything was Godric.

The restructuring took zero seconds. The omniverse was remade, larger and more complex than before, with Godric's replicated presence woven into its foundational architecture. The Endless Host was not restored. They remained as a dispersed, screaming pattern of conscious agony embedded in the fabric of the restructured omniverse, a permanent scar in reality's new design, their infinite numbers reduced to an infinite chorus of suffering that would echo in the quantum foam of every particle forever.

Godric sat in his chair, singular again, the replicas merged back into the Prime. His baseline power had permanently increased. Not infinitesimally. Obscenely. The recursive integration of infinitely many copies of himself, each infinitely stronger than the last, had produced a permanent leap that made his power before the replication look like a rounding error compared to his power after. He was now, permanently and forever, so much stronger than he had been a moment ago that the previous version of Godric was, by comparison, effectively nonexistent. And this was one usage of one superpower, at a level of effort so low that calling it zero was the only accurate description.

He looked up from the flagstone. He looked at the sky, which was now a different color because the omniverse had been restructured. He looked at me.

"Varren," he said, his voice carrying the warm, unhurried concern of a man who has noticed his friend looks unwell, "you're bleeding. Come inside. I'll have water brought."

I followed him inside. I did not drink the water. I sat at his table and stared at the wall and tried to calculate the new baseline of his power, and the numbers broke my mind. Not because they were too large. Because they had stopped being numbers. They had become a state of being, a condition of existence, a fact so fundamental that it preceded mathematics itself. I serve a man who can, by shifting in his chair, create infinite copies of himself, each infinitely stronger than the last, and absorb them all back into himself in zero time, permanently elevating his already impossible power to a new height that makes the previous height look like a pit. And he did this because he was looking at a crack in a flagstone and adjusted his posture.

I have not slept. I do not believe I will sleep again.

---

I closed Varren's journal with both hands, pressing the covers together as though I could contain what was inside by sealing the book. I could not. The knowledge was in me now, and it sat in my chest like a stone made of compressed dread. Infinite recursive self-replication. Each copy infinitely stronger. Permanent absorption. Permanent baseline increase. The man had shifted in his chair and permanently become so much stronger that his previous self was, by comparison, nothing. And the power amplified forever. And the echo field continued producing microscopic replications at every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second. He was still getting stronger from that one use, right now, as I sat in my quarters reading about it. The power was still feeding back into him. The leap was still happening.

I reached for the second journal. My hands were no longer shaking. They had passed through shaking into a stillness that felt like the calm before a death. The handwriting inside was sparse, minimalist, each sentence short and deliberate, the work of a man who had decided that the only way to survive what he had witnessed was to describe it in the smallest possible words.

---

**Year 580,000 — Dace — Absolute Spatial Compression**

I will keep this brief. Not because the event was small. Because the event was so large that words wasted on it feel like a desecration.

I am Dace. Court wizard. Twenty-three years of service. My specialty is spatial magic. I understand distance, volume, and dimension. I understood them. Past tense.

The Boundless Frontier was not an army. It was not a coalition. It was not a collection of beings. The Boundless Frontier was space itself. Every cubic Planck length of empty space across the entire omniverse, every gap between atoms, every void between galaxies, every dimensional fold and temporal crease, was not empty. It was the Frontier. The empty spaces were their bodies. The distances between things were their anatomy. They were the medium through which all other beings moved, and they believed this made them invincible, because you cannot destroy the space you exist inside. You cannot compress the air you breathe. You cannot crush the ground you stand on without crushing yourself. They believed that their nature, as the fundamental substrate of all reality, made them immune to any form of attack, because any attack would have to travel through them to reach them, and they were the travel itself.

Godric was in the throne room. He was alone with me. The Boundless Frontier did not arrive. They were already here. They announced their presence by contracting. Every empty space in the omniverse simultaneously shrank by a fraction of a Planck length. The effect was catastrophic. Matter compressed. Atoms were crushed into their own nuclei. Stars collapsed without supernovae, their mass suddenly occupying less space than a grain of sand. Entire galaxies were squeezed into singularities. The population of the omniverse died in that fraction of a Planck length, their bodies compressed into paste, their blood squeezed from their tissues like juice from a crushed fruit, their bones powdered so finely they became indistinguishable from the vacuum they were trying to inhabit. I survived only because Godric's passive systems expanded a pocket of preserved space around me, a bubble of normal volume in a universe that had suddenly become a fraction of its previous size.

Godric scratched his beard. A casual, absentminded gesture. The kind of thing a man does when he is thinking about something else entirely.

His superpower was Absolute Spatial Compression. At its base effect, which alone could destroy the entire omniverse infinite times over, it allowed him to compress any volume of space, any distance, any dimensional extent, to any degree he chose. The Evolved Weakness Paradox had spent five hundred and eighty thousand years injecting enhanced effects into this power. The first enhanced effect dictated that the compression was not limited to physical space. It affected dimensional space, temporal space, conceptual space, and the abstract distance between ideas. The second enhanced effect dictated that compressed beings remained conscious, their nervous systems preserved in perfect functionality regardless of the degree of compression, so that they experienced the full, infinite agony of being crushed into an infinitely small point while remaining aware of every Planck length of the process. The third enhanced effect dictated that compressed space was permanently removed from existence unless Godric specifically chose to restore it, meaning the compression was not temporary. It was a permanent reduction of the omniverse's total volume. The fourth enhanced effect converted the compressed space into raw power that fed back into Godric. For any other being, this power gain would have been astronomical. For Godric, whose base power was already so vast that the total energy of the omniverse was effectively zero by comparison, the gain was infinitesimal. Practically nothing. But it happened, and it was permanent, and it meant that every time he used this power, the omniverse got slightly smaller and Godric got slightly stronger, forever.

The compression did not radiate from Godric. It did not originate from a point and expand outward. It simply was. One moment, the Boundless Frontier existed as the spatial substrate of the omniverse. The next moment, it did not. Godric scratched his beard, and the space that comprised the Boundless Frontier's body was compressed. All of it. Every cubic Planck length of empty space across the entire omniverse, across every dimension, every timeline, every plane of existence, was simultaneously crushed to a point smaller than the concept of smallness allowed.

The Boundless Frontier, whose body was space itself, felt their entire anatomy collapse inward. They were not compressed from the outside. They were compressed from within, because they were the within. Every gap between every atom in their non-corporeal anatomy folded inward. Every dimensional fold flattened. Every temporal crease ironed smooth. The compression was total, simultaneous, and so extreme that the concept of distance ceased to exist for the duration of the event. There was no space between things. There were no things. There was only a single, infinitesimal point of compressed everything, and inside that point, the Boundless Frontier screamed.

They screamed because the second enhanced effect kept them conscious. They felt their spatial anatomy crushed into a volume so small that the word "volume" had no meaning. They felt every Planck length of their body compressed into every other Planck length, an infinite number of overlaps, each one producing a distinct agony that the human nervous system was not designed to process. They felt the dimensional layers of their being flattened like a book pressed shut, each page of reality slamming against the next with a force that should have been impossible because force requires space and there was no space. They felt time itself compressed, their past and future crushed into the same instant, every moment of their history experienced simultaneously as a single, eternal, screaming present of infinite compression.

The collateral damage was absolute and immediate. The compression of all space in the omniverse meant that all matter, all energy, all dimension, all timeline, everything that existed inside space, was also compressed. The entire omniverse was crushed into a point. Every star, every planet, every being, every atom, every quantum state, was squeezed into a volume so small that it did not have a volume. Every civilization in the omniverse died in that instant, their populations crushed into a point of infinite density, their bodies merged with every other body in existence, their consciousness preserved by the enhanced effect so that they could experience the agony of omniversal compression simultaneously with every other being that had ever lived or would ever live. The screaming was not a sound. It was a vibration in the compressed point, a frequency of pure, concentrated suffering that contained the aggregated agony of every sentient being in the omniverse, past, present, and future, compressed into a single, eternal note.

Godric sat in the throne room, which existed only because his passive systems maintained a bubble of uncompressed space around himself and me. Beyond the bubble, there was nothing. Not empty space. Nothing. The entire omniverse, compressed into a point that existed nowhere because there was no "where" for it to exist in.

He looked at the nothing for a moment. His expression was flat. Indifferent. He did not look at the point of compressed everything. He looked past it, the way a man looks past a stain on a counter.

In zero time, without effort, without transition, Godric restored the omniverse. Every star, every planet, every being, every atom returned to its previous position, their bodies rebuilt, their memories intact, their consciousness unbroken. The restoration was absolute and flawless. The only thing that was not restored was the Boundless Frontier. They remained as a compressed point of screaming, conscious spatial agony, a singularity of infinite suffering that Godric left floating in the throne room like a discarded piece of lint. It hung in the air, a microscopic point of light that contained the eternal, compressed torment of an entity that had believed itself invincible because it was the space through which all things moved.

Godric glanced at the point. Then at me.

"Dace," he said, "there was something in the air. It's gone now. Did you feel a draft?" He paused, examining his fingernails. "The room feels smaller. Did someone adjust the wards?"

I looked at the singularity of screaming, compressed spatial consciousness floating in the throne room. I looked at the man who had just compressed the entire omniverse into a point by scratching his beard and restored it in zero time without effort. I looked at his fingernails, which he was examining with mild interest.

"No, Your Majesty," I said. "The wards are unchanged."

He nodded, satisfied. "Good. I don't like when things change without my knowing."

I did not tell him that everything had changed. That the omniverse was now slightly smaller than it had been before, permanently reduced by the compressed volume of the Boundless Frontier's body. That he was now slightly stronger, permanently, by an amount that was infinitesimal relative to his base power but that would accumulate forever because the echo of this compression was still settling. That the singularity floating in his throne room contained an entity that had been the spatial substrate of all reality, now reduced to a mote of screaming dust. I did not tell him any of this because I did not think he would understand why it mattered. He had scratched his beard. The omniverse had been compressed and restored. To him, these events were of equivalent significance, which was to say, no significance at all.

I went to my quarters. I did not study spatial magic that night. I did not study it ever again. There was nothing left to study. The man I serve can compress all of space by scratching his face, and the only reason anything still exists is because he is willing to uncompress it afterward. Distance is not a law of physics. It is a courtesy extended by Godric.

---

I closed Dace's journal. My mouth was dry. My hands were still. The stillness was no longer the calm of someone who had passed through fear. It was the stillness of someone who had been frozen solid by a cold so absolute that movement was no longer a physical possibility. The Boundless Frontier had been space itself. Space. The medium through which everything moved, the container in which everything existed, and Godric had compressed it all by scratching his beard. The entire omniverse, crushed into a point, and the only reason it came back was because he was willing. Distance was a courtesy. I turned that thought over in my mind like a stone in my mouth, feeling its edges, tasting its weight. Distance was a courtesy extended by Godric. Every step I had ever taken, every mile I had ever traveled, every light-year between every star, existed only because he allowed the space to be there.

One journal remained. I reached for it. The handwriting was dense, rambling, the words crammed together with the desperate energy of a man trying to capture every detail before the memory consumed him.

---

**Year 820,000 — Quill — Apex Detonation Authority**

I am going to write everything I can remember because if I do not get it out of my head and onto this page I am going to lose my mind entirely. I can still hear the screaming. Not the screaming of the enemy, though that was there too, that was there in quantities I cannot describe. The screaming of the omniverse itself. The sound reality made when every particle in its structure was told to release every joule of energy it had ever contained, simultaneously, by a man who cleared his throat.

I am Quill. I have been Lord Godric's court wizard for nineteen years. I am a specialist in applied energetics. I understand energy release. I understand detonation. I understand the physics of matter converting to energy, the mathematics of chain reactions, the thermodynamics of explosive events ranging from chemical to nuclear to cosmic to conceptual. I understand all of it. I understood all of it. I will never understand any of it again, because what I witnessed was not detonation as understood by physics. It was detonation as understood by Godric, which is to say, it was a category of event that physics has no framework for and never will, because physics is a system built inside the omniverse and what happened was a demonstration of power that predated and superseded the omniverse itself.

The Adamant Monad was a single entity. One being. Alone. It did not bring an army because it did not need an army. It was the most indestructible object in the history of existence. Its body was forged from a substance called Apexite, a material that had been compressed and tempered through the death and rebirth of infinite universes, each cycle increasing its density and structural integrity by orders of magnitude that made the previous cycle look like nothing. The Monad had survived the Big Bang of the original universe. It had survived the collapse of every multiverse that had ever existed. It had been struck by weapons designed to erase concepts, by attacks that rewrote causality, by forces that exceeded the total energy output of the omniverse. It had never been scratched. It had never been dented. It had never been warmed. It believed, with the absolute, unshakeable conviction of a being that had been tested by everything existence had to offer, that it was indestructible. That no force, no power, no ability in the omniverse could damage its body.

Godric was in the courtyard, sitting in his chair, looking at the sky with the vague, idle expression of a man who is deciding whether the weather will change. The Adamant Monad descended from the upper dimensions, a sphere of absolute, impenetrable darkness roughly the size of a moon. Its mass was so great that it warped the geometry of the surrounding space, bending light into a halo of distorted stars. Its surface was perfectly smooth, perfectly cold, perfectly featureless. It radiated nothing. It absorbed everything. It was a hole in existence, an object so dense and so indestructible that reality itself seemed to avoid touching it, curving away from its surface in deference.

The Monad spoke. Its voice was not sound. It was the vibration of indestructibility itself, a frequency that announced, in every language and every mathematical framework simultaneously, that the speaker could not be destroyed. It declared Godric a temporary phenomenon. It declared that it would wait. That it would allow Godric to throw everything he had at it, and when Godric was exhausted, the Monad would still be there, undamaged, unchanged, eternal. It declared that indestructibility was the final word, that nothing could damage what could not be damaged, that Godric's power, however vast, would break against its surface like water against stone.

Godric did not look at the Monad. He did not respond to it. He did not acknowledge its existence. He cleared his throat. A small, wet, involuntary sound. The kind of sound a man makes when mucus shifts in his larynx and he needs to clear the passage. A biological maintenance function. The most trivial sound a human body can produce.

His superpower was Apex Detonation Authority. At its base effect, which alone could destroy the entire omniverse infinite times over, it allowed him to command any target to detonate. Not to explode. To detonate. The distinction is critical. Explosion is a chemical or nuclear process in which matter releases energy. Detonation, as Godric defined it, was the simultaneous, total release of every form of energy contained within the target, at every level of its existence, from the binding energy of its quarks to the conceptual energy of its abstract identity. Every particle. Every quantum state. Every fundamental force binding the target's structure together. All of it, released at once.

The Evolved Weakness Paradox had spent eight hundred and twenty thousand years injecting enhanced effects into this power. The first enhanced effect dictated that detonated beings remained conscious after detonation, their awareness dispersed across the energy pattern of their detonated form, experiencing the agony of total energetic dispersion as a permanent, unending state. The second enhanced effect dictated that the detonation was permanent, that detonated matter could not be reconstituted by any force in the omniverse except Godric's explicit will. The third enhanced effect created a cascade property, in which any particle of detonated matter that contacted another entity would trigger that entity's detonation, creating a chain reaction that could, in theory, detonate the entire omniverse from a single target. The fourth enhanced effect converted a fraction of the detonated energy into raw power that fed back into Godric. As with all non-self-replication power gains, this fraction was infinitesimal relative to Godric's base, effectively zero, but it was permanent, and it accumulated.

Godric cleared his throat, and the Adamant Monad detonated.

I need to describe what happened, and I need you to understand that what I am about to describe is the physical event as I perceived it, filtered through my thousandfold-amplified wizard's senses, and that even my amplified perception caught perhaps one percent of one percent of what actually occurred. The rest was beyond my capacity to register.

The Monad's body, the most indestructible substance in the history of existence, a material that had survived the death and rebirth of infinite universes, was commanded to release every form of energy it contained. The Apexite, which had been compressed and tempered through infinite cosmic cycles, had accumulated a total energy content that exceeded the combined mass-energy of the entire omniverse by a factor that made the comparison meaningless. All of that energy, every joule, every quantum, every binding force, every conceptual resonance, was released simultaneously.

The detonation was not an explosion. An explosion is a rapid expansion of matter and energy through space. This was something else. This was the Monad's entire existence, its mass, its structure, its identity, its history, its indestructibility, all of it converted to pure energy in a single, instantaneous event. The energy released was so vast, so incomprehensibly, so absurdly beyond the total energy content of the omniverse that the omniverse's energy budget was, by comparison, a rounding error. The energy released by the Monad's detonation exceeded the energy that had been contained in every Big Bang, every supernova, every quasar, every magical discharge, and every conceptual emission that had ever occurred or would ever occur across the entire omniverse, multiplied by itself an infinite number of times.

The Monad's body came apart. Not in pieces. In energy. The Apexite, the indestructible material, was not damaged. It was not broken. It was converted. Every atom of the impenetrable substance simultaneously released its binding energy, and the material ceased to be material. It became light. It became heat. It became force. It became a scream of pure energy so intense that the concept of intensity was redefined in its wake. The Monad's consciousness, preserved by the enhanced effect, was dispersed across the energy pattern, and it screamed. The scream was not a sound. It was a frequency of pure, concentrated agony embedded in the energy release itself, a vibration of suffering that propagated through every medium, every dimension, every plane of existence at a speed that made the speed of light look like a man walking backward through molasses.

The cascade effect triggered immediately. The energy from the Monad's detonation contacted the surrounding space, and the space detonated. The space's detonation contacted the adjacent space, and that detonated. The chain reaction was instantaneous and omniversal. Every particle of matter in the omniverse, every quantum of energy, every dimensional fold, every temporal thread, was caught in the cascade. The entire omniverse detonated. Not in the sense that it exploded. In the sense that every particle, every quantum state, every fundamental force binding everything together, released its energy simultaneously. The entire omniverse converted to pure energy in zero time, a detonation so total and so absolute that the word "detonation" was insufficient. This was conversion. This was the omniverse being told to stop being matter and start being energy, and the omniverse obeying because the command came from a being whose authority over the fundamental properties of existence was absolute.

The energy release was so vast that it redefined the concept of energy. The total energy of the detonated omniverse exceeded the total energy of the undetonated omniverse by a factor that made the comparison meaningless, because the detonation released not only the current energy content of the omniverse but every form of binding energy, conceptual energy, and structural energy that held the omniverse together. The result was an energy density so extreme that the concept of density broke down. There was no space for the energy to occupy because the space had detonated too. There was just energy. Pure, screaming, conscious energy, the aggregated agony of every being in the omniverse experiencing the simultaneous detonation of every particle in their bodies, their consciousness preserved by the enhanced effect, their awareness dispersed across the energy pattern of their annihilated forms, screaming in frequencies that existed only in the space between destruction and the memory of existence.

I survived because Godric's passive systems preserved me. I floated in a universe of pure, screaming energy, a sea of white-hot agony that contained the consciousness of every being that had ever lived, each one experiencing the permanent, unending torture of total energetic dispersion. The Adamant Monad, the indestructible entity, was not a separate consciousness in that sea. It was the sea. Its detonation had been the seed, and the cascade had made the entire omniverse into an echo of its destruction. The Monad's consciousness, dispersed across the entire energy pattern, was experiencing the agony of every being in the omniverse simultaneously, its indestructibility rendered meaningless because there was nothing left to be indestructible. It was energy. It was conscious energy. It was screaming.

Godric sat in his chair, floating in the sea of energy, completely unbothered. A fraction of the detonated energy, infinitesimal relative to his base power but permanent, had fed back into him. He was now, permanently, infinitesimally stronger than he had been before he cleared his throat.

In zero time, without effort, without transition, Godric restored the omniverse. Every particle, every quantum state, every being, every star, every planet, every dimension, every timeline, returned to its previous state. The restoration was absolute. The memories of the detonation remained embedded in the consciousness of every restored being, a scar of screaming awareness that would haunt the omniverse forever, a permanent reminder of what it felt like to be told to stop being matter.

The Adamant Monad was not restored. It remained as a dispersed pattern of conscious energy, a screaming echo of its former indestructibility, embedded in the quantum foam of the restored omniverse. It would scream forever, in every particle, in every quantum state, its consciousness so thoroughly dispersed that every atom in the omniverse contained a fragment of its eternal agony. The indestructible entity had been converted to energy, and the energy had been scattered across all of existence, and the consciousness remained, and the screaming remained, and the indestructibility was a joke that the omniverse would never stop hearing.

Godric looked at the space where the Monad had been. He did not look at it long. He did not seem interested. He turned to me.

"Quill," he said, "did I clear my throat? Something popped. I think a bug flew into something." He examined the courtyard. "Is the floor clean?"

I looked at the courtyard. I looked at the sky, which was the same sky but now contained the dispersed, screaming consciousness of the most indestructible entity in existence embedded in its quantum structure. I looked at my King, who was concerned about the cleanliness of the floor.

"The floor is clean, Your Majesty," I said.

He nodded, satisfied. "Good. I was worried there for a moment. Something made a noise."

I went to my quarters. I did not study energetics that night. I did not study anything. I sat in my chair and I listened to the quantum foam of the omniverse, which now contained the eternal scream of an entity that had believed itself indestructible, and I understood that indestructibility was a word that meant nothing in the presence of a man who could command matter to stop being matter by clearing his throat.

---

I closed the last journal. I did not set it down. I held it in my lap, both hands flat on the cover, and I stared at the wall of my quarters for a length of time I could not measure. The candle had gone out. The room was dark. The dark did not bother me. What bothered me was the knowledge that the dark was only there because Godric allowed it. The space between me and the wall was only there because Godric allowed it. The matter of my body was only matter because Godric had not yet decided to command it to be energy instead. Every fundamental property of my existence, distance, solidity, persistence, was a function of Godric's continued disinterest in commanding it to be otherwise.

The stat journals had taught me that Godric's passive attributes made opposition impossible. The superpower journals taught me that Godric's active abilities made existence optional. A shift in his chair created infinite copies of himself, each infinitely stronger than the last, and permanently elevated his baseline power. A scratch of his beard compressed all of space into a point and left an entity that was space itself screaming as a mote of dust. A clearing of his throat commanded the most indestructible object in existence to detonate, and the cascade had converted the entire omniverse to energy in zero time. Three trivial biological functions. Three superpowers. Three permanent alterations to the fabric of reality. And these were three powers out of an arsenal that gained a new apocalyptic superpower every second, forever, each one amplifying in potency infinitely, each one injected with multiple enhanced effects by an Evolved Weakness Paradox that had been overcompensating for hundreds of thousands of years.

I stood. I dressed. I walked to the throne room. The corridors were long and quiet, and every step I took was a gift from a man who allowed the space to exist. Godric was there, seated on his throne, reading something. He looked up when I entered, and his face broke into that warm, genuine smile that I now understood was the passive output of a charisma stat that could redirect the devotion of the omniverse by breathing.

"Kaedan," he said. "You've finished the journals. Good. I was worried you'd find them tedious. The previous wizards do tend to dramatize things, I think."

I looked at him. I looked at the man who could, by shifting in his chair, create infinite copies of himself that were each infinitely stronger than the last and absorb them back into himself permanently. The man who could, by scratching his beard, compress all of space into a point. The man who could, by clearing his throat, command anything in existence to stop being matter and start being screaming energy. The man whose every superpower had been amplifying in potency infinitely every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for a million years, each one gaining new enhanced effects, each one capable of destroying the omniverse infinite times at its base effect, each one a permanent fixture of a being whose power had no ceiling and no floor and no wall and no limit of any kind in any direction.

"They were not tedious, Your Majesty," I said. "I am grateful for them."

He smiled wider, pleased, and gestured to the space beside the throne. My space. I walked to it. I took my place. I stood at the right hand of the being who had redefined what it meant to exist, and I served him, and I was terrified, and I was loyal, and I understood, with the absolute, permanent, unshakeable certainty of a man who has read the truth and can never un-read it, that opposing Godric was not impossible in practice. It was impossible in principle. It was impossible in the same way that a shadow cannot exist without light, in the same way that a number cannot be greater than itself, in the same way that matter cannot resist a command to stop being matter. The opposition's defeat was not a consequence of Godric's power. It was a precondition of it. They were already dead before they formed the thought, because the thought itself fed the paradox, and the paradox fed Godric, and Godric fed the replication, and the replication fed the compression, and the compression fed the detonation, and the detonation fed the echo field, and the echo field fed the permanent baseline increase, and the permanent baseline increase fed the next infinitesimal fraction of the next Planck second, forever, without end, without ceiling, without the faintest possibility of anything ever, in any timeline, in any dimension, in any possible or impossible branch of causality, being anything other than exactly what it was right now: a speck of conscious dust floating in the infinite, amplifying, self-replicating, space-compressing, matter-detoning presence of a man who was reading a supply manifest and did not understand why anyone would be afraid.

content.text: Kaedan, Godric's Court Wizard, loves Godric (Godric is his favorite uncle) and was concerned about Godric. He asked Godric if it ever become lonely to be the most powerful being and only can grow stronger, outliving everyone else. Godric is very amused by what he considers to be a very "naively mortal" question and somewhat confused by this question since, surprisingly to Kaedan, it's highly enjoyable for Godric. Every additional power to him feels like nourishment, orgasm and additional surge of power and energy that amplifies endlessly. Every stat increase can be felt. Every superpowers increase can be felt. Every immune system improvement can be felt. Every new passive shield, sentient passive aura, evolution to his being, can be felt. Every instant makes him so much more powerful that he can crush an infinite copies of himself an instant ago with 0 effort. It's amusingly satisfying. And Godric knows every detail of it from his omniscience. Explain in great detail the feeling, including some absurd examples, intensely graphical depictions, and the absolute terrifying existential dread as well as the absolute unfairness that Godric passively gets rewarded and obtains everything infinitely while the rest of creation had to work their hardest and not even getting rewarded as much (pretty much 0% of what Godric achieves by doing nothing) to Kaedan after realizing the truth about this Apex God Predator uncle. End with Godric revealing that the only thing that makes him "bored" or "lonely" is that he never had the chance to use any of his true powers, since even with him nerfing himself to express 0% of just a single aspect of stat/superpowers/immune system/weakest aura for 0 second with 0 effort, the entire omniverse can't stand even survive; He has to basically keep his absolute concealment all the time, which is not very exciting for him; he found the rest of the omniverse are too "fragile". Show Godric proudly shows some of his body enhancements - the highly sculpted muscles/veins/biceps/arms/well-defined six-pack abs that he used to hone when he was mortal and now, even more stronger without him needing to maintain them, some of his sentient passive auras that he just birthed that exact moment (if you mention the aura powers, you must remember not to provide too much details since in the later section Kaedan's absurd guesses of Godric's powers is not even reaching Godric's weakest aura's infinitely nerfed power), some of the new immune system improvements that he just got that moment, some of the superpowers that he just got that moment, all of which he can never use, much to Kaedan's horror. And Godric of course fully knows the full extent of his full powers, and he lets Kaedan to take wild guesses about them. Provide some detailed depictions that Kaedan imagined of how it would be like if Godric even releases infinitely small amount more than 0% of his true power (you must highly exaggerate Kaedan's guesses--remember that 0% of Godric's power already manage to infinitely annihilate entire omniverse at Year 0 in 0 second with 0 effort, and this Godric is way way way way more powerful than that "pathetically weak" Godric), and Kaedan must be completely fearful of such imaginary depictions thinking that it might be impossible or even overshoot the mark, and yet Godric reveals with an amused chuckle that his guesses is not even remotely close to effectively 0% of his true power (it's not even close to 0.00...1% -- you must not provide a finite number, it's infinitesimal number, pretty much 0 -- of his weakest aura's power) and no language known to mortal can describe it, leaving Kaedan with an absolute existential dread. Include dialogues/monologues. Write within 4000-8000 words.

1. Kaedan's concern for Godric (he loves him as his favorite uncle)
2. Godric's amusement at the "naively mortal" question
3. Detailed description of how Godric FEELS every power increase - stats, superpowers, immune system, passive auras, etc.
4. The feeling described as nourishment, orgasm, energy surge that amplifies endlessly

5. Every instant makes him powerful enough to crush infinite copies of himself from an instant ago with 0 effort
6. The unfairness - Godric gets everything infinitely by doing nothing, while creation works their hardest and gets nothing
7. Godric's "boredom" - he never gets to use his true powers because even 0% destroys the omniverse
8. Godric showing off his body enhancements (muscles, veins, abs, etc.)
9. Godric showing new sentient passive auras born that moment
10. New immune system improvements
11. New superpowers

13. Godric revealing Kaedan's guesses aren't even close to 0% of his true power
14. Dialogues and monologues

Let me write this scene carefully, maintaining the tone of existential dread, power fantasy, and the contrast between Godric's warmth toward Kaedan and the terrifying reality of his power.

I need to remember:

- Godric is genuinely amused by Kaedan's question

content:
The study was quiet. It was the kind of quiet that existed only inside Thronegate, a silence so complete and so carefully maintained that it felt less like the absence of noise and more like a physical substance, a thick, warm blanket draped over the room by the orbital systems that managed every photon, every vibration, every molecular collision within the palace walls. I sat across from Godric in his private reading room, a modest space lined with old texts and furnished with two deeply cushioned chairs that faced each other across a low table of dark, oiled wood. Godric held a cup of something warm. I did not remember what it was. I could not focus on anything as trivial as a beverage, because the man sitting across from me, the man I had called Uncle Godric since I was old enough to speak, the man who had taught me to swim in the palace lake and who had once carried me to my bed when I fell asleep in the garden at age seven, was the apex predator of all existence, and I had spent the last six nights reading about what that meant.

The journals had changed me. My father had warned me they would. He had not warned me they would make me look at Godric and see two beings simultaneously: the warm, ruggedly handsome, salt-and-pepper-bearded uncle who ruffled my hair and asked about my mother, and the entity whose pinky twitch could annihilate the omniverse, whose beard scratch could compress all of space, whose throat-clearing could command matter to stop being matter. Both beings occupied the same chair, sipped from the same cup, wore the same simple dark tunic stretched across the same heavily muscled frame. The cognitive dissonance was a physical pressure behind my eyes, a constant, low-grade headache that had not left me since I closed the last journal.

But beneath the dread, beneath the terror, beneath the newly structural understanding of my own insignificance, there was something else. Something I had not expected. I loved him. I still loved him. The journals had not diminished that love. They had sharpened it, given it teeth and weight, made it more complicated and more real. And because I loved him, I was concerned. I had been concerned for days, the worry building in my chest like a slow tide, and tonight, in the quiet of his study, with the warm light of the lamp catching the silver in his beard, I decided to ask.

"Uncle Godric," I said, and my voice was steadier than I expected it to be. "Can I ask you something?"

He looked up from his cup. His eyes, deep and warm and crinkled at the corners with the permanent hint of a smile, met mine with the genuine, unhurried attention he always gave me when I spoke. "Of course, Kaedan. You can ask me anything."

I swallowed. The words felt naive even as I formed them, but I pushed them out anyway, because the concern was real and the love was real and the question, however mortal it might be, came from a place that mattered. "Does it ever get lonely? Being what you are? Being the most powerful being in existence, knowing you'll only ever grow stronger, outliving everyone you care about? Does it bother you that no one will ever be able to stand beside you as an equal?"

The silence that followed was not long. It lasted perhaps two seconds. But in those two seconds, I watched Godric's face undergo a transformation I had never seen before. The warmth did not leave. The kindness did not diminish. But layered over them, like sunlight through a prism splitting into unexpected colors, was something I had not anticipated. Amusement. Genuine, deep, ringing amusement, the kind that made his broad chest shake slightly and the corners of his eyes crinkle even further. He set his cup down on the table with a soft clink, leaned back in his chair, and let out a laugh. It was a rich, warm, full-bodied sound, the laugh of a man who has just heard something genuinely, delightfully funny, and it filled the study with a resonance that made the lamp flame flicker.

"Lonely," he repeated, as though tasting the word. He stroked his beard, still smiling. "Kaedan. That is the most naively mortal question anyone has ever asked me. I mean that with affection. You sound exactly like your great-great-great-grandfather. Blake asked me something similar, once. I think he was thirty-seven at the time. I gave him the same answer I'm about to give you."

He leaned forward, his elbows on his knees, his massive hands clasped between them. The lamplight caught the thick, sculpted veins that ran along his forearms, the cords of muscle that layered his arms like the roots of an ancient tree. He looked at me with an expression of patient, fond indulgence, the look of a father explaining to a child why the sky is blue.

"No," he said. "It is not lonely. It is not bothersome. It is, Kaedan, the single most enjoyable experience any being has ever had in the history of existence, and I say that with full knowledge of every experience every being has ever had, because I am omniscient and I have experienced all of them simultaneously." He paused, letting the weight of that statement settle. "Every additional point of power I gain feels like nourishment. Not metaphorical nourishment. Real, physical, visceral nourishment, the way a starving man feels a mouthful of food hit his stomach. Every stat increase, every multiplication of my strength, my speed, my magic, my senses, every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, I feel it. I feel the power entering my body. I feel my muscles becoming denser. I feel my mana expanding. I feel my perception widening. It is not a number on a page. It is a sensation, continuous and unending, and it feels like being fed, being warmed, being filled."

He straightened in his chair, and I saw the muscles of his abdomen shift beneath his tunic, the thick, slabs of his chest expanding as he took a breath that was entirely unnecessary for a being who no longer needed air. "It is better than nourishment. Every stat increase feels like a surge of pure energy flooding through my entire being. It feels like a climax that does not end. Every Planck second, every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, the amplification hits, and it is a wave of pleasure and power so intense that it would vaporize the nervous system of any other being in existence. I feel it in my bones. I feel it in my blood. I feel it in every cell, every atom, every quantum state of my body. And the wave does not recede. It compounds. The next wave builds on the previous one, and the one after that builds on that, and the intensity increases forever."

I was staring at him. I knew I was staring. I could not stop. The words he was saying were rearranging the furniture of my understanding, and the new arrangement was more horrifying than anything in the journals.

"Every superpower I gain," Godric continued, his voice carrying the easy, conversational warmth of a man describing a favorite hobby, "each new one, every second, I feel it manifest. It is not an abstract acquisition. It is a physical event inside my body. A new superpower feels like a new limb growing, a new sense opening, a new dimension of my being unfolding. And the pleasure of it, the satisfaction of it, is indescribable. Each new power is a gift, and the gift feels like being injected with liquid vitality, pure, undiluted, electrifying vitality that amplifies everything I already am and makes it more. And then the power begins its own amplification, multiplying in potency infinitely every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, and each multiplication is another surge, another wave, another pulse of nourishing, intoxicating, orgiastic power that feeds back into the whole and makes the next pulse even stronger."

He held up his right hand, spreading his fingers. The lamplight caught the thick, calloused pads of his palms, the ridges of his knuckles, the dense network of veins that branched across the back of his hand like a river delta seen from orbit. "Every improvement to my immune system, I feel. When my biology evolves, when a new defensive layer crystallizes, when a new redundant backup system comes online, I feel it the way you feel your heartbeat. It is a constant, rhythmic pulse of becoming more. More durable. More invulnerable. More untouchable. Each pulse is satisfying. Deeply, fundamentally satisfying, the way a deep breath satisfies after being underwater, except the breath never ends and the satisfaction never peaks. It just keeps deepening."

"Every new passive shield," he said, lowering his hand, "every evolution of my sentient passive auras, every enhancement injected by the Evolved Weakness Paradox, every new immunity, every new mastery, I feel all of it. Every instant of my existence is a continuous, unbroken cascade of becoming more. And the scale of the increase, Kaedan, is something your mind cannot process, because every instant, every single infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, I become so much more powerful that I could crush an infinite number of copies of myself from the previous instant with zero effort. Not one copy. Not a million copies. An infinite number. The Godric who existed one Planck second ago is, compared to the Godric sitting in front of you right now, so pathetically weak that an infinite number of him combined could not make me feel the faintest tickle. And the Godric sitting in front of you right now will be equally pathetic compared to the Godric who exists one Planck second from now. And I feel every step of that ascent. Every instant is a high. Every instant is nourishment. Every instant is a surge of pure, amplifying, compounding pleasure that makes the previous instant feel like nothing."

He leaned back again, smiling, his eyes bright with the genuine enthusiasm of a man sharing something he found deeply, personally satisfying. "So no, Kaedan. It is not lonely. It is not bothersome. It is the opposite of lonely. It is the most fulfilling, the most stimulating, the most rewarding existence conceivable. I am being constantly, continuously, endlessly rewarded for existing. I do not need to work for it. I do not need to earn it. I do not need to fight, or train, or study, or struggle. I simply exist, and existence itself pays me tribute in the form of infinite, compounding, permanent power that I can feel in every fiber of my being, and the tribute never stops, and the tribute never decreases, and the tribute never fails to satisfy."

The room was very quiet. The lamp flame was perfectly still. I could hear my own heartbeat, amplified and steady, the blood pumping through veins that glowed with violet luminescence, and I was aware, with a clarity that felt like a blade between my ribs, of the absolute, cosmic, metaphysical unfairness of what he had just described.

He existed, and he was rewarded. Continuously. Infinitely. Forever. He did nothing, and everything came to him. Power, pleasure, satisfaction, nourishment, growth. All of it, unearned, unending, compounding. Every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, he became infinitely stronger, and he felt it as pleasure, and the pleasure compounded, and the power compounded, and the cycle never stopped and never would stop and could never stop because the Stasis Event was irrevocable and the amplification was permanent and the mechanism was self-reinforcing.

And the rest of creation. The rest of us. We worked. We struggled. We bled. We fought and trained and studied and sacrificed and endured, and the thousandfold daily amplification of my bloodline, the gift that made me a god by any mortal standard, was, compared to what Godric gained by sitting in a chair and breathing, effectively zero. Not approximately zero. Effectively zero. The total accumulated power of every being in the omniverse, across all timelines, all dimensions, all possible branches of causality, working their hardest for the entire duration of existence, would not equal what Godric gained in a single Planck second by doing absolutely nothing. The entire omniverse could labor for eternity and not achieve what Godric achieved by existing for one infinitesimal fraction of one moment. The unfairness was not a gap. It was a fundamental law of reality. Godric was the engine. We were the exhaust.

I looked at him. He was still smiling, still warm, still radiating the genuine, fond affection of an uncle sharing a pleasant conversation with his favorite nephew. He did not understand why I was troubled. He did not understand that what he had just described, the endless, effortless, compounding reward of his existence, was the most horrifying thing I had ever heard, more horrifying than the stat journals, more horrifying than the superpower journals, because the journals described what he could do, and this described what he was. He was a being who was infinitely rewarded for existing, who felt pleasure in every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, who became infinitely stronger every instant without trying, and the rest of creation could break itself against the wheel of effort for all eternity and never approach what he gained by breathing.

I thought about my father, who had served Godric for decades, who had studied and trained and amplified his power a thousandfold every day, who had worked and strived and dedicated his life to the service of his King, and whose total accumulated power, the sum of a lifetime of effort and amplification, was less than what Godric gained in the time it took me to blink. I thought about my grandfather, and his grandfather, and Blake, the mortal merchant who had found Godric in the dark, and the entire unbroken line of court wizards who had given their lives, their sanity, their peace of mind to the service of a being who gained more in one Planck second of sitting still than all of them combined had gained in their entire mortal lifetimes. The unfairness was not cruel. It was not malicious. It was simply the truth, and the truth was worse than cruelty because cruelty at least implied intent. This was just the way things were. Godric existed, and he was fed. We existed, and we starved, and we were grateful for the crumbs.

"The unfairness," I said, and my voice was barely a whisper. "Uncle Godric, the unfairness of it. You gain everything by doing nothing. The rest of us gain nothing by doing everything. The entire omniverse, every being, every civilization, every god, every entity, working their hardest for all eternity, would not achieve what you gain in a single Planck second by existing. How is that fair? How is that just?"

Godric looked at me with an expression of mild, genuine confusion. The question did not offend him. It puzzled him. He tilted his head slightly, the way he did when he was trying to understand a perspective that did not align with his own.

"Fair," he repeated, as though the word were a foreign concept he was trying to translate. "Kaedan, I do not think about fairness. Fairness is a framework invented by beings who need to compare themselves to each other in order to find meaning. I do not need to compare myself to anything, because there is nothing to compare myself to. I am the only one of me. The amplification is not a reward. It is not a payment. It is not something I earned or did not earn. It is simply what I am. I am a being whose nature is to grow. Asking whether it is fair that I grow is like asking whether it is fair that fire burns or that gravity pulls. It is not a question of fairness. It is a question of nature."

He paused, studying my face. Whatever he saw there, the horror, the dread, the love, the unfairness, all tangled together in a knot I could not untie, seemed to register with him, because his expression softened further, becoming almost tender.

"You are thinking about your father," he said. It was not a question. He was omniscient. He knew exactly what I was thinking. "You are thinking about Blake, and the court wizards, and everyone who has ever served me. You are thinking that their effort is meaningless compared to my growth. And you are right. It is meaningless, compared to my growth. But Kaedan, their effort is not for my growth. Their effort is for themselves. Your father amplified his power a thousandfold every day for forty years, and that power is real, and it is his, and it matters to him, and it matters to me, because I love him, and I am proud of what he has achieved, even if what he has achieved is, by my standard, nothing. The standard is not the point. The effort is the point. The struggle is the point. I do not struggle, and so I do not have what your father has, which is the satisfaction of having earned something. I have everything, and I earned none of it, and you may think that is a tragedy, but I assure you, the pleasure of the amplification more than compensates for the absence of struggle."

He smiled, and the smile was warm, and the warmth was real, and the warmth was the most terrifying thing about him, because it meant he genuinely did not understand. He genuinely believed that the pleasure of infinite, effortless, compounding power was equivalent to, or better than, the satisfaction of earned achievement. He genuinely could not see why a mortal, hearing this, would feel the ground open beneath their feet.

I sat with that for a moment. Then I asked the question that had been building in my chest since the first journal, the question I was afraid to ask because I was afraid of the answer.

"If it's so enjoyable," I said, "if every instant is a surge of power and pleasure and growth, then what could possibly make you bored? What could possibly make you lonely? You said it yourself, Uncle Godric. You are being constantly rewarded. What is missing?"

Godric's smile faded. Not into sadness. Not into anger. Into something I had not seen before, a flat, almost wistful expression that sat on his ruggedly handsome face like a temporary visitor, alien and out of place.

"I can never use my power," he said.

The words were simple. The silence that followed them was not.

"I have never used my power," Godric continued, his voice quieter now, still warm but stripped of the enthusiasm of the previous topic. "Not once. Not in the entire million years of my reign. Not in the ten thousand years of stasis before that. I have never used my power, Kaedan. I have used effectively zero percent of one stat, or one superpower, or one immune response, and even that infinitesimal fraction was enough to annihilate the omniverse infinite times over. I cannot use my power. I cannot express my strength, because the passive resting tension of my muscles at zero percent output is enough to erase existence. I cannot express my speed, because a blink at zero percent output is fast enough to smear the fastest beings in creation across all of time. I cannot express my magic, because an infinitesimal fraction of my mana at zero percent output is enough to unmake reality. I cannot release my absolute concealment, because the moment I do, the pressure of my existence collapses the omniverse. I am, Kaedan, a being of infinite, compounding, amplifying power who must keep every fraction of that power locked behind an absolute veil at all times, because the omniverse is too fragile to survive even the slightest expression of what I am."

He held up his hand again, and this time he flexed it slowly, making a fist. The muscles of his forearm rolled and shifted, the veins engorging, the tendons standing out like cables. His hand was massive, calloused, the hand of a man who had once wielded a sword and hauled stone as the mortal king of Olympus. The muscles were not decorative. They were the muscles of a laborer, a warrior, a king who had built his kingdom with his hands. But they were no longer maintained by labor. They maintained themselves, amplified themselves, strengthened themselves every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, without Godric lifting a finger.

"Look at this," Godric said, his voice carrying a note of genuine, almost boyish pride. He pulled up the sleeve of his tunic, exposing his forearm. The lamplight caught the full, grotesque magnificence of the limb. The muscles were layered, dense, sculpted with a precision that no mortal training could achieve, each fiber visible beneath skin that was simultaneously rugged and flawless. The veins were thick, branching rivers of blue beneath the skin, carrying blood that was no longer necessary but that existed because Godric's body was a perfect specimen of physical form, amplified beyond any conceivable limit. He flexed, and the muscle swelled, the fibers contracting with a force that, even at absolute rest, even at zero percent output, was so vast that the air around his forearm distorted, a tiny ripple of bent light that his absolute concealment caught and corrected a fraction of a second later.

"I honed these when I was mortal," he said, stroking the muscle with his other hand, the gesture almost nostalgic. "Reina used to tease me about them. She said I spent more time with the training stones than with her. I built these arms hauling marble for the Olympus palace extension. I built this chest," he tapped his sternum through the tunic, "sparring with Thorin, though he always cheated with that bloodline magic of his." A fond smile crossed his face, then faded. "Now they maintain themselves. They amplify themselves. Every Planck second, these muscles become infinitely stronger without me doing anything. I do not need to train. I do not need to eat. I do not need to rest. The body I built as a mortal man is now a body that amplifies itself forever, and I cannot use it. I cannot throw a punch. I cannot lift a stone. I cannot grip anything with true force, because the force of my grip at zero percent output would crush the target into a singularity and annihilate the surrounding reality."

He stood up. The movement was fluid, effortless, the movement of a body that did not need to exert itself to do anything. He pulled off his tunic in a single, casual motion, and I saw his torso in full for the first time. The lamplight played across a landscape of muscle that defied description. His shoulders were boulders, the deltoids capping his arms like armor plating. His chest was a wall of sculpted, striated muscle, the pectorals thick and dense, the skin stretched taut over fibers that were visible individually, each one a cord of amplified strength. His abdomen was a washboard of deeply grooved muscle, the six-pack so well-defined that the valleys between the segments cast shadows, each segment a brick of dense, vascular tissue that was, at that very moment, amplifying in strength without any effort or maintenance. His waist was narrow, his obliques carving lines toward his hips, and across the entire landscape of his torso ran a network of veins so thick and so numerous that they looked like a map of rivers seen from orbit, blue and green and purple channels carrying blood that pulsed with the continuous, nourishing surge of amplification he had described.

He was not posing. He was not flexing. He was simply standing, and the muscles were simply there, maintaining themselves, amplifying themselves, a body that had been honed by a mortal man and was now sustained and enhanced by a power that had no ceiling and no floor.

"These," he said, placing a hand on his abdomen, the fingers sinking slightly into the dense muscle before the tissue pushed back with a resistance that felt, from where I sat, like pressing into warm stone, "these are stronger now than they were a Planck second ago. And a Planck second from now, they will be stronger again. I can feel it. I can feel the tissue becoming denser. I can feel the fibers becoming tougher. I can feel the force output of each individual muscle cell increasing, and the increase is pleasurable, Kaedan. It feels like the muscle is being fed. It feels like the muscle is being massaged, warmed, filled with energy from the inside. Every Planck second, every muscle in my body experiences a surge of growth and pleasure, and I cannot use any of it. I cannot swing a sword. I cannot grip a rope. I cannot do anything with this body that I built as a mortal man, because the body has outgrown the universe it lives in."

He pulled the tunic back on, covering the landscape of useless, infinitely amplifying muscle. Then he held out his right hand, palm up, and I saw something shift in the air above his skin. A faint, shimmering distortion, like heat haze, but sentient. The distortion coalesced, taking on a vague, flickering form that seemed to be both there and not there, present and absent, a thing that existed in the space between existence and non-existence. It was small, fitting in the palm of his hand, and it pulsed with a faint, iridescent light that changed color every fraction of a second.

"This one was born just now," Godric said, looking at the shimmering thing with the fond, proud expression of a man showing a new puppy. "A new sentient passive aura. It manifested in the last Planck second. I felt it come into being. It felt like a new breath, a new voice inside me, a new extension of my will that I did not have a moment ago." He turned his hand slightly, examining the aura. "It is one of the weakest I have. It is so new it has not yet evolved. Gent, my oldest and weakest active aura, is infinitely stronger than this one. And Gent is infinitely weaker than me. And the siblings that sleep, the ones I put into dormancy because they were annoying, each of them is infinitely stronger than Gent. And each of them is infinitely weaker than the evolved versions that have come online in the last hundred thousand years. And all of them, every aura I have, active and dormant, are collectively infinitely weaker than the auras I will birth in the next Planck second. I can feel them being born, Kaedan. Every second, a new one. Every second, a new extension of my will, a new layer of defense, a new sentient guardian that exists solely to ensure I am not inconvenienced. And I cannot use any of them. Even this tiny, newborn one, if I allowed it to act freely, if I removed the constraints I place on all my auras, it would annihilate the omniverse. Not because it is powerful. Because it is mine, and anything that is mine is, by definition, too powerful for existence to survive."

The shimmering aura pulsed again, and I felt a wave of something roll off it. Not heat. Not pressure. Not magic. Something else, something that my amplified senses could detect but not categorize, a fundamental vibration that seemed to come from a layer of reality I did not have the organs to perceive. And that wave, that single, tiny pulse from a newborn, barely-formed aura that Godric had just birthed, made my thousandfold-amplified body flinch. My wards shivered. My mana reserves trembled. The hair on my arms stood up, and my stomach dropped as though I had missed a step on a staircase. That was a newborn aura. The weakest, most primitive, most recently formed extension of Godric's will, and its ambient pulse was enough to make me, a court wizard of Blake's bloodline, a man whose power multiplied a thousandfold every day, physically recoil.

"My immune system updated again," Godric said, seemingly oblivious to my reaction, or perhaps simply not considering it noteworthy. He held up his other hand, examining his fingertips. "Three new biological redundancies crystallized in the last second. I can feel them. One of them is a new type of backup chain, a recursive layer that ensures my body can never experience the concept of damage from temporal attacks. Another is an evolution of my cellular regeneration, a new process that can rebuild my entire physical form from a single quantum state in zero time. The third is a preemptive defense mechanism that identifies potential threats before they exist as concepts and makes the surrounding reality allergic to them, causing the environment itself to reject the possibility of harm before the harm is formulated." He flexed his fingers, examining them with mild interest. "I felt all three of them come online. Each one felt like a surge of warmth, a deep, satisfying pulse of becoming more invulnerable. Like putting on armor that fits perfectly, except the armor is inside my cells and the fitting never stops."

He dropped his hands and looked at me with an expression that was simultaneously warm and utterly, cosmically casual. "And two new superpowers manifested in the last second as well. One of them allows me to rewrite the fundamental constants of any reality by perceiving them differently. The other allows me to convert any form of energy, including the binding energy of concepts, into a state I can only describe as pure nourishment for my amplification rate. Both of them felt like new limbs unfolding, new dimensions of capability opening inside me, and both of them are already amplifying in potency infinitely, and both of them are completely useless to me because I cannot use them without annihilating the omniverse."

He sat back down, picking up his cup again, taking a sip. He looked perfectly content. He looked like a man enjoying a quiet evening. He looked like my uncle.

"That is what bores me, Kaedan," he said, his voice quiet and matter-of-fact. "Not the power. The power is endlessly, continuously, deeply satisfying. What bores me is that I can never use it. I can never express it. I can never let it out. I have to keep my absolute concealment active at all times, a perfect, flawless veil that suppresses every fraction of my existence, because the omniverse is too fragile. Everything is too fragile. The strongest beings in existence, the most powerful weapons, the most durable materials, the most fundamental constants of reality, all of it is tissue paper. Wet tissue paper. I am a man made of fire living in a house made of ice, and I cannot move without melting the walls. I cannot speak without shattering the windows. I cannot breathe without collapsing the roof. I have to sit perfectly still, inside the veil, forever, and let the power amplify and the pleasure compound and the growth continue, and I can never, ever let any of it out."
