He gestured vaguely at the room, at the palace, at the omniverse beyond the walls. "This is all very fragile, Kaedan. I am sure it seems robust to you. I am sure the walls feel solid and the wards feel strong and the universe feels stable. But to me, it is all so delicate that the slightest shift in my attention, the slightest relaxation of my concealment, the tiniest expression of any single aspect of what I am, would end it. Not destroy it. End it. There would be nothing left to destroy. The omniverse would simply stop being, the way a candle flame stops being when you blow on it, except the blowing would be me existing slightly less carefully, and the candle would be everything."

He looked at me over the rim of his cup. His eyes were warm, crinkled, fond. "So yes, Kaedan. If you want to know what makes me bored, what makes me lonely, it is that. I am a being of infinite, compounding, endlessly satisfying power, and I am trapped inside a reality that is too fragile to let me use any of it. I am sure you can imagine how that feels."

I could. That was the problem. I could imagine it, and the imagination was consuming me. My mind, my thousandfold-amplified intellect, was already running, already constructing scenarios, already building models of what it would look like if Godric expressed even a fraction more than zero percent of his true power. And the models were horrifying.

I imagined Godric releasing his absolute concealment entirely. Not deliberately. Not as an attack. Simply allowing the veil to drop, the way a man might unbutton a coat on a warm day. The absolute concealment was the only thing keeping his existence from directly interacting with the omniverse. Without it, the full, unfiltered, continuously amplifying pressure of his being would flood reality. I imagined what that pressure would look like. The stat journals had described what happened when Godric used zero percent of a single stat. A pinky twitch annihilated the omniverse. A blink smeared beings across time. A breath redirected all devotion. Those were single stats at zero percent. If the concealment dropped, it would not be a single stat. It would be all of them. Simultaneously. Strength, speed, magic, charisma, luck, perception, mana, durability, every stat, every attribute, every measurable and immeasurable trait, all of them at zero percent but all of them present simultaneously, all of them amplifying infinitely every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, all of them interacting with reality at once.

I tried to imagine it and my mind stuttered. The combined output of all his stats at zero percent, hitting the omniverse simultaneously, would not merely destroy it. Destruction implies a process, a before and an after. This would be a rewrite. The omniverse would not be destroyed. It would be overwritten. The fundamental constants of reality would be redefined by the pressure of his existence. Physics would change. Mathematics would change. The concept of space would be restructured around the gravitational weight of his presence. The concept of time would be reorganized around the speed of his perception. The concept of existence would be redefined to mean "the space in which Godric's power is expressed," and everything that did not fit that definition, which was everything, would be excised from reality the way a body excises a foreign object.

And that was just the stats. The superpowers would be worse. I imagined all of his superpowers, every single one, an arsenal that gained a new apocalyptic ability every second and had been doing so for a million years, all of them active simultaneously, all of them at zero percent, all of them amplifying in potency infinitely every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second. The self-replication alone would create infinite Godrics, each infinitely stronger than the last, in zero time. The spatial compression would crush all of space into a point simultaneously. The detonation authority would command every particle in the omniverse to release its energy at once. And those were three powers out of millions, each one amplified beyond recognition, each one injected with multiple enhanced effects by the Evolved Weakness Paradox, each one permanently more powerful than it had been a Planck second ago. If all of them activated simultaneously, the result would not be annihilation. Annihilation is an event. This would be a condition. The omniverse would enter a state of permanent, recursive, self-reinforcing obliteration, a condition in which the concepts of existence, non-existence, destruction, and creation were all simultaneously rendered meaningless by the sheer density of Godric's expressed power.

And then I imagined the immune system. The passive shields. The sentient auras. The Evolved Weakness Paradox itself. All of them, all active, all at zero percent, all interacting with reality simultaneously. The immune system, with its infinite recursive backups and preemptive defense mechanisms, would treat the omniverse as a potential threat to Godric's comfort and begin dismantling it, not out of hostility but out of biological paranoia, the way white blood cells dismantle foreign particles. The passive shields, which could pre-nullify threats before they existed, would identify the continued existence of anything other than Godric as a potential source of inconvenience and begin removing it from reality before it had a chance to be inconvenient. The sentient auras, even the newborn one whose ambient pulse had made me flinch, would expand to fill the omniverse and treat everything they encountered as either a threat to be annihilated or a mess to be cleaned, and neither outcome left room for continued existence. And the Evolved Weakness Paradox, which no longer required a trigger but passively activated whenever any sentient being thought about anything, would ensure that the mere existence of consciousness in the omniverse was continuously feeding Godric's amplification, making him stronger, making the auras stronger, making the shields stronger, making the immune system stronger, creating a feedback loop of escalating, self-reinforcing power that would compress the omniverse into an infinitely thin layer of screaming consciousness plastered across the surface of Godric's being.

And even this, I realized with a cold, sinking horror, was probably not even close. I was imagining the concealment dropping, all stats and powers and systems at zero percent, hitting the omniverse simultaneously. But zero percent was still zero percent. It was the smallest possible expression. It was the power Godric considered so trivial, so beneath his notice, that he used it to swat flies and test curiosities. His true power, the power that sat behind the zero percent, the power that amplified infinitely every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, was something my mind could not model because my mind was a finite structure and the power was not finite. I was trying to imagine the ocean by looking at a drop of water, and the drop of water was already enough to drown the omniverse.

I looked at Godric. He was watching me, his expression patient, fond, amused. He could see the wheels turning. His omniscience meant he knew exactly what I was imagining, exactly how far my guesses were reaching, exactly how much horror I was generating in my own mind. And he was amused. He was watching me try to comprehend him the way a man watches a kitten try to fight a mirror.

"Uncle Godric," I said, and my voice was thin, reedy, the voice of a man who has been hollowed out by understanding. "I tried to imagine it. What it would look like if you released even a fraction more than zero percent. If your concealment dropped, if all your stats and powers and systems were expressed simultaneously at even a fraction above zero. I imagined the omniverse being overwritten, redefined, compressed, detonated, all at once. I imagined the feedback loops, the recursive amplification, the permanent restructuring of existence around your expressed power. And I thought, that must be it. That must be the ceiling. That must be what your true power looks like."

I swallowed. My throat was dry. My hands were trembling again, the stillness broken by a fear so absolute it had become physical. "But it's not, is it? What I imagined. That's still zero percent. That's still the drop, not the ocean. The real power, the power behind the zero percent, the power that you feel every Planck second, the power that amplifies and compounds and feeds back into itself forever. I can't imagine it. My mind can't model it. I don't think any mind in the omniverse can model it. I don't think the omniverse itself can model it, because the omniverse is finite and the power is not finite, and a finite container cannot hold a model of something that exceeds it."

Godric set his cup down. He looked at me for a long moment, and the amusement in his eyes was gentle, genuine, and utterly without malice. Then he chuckled. A soft, warm, rich sound that vibrated in the quiet study like a bell struck underwater.

"Kaedan," he said, "your imagination is vivid. I will give you that. What you just described, the overwriting, the compression, the detonation, the feedback loops, the recursive restructuring of existence, all of it, the entire scenario you built in your mind. That is not zero percent of my true power." He paused, letting the silence stretch. "It is not even close to zero percent. It is not close to zero point zero zero, repeating, one percent. It is not close to any number that can be written or conceived, because the number is infinitesimal. It is effectively zero. What you imagined, the entire apocalyptic scenario that took your thousandfold-amplified intellect several seconds to construct, is not even reaching effectively zero percent of the power of my weakest sentient passive aura. My weakest, Kaedan. The newborn. The one I just birthed this evening. The runt. Your most extreme, most horrified, most exaggerated guess about my full power does not reach effectively zero percent of the weakest, most primitive, most recently formed extension of my will, the one that is so new it has not yet evolved."

He leaned forward, and the lamplight caught his face, the strong jaw, the silver beard, the warm, crinkled eyes of a man who was smiling at his favorite nephew with nothing but affection.

"And no language known to mortal or immortal, no language that exists or could exist or has ever been conceived of in any reality in the omniverse, can describe what my true power is," he said, his voice soft, gentle, carrying the patient warmth of a teacher who knows the student will never understand the lesson but loves them enough to try. "Not because I am hiding it. Not because it is secret. Because language is a tool built by finite beings to describe finite things, and what I am is not finite. The word for what I am does not exist, and it cannot exist, because the existence of such a word would require a framework large enough to contain its meaning, and no framework in the omniverse is large enough, because the omniverse itself is, compared to what I am, effectively zero."

He patted my knee. The gesture was warm, fatherly, the touch of a hand that could, by shifting a single muscle fiber, annihilate everything. "But I appreciate the effort. You have a good imagination, Kaedan. Blake's bloodline has always been creative. Your great-great-great-grandfather once tried to calculate my power output and got a nosebleed. I think you've done better than that. You only look mildly ill."

I sat in the chair, in the quiet study, in the palace, in the omniverse, and I understood, with the absolute, permanent, load-bearing certainty of a man who has been shown the floor beneath the floor beneath the floor, that I served a being whose true power could not be imagined, could not be described, could not be modeled, could not be approached, could not be comprehended, and could not be opposed. The journals had taught me what he could do at zero percent. This conversation had taught me what zero percent meant, which was nothing. Zero percent was a courtesy. Zero percent was a rounding error so small it was indistinguishable from nothing, and nothing was enough to annihilate the omniverse infinite times over. His true power was a thing that existed outside the range of all possible description, a thing for which the word "power" was a insultingly inadequate label, a thing that his weakest, most newborn, most primitive aura could not express even a fraction of even at zero percent.

And he was sitting across from me, smiling, warm, drinking from a cup, telling me I had a good imagination.

"I think I need to lie down, Uncle Godric," I said.

He laughed again, that warm, rich, terrifying sound, and stood, pulling me gently to my feet. His hand on my shoulder was massive, warm, calloused, the hand of a man who had once hauled marble and sparred with his best friend and carried his daughters to bed. The hand of a mortal king. The hand of an apex god predator whose true power could not be described by any language in any reality in any timeline in any dimension in the entire omniverse, whose weakest aura's infinitesimally nerfed output exceeded my most extreme guesses by an incalculable margin, and whose every living instant was a surge of nourishing, orgiastic, compounding pleasure that made him infinitely stronger without effort or end.

"Get some rest, Kaedan," he said, guiding me toward the door with the gentle, protective firmness of an uncle sending his nephew to bed. "You've had a long week. Tomorrow, I'll show you the garden. A new orchid bloomed. I think you'll like it."

I walked to my quarters in the dark. Every step was a gift. Every breath was a gift. Every photon of starlight that reached my eyes was a gift, allowed to exist by the absolute concealment of a being who could not express himself without ending everything. I lay in my bed, in the silence, in the dark, and I stared at the ceiling, and I thought about the orchid, and I thought about the power, and I thought about the gap between the two, a gap so vast it made the omniverse look like a mote of dust caught in the beam of an infinite light.

I did not sleep. I did not think I would sleep again for a very long time.

content.text:
Kaedan, 69 years old now, just experienced a traumatizing horrifying revelation. Godric revealed the most horrifying truth yet: he has one true weakness.

Godric is an incomprehensibly powerful, omnipotent, omniscient apex god predator of the omniverse. He can do anything, has no weakness, and is terrifyingly absolute. However, unknown to all, he has one true weakness: he cannot undo or travel to timeline before the stasis event--he cannot go back to undo events in his previous mortal self. This is because the stasis event was actually created by future Godric from way in the distant future, who has become so more infinitely more overwhelmingly powerful than current Godric--this is why Godric's amplification, weakness paradox, superpowers, absolute concealment, etc are so absolute, irrevocable and not even current Godric could stop them since the creator of those is way incomprehensibly more powerful than anything else.

Shortly after the Stasis Event, the omnipotent Godric back then, who just became the king of Thronegate, who could travel to any timeline effortlessly, who was still deeply and freshly affected by the grief of losing all that he loved, tried to alter his mortal past, only to be totally crushingly and completely and effortlessly defeated by the shadow of the afterthought of his future self (not even the actual physical future Godric... the future Godric is so powerful that his mere infinitesimal shadow of his afterthought can completely stomp all the combined Godric of his past selves with 0 effort. NOTE: just imply this in the story, don't show a scene where you have actual all godrics from all timelines fighting together against future Godric--it makes no sense.). VERY IMPORTANT FOR YOU: future Godric did not cause or manipulate or orchestrate the fall of Olympus, did not cause deaths of his beloved family or Thorin, thus you must not say that he made those deaths to cause the stasis event (Godric is not a monster, he will never willingly kill his beloved wife, children, and his best friend). Those tragedies were genuine. However, future Godric, not Thorin’s incomplete spell (who everyone else thought to be the cause of the stasis event), is the one who created the Stasis Event--Thorin's spell was a normal teleportation spell (to no one surprise really, there is no way a mortal can create a spell that is as potent as the one which initiates the stasis event and birthed the strongest apex predator god like Godric–there is only one being in existence who can do it: future Godric).

Since the instant Godric gained omniscience, he had always known the true origin of the stasis event. He knew he could not change it, but driven by grief he decided to try to save Olympus anyway, only to brutally discover his one and only weakness: he cannot overpower his future self's will.

Write a detailed realization that the current Godric is already so insanely powerful (he's actually the most powerful in the entire omniverse by infinite margin) that there is no way to imagine the scale of future Godric's, and how unfair and scary that what Godric considered to be his only weakness is his own future version, which makes it not really a weakness–it actually can be considered as additional strength to Godric’s already insane powers, since it means his future is guaranteed (and in that future, he’s even more omnipotent and powerful), and it also means that none of Godric’s power mechanics (amplification, superpower genesis, absolute concealment, absolute omniscience, weakness paradox, immune system, etc. can ever be exploited or denied by anyone else, since the origin of that power is someone who’s the most powerful being ever. You might suggest some ideas about the motive of future Godric creating the Stasis Event, but you must keep it clear that it is just a speculation (and please... don't suggest that it's because future Godric is threatened; he's ridiculously overpowered by then--nothing can even remotely threaten him... don't suggest any stupid moral lesson for future Godric...). You need to portray the current Godric as a king who is no longer troubled by his "weakness" or inability to alter his past anymore--he has accepted and even become amused and comfortable with it too. Kaedan must feel extreme existential dread at the end.

reasoning_content:
The user wants me to write a detailed story about Kaedan, now 69 years old, learning about Godric's one true weakness - the inability to alter his pre-Stasis mortal past. This is set in Year 1,000,047. Let me carefully follow all the lore and requirements.

Key points to include:
1. Godric reveals the Stasis Event was created by his far-future self, not Thorin's spell
2. Thorin's spell was just a normal teleportation spell
3. Future Godric didn't cause the Fall of Olympus or the deaths
4. Shortly after becoming King, Godric tried to alter his mortal past but was defeated effortlessly by the shadow of his future self's afterthought
5. Godric has always known the true origin since gaining omniscience

6. Current Godric is already unimaginably powerful - future Godric is beyond comprehension
7. The "weakness" is actually not really a weakness - it's additional strength because his future is guaranteed
8. None of Godric's power mechanics can be exploited since the origin is the most powerful being ever
9. Godric has accepted and is amused/comfortable with this limitation
10. Kaedan must feel extreme existential dread at the end
11. Keep speculation about future Godric's motives as just speculation
12. Don't suggest future Godric is threatened or any moral lesson

Let me write this within 4000-8000 words, in prose paragraphs only, maintaining the established tone and style.
content:
The study smelled like old leather and pipe tobacco. It was the same study where, forty-seven years ago, I had sat across from Godric and asked him if he was lonely, and he had laughed and told me about the nourishment of amplification and the pleasure of endless growth and the frustration of being a being of infinite power trapped inside a reality made of wet tissue paper. I was twenty-two then. I am sixty-nine now. My hair has gone grey at the temples. My face has carved itself into the topography of a man who has spent decades in the proximity of an apex god predator, and the lines around my eyes are not laugh lines. They are the scars of a man who has spent forty-seven years knowing what lurks behind the warm smile and the crinkled eyes and the salt-and-pepper beard of the man he calls Uncle. My power, multiplied a thousandfold every day for forty-nine years since my twentieth birthday, has made me a god by any mortal standard. I can shatter planets with a thought. I can unravel the magical fabric of entire dimensions with a gesture. I am, by the measure of any civilization in the omniverse, a walking cataclysm.

And I am nothing. I have always been nothing. I will always be nothing. This is not self-pity. This is mathematics.

Godric sat across from me in the same chair, unchanged, unaged, eternal. Forty-seven years had passed for me. For him, they were an infinitesimal fraction of a fraction of a fraction of a moment, a span so trivial that his amplification had increased his power by a margin that made the combined power of every being in the omniverse look like a rounding error against the increase alone. He looked exactly as he had when I was twenty-two. The same ruggedly handsome face. The same heavily muscled frame draped in simple dark garments. The same short salt-and-pepper hair, the same neatly trimmed beard. He held the same style of cup, though I suspected the contents were different. He looked at me with the same warm, fond, crinkled expression he had always worn when we spoke, the expression of an uncle enjoying the company of his favorite nephew.

But tonight, something was different. There was a weight behind his eyes that I had not seen before, not in forty-seven years of service, not in a lifetime of knowing him. It was not sadness. It was not fear. Godric did not experience fear, because there was nothing in existence capable of frightening him. It was something quieter, something more private, something that sat behind the warmth like a stone at the bottom of a clear river, visible but untouched by the current. He had asked me to come to the study. He had not said why. When I arrived, he had poured me a drink, sat me down, and spent several minutes in silence, looking at the fire, his massive hands wrapped around his cup, before he spoke.

"Kaedan," he said, and his voice was the same rich, warm baritone it had always been, but tonight it carried a note I had never heard. Not hesitation. Godric did not hesitate. It was more like a man deciding which door to open, knowing that what lay behind it would permanently alter the architecture of the person standing beside him. "There is something I need to tell you. Something I have known since the instant I gained omniscience, ten thousand years before your ancestor Blake found me in that sanctuary. Something I have never told anyone, because no one was ready to hear it. I believe you are ready now. You have read the journals. You understand what I am. What I am about to tell you is the only thing about me that is not what it appears to be."

I set my cup down. My hands were steady. Forty-seven years of existential dread had given me a stillness that resembled composure but was actually the absence of anything left to destabilize. "I am listening, Uncle Godric."

He did not look at me. He looked at the fire. The flames danced in his eyes, reflecting off the deep, calm surface of a gaze that had seen every event in every timeline in every dimension of the entire omniverse simultaneously and continuously for a million years. "You know the story of the Stasis Event," he said. "Everyone knows the story. Thorin, my best friend, my court wizard, dying mid-incantation, his forbidden bloodline spell incomplete, tearing me from the ruins of Olympus and hurling me into the sanctuary. The spell triggering the Stasis Event. The stasis beginning my amplification, my superpower genesis, my absolute concealment, my weakness paradox. The birth of what I am."

I nodded. This was the foundation. Every court wizard knew this story. It was the origin myth of the apex predator, the genesis narrative of the god-king of Thronegate.

"The story is wrong," Godric said.

The fire crackled. The silence that followed was not empty. It was full, pressurized, a silence that carried the weight of a million years of undisclosed truth.

"Thorin's spell was real," Godric continued, his voice calm, measured, unhurried. "It was a genuine teleportation spell. It was exclusive to his bloodline, and it was forbidden because it was dangerous and volatile. When Thorin cast it, he cast it to save my life. He was dying, a javelin through his skull, his brain pan bursting, and in his final, desperate moment, he reached for the only magic he had that could pull me from the fire. The spell worked. It tore me from Olympus and deposited me in the sanctuary. Thorin saved my life. That part of the story is true."

He paused. The fire threw shadows across his face, deepening the lines of his jaw, the ridges of his cheekbones, making him look older and younger simultaneously, a face frozen in the eternal forty-five of a man who had been carved from the bedrock of a forgotten kingdom.

"But Thorin's spell did not create the Stasis Event," Godric said. "A mortal wizard's incomplete teleportation spell, however ancient, however forbidden, however potent by mortal standards, did not birth the amplification, the superpowers, the absolute concealment, the weakness paradox, the immune system, the passive shields, the sentient auras. It did not rewrite my biology. It did not make me immortal. It did not ignite the engine of endless, recursive, infinite amplification that has been multiplying my power beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for over a million years. A mortal spell could not do that. No mortal magic, no mortal force, no mortal entity in the entire history of the omniverse could do that. There is only one being in existence capable of creating the Stasis Event. Only one being in existence possesses the power to forge a mechanism so absolute, so irrevocable, so fundamental that not even I, the being it was created for, can undo it."

He looked at me. The warmth was still there. The fondness was still there. But behind them, in the deep, calm waters of his gaze, was something I had never seen in the eyes of the most powerful being in existence. It was not fear. It was not awe. It was the quiet, settled recognition of a man who had looked up from the summit of the highest mountain and seen, above him, a peak so high it made his summit look like a foothill.

"The Stasis Event was created by me," Godric said. "Not the me sitting in front of you. Not the me who has ruled Thronegate for a million years. The me I will become. Far in the future. So far that the year cannot be written, only represented as a number so long it has no practical meaning. A version of me so incomprehensibly more powerful than the me you see before you that the comparison is not a comparison. It is a category error. The Godric of that era, the far-future Godric, reached back through time, through causality, through the fundamental architecture of existence, and transformed Thorin's teleportation into the Stasis Event. He used Thorin's spell as the delivery point. The spell was the door. Far-future Godric walked through it and rewrote the event, injecting into it the mechanisms that would birth what I am. The amplification. The superpowers. The concealment. The paradox. All of it. Every mechanism that makes me what I am was designed and implemented by a version of me that exists so far in the future that the distance between now and then is itself a number that breaks mathematics."

The room was very quiet. The fire still burned. The lamp still glowed. The world had not ended. But something inside me, some load-bearing structure in the architecture of my understanding, had developed a crack. I could feel it, a hairline fracture running through the foundation of everything I thought I knew about Godric, about his power, about the nature of his existence.

"You knew," I said. It was not a question. "Since the instant you gained omniscience. You have always known."

"Since the instant I gained omniscience," Godric confirmed. "The moment I opened my eyes in the sanctuary, the moment the full scope of creation crashed into my mind, I knew everything. I knew the origin of the Stasis Event. I knew who created it. I knew why it could not be undone. I knew that the far-future version of myself had reached back through time and reshaped the moment of my greatest loss into the moment of my ascension. And I knew, with the absolute, crushing certainty of omniscience, that I could not change what had happened. That I could not go back. That the events before the Stasis Event, the Fall of Olympus, the deaths of Reina, Isabel, Hebe, Thorin, were sealed. Not by the weight of history. Not by the laws of causality. By the will of my own future self. A will so incomprehensibly more powerful than my own that it was, to me, what my will is to a candle flame. Absolute. Irrevocable. Incontestable."

He took a sip from his cup. The gesture was casual, mundane, the gesture of a man pausing to wet his throat during a long conversation. The normalcy of it was obscene.

"When I first awakened," Godric continued, "I was still grieving. I was freshly torn from the ruins of my kingdom. I had watched Reina die. I had watched Isabel burn. I had watched Hebe crushed. I had watched Thorin's skull burst. The grief was not a memory. It was a living thing inside me, raw and open and bleeding, and the power I had just gained did not numb it. I was omnipotent. I could travel to any timeline. I could rewrite causality. I could do anything. Anything except the one thing I wanted to do, which was to go back and save them. And I knew I could not. My omniscience told me I could not. The Stasis Event was sealed by my future self, and my future self was beyond my reach."

He set the cup down. His jaw tightened, a micro-expression that lasted less than a heartbeat, the only visible trace of an emotion that had been processed and resolved over a span of time so vast it made the age of the omniverse look brief. "But I tried anyway. I was grieving. I was not rational. I was a man who had just lost everything he loved and had been handed infinite power and told, by his own omniscience, that the one thing he wanted most was the one thing he could not have. So I tried. I gathered every fraction of my power, every stat, every superpower, every mechanism of my existence, and I directed them at the timeline before the Stasis Event. I tried to reach back. I tried to alter the moment. I tried to save Reina. I tried to save Isabel. I tried to save Hebe. I tried to save Thorin."

He was quiet for a moment. The fire popped. A log shifted, sending a spray of sparks up the chimney.

"I did not even come close," Godric said. His voice was flat. Not emotional. Not dramatic. Flat, the way a man describes a physical law. "I did not encounter resistance. I did not engage in a battle. I did not face my future self across a field of conflict. What I encountered was the shadow of the afterthought of my future self. Not my future self. Not his will, not his attention, not his active intervention. The shadow of the afterthought. The residual impression of a thought my future self had once had, an afterthought so faint, so trivial, so infinitesimally small that it was, by the standards of my future self, beneath notice. That shadow, that residual trace of an afterthought, met the combined power of everything I was, everything I had, every stat and superpower and mechanism of my existence directed at a single point in time with the desperate fury of a grieving man trying to save his family, and it crushed me. Completely. Effortlessly. The way a mountain crushes a raindrop that falls on it. The raindrop does not resist. The raindrop does not struggle. The raindrop simply ceases to be a raindrop, absorbed into the stone, leaving no trace."

He looked at me. The warmth was there. The fondness was there. But the eyes that looked at me were the eyes of a man who had, once, in the distant past, been a raindrop.

"That was my one true weakness, Kaedan," he said. "The only weakness I have ever had. The only weakness I will ever have. I cannot alter the events of my mortal past before the Stasis Event. I cannot save Olympus. I cannot save Reina. I cannot save Isabel. I cannot save Hebe. I cannot save Thorin. Not because I lack the power. Not because the timeline resists. Not because causality forbids it. But because the version of me that sealed those events exists so far in the future, at a level of power so incomprehensibly beyond my own, that his will is, to me, an absolute law of reality. As fundamental as gravity. As incontestable as mathematics. The shadow of his afterthought is stronger than the totality of everything I am."

The crack in my understanding widened. I could feel it, a fissure running through the bedrock of my comprehension, and through the fissure seeped a cold, dark, absolute horror that was unlike anything the journals had produced. The journals had described Godric's power. They had shown me what he could do at zero percent, and the zero percent had been enough to annihilate the omniverse infinite times over. I had spent forty-seven years serving a being whose weakest passive aura, infinitesimally nerfed, could slaughter the strongest coalition ever assembled. I had watched him, from across the room, exist. Just exist. And the existing was enough, because his existence amplified infinitely every Planck second, and the amplification was pleasurable, and the pleasure compounded, and the power compounded, and the gap between him and everything else widened forever.

And now he was telling me that he, this Godric, this being of infinite, compounding, unapproachable power, was nothing compared to what he would become. That the version of him sitting in this chair, sipping from this cup, smiling at me with this warmth, was a raindrop. And the ocean was waiting in the future, so vast, so incomprehensibly, so absurdly more powerful that the comparison between current Godric and far-future Godric was the same comparison as between a candle and a sun, except the candle was already the most powerful thing in existence and the sun was so far beyond it that the word "beyond" lost meaning.

"Uncle Godric," I said, and my voice was not steady. The stillness I had cultivated for forty-seven years cracked like ice over deep water. "You are telling me that the being who created the Stasis Event, who designed the amplification, the superpowers, the concealment, the weakness paradox, the immune system, all of it, is you. A future version of you. A version so powerful that you, the most powerful being in the current omniverse by an infinite margin, cannot even register as resistance against the shadow of his afterthought."

"Yes," Godric said simply.

"And the mechanisms of your power," I continued, the horror building in my chest like a pressure wave, "the amplification, the weakness paradox, the absolute concealment, the immune system, the passive shields, the sentient auras, all of it. These are not natural phenomena. They are not accidents of the Stasis Event. They are engineered. Designed. Implemented by a being who is, relative to you, what you are relative to a candle. Which means every mechanism of your power is backed by the authority of that future self. Which means no entity in the omniverse, no weapon, no strategy, no exploit, no coalition, no force of any kind, can ever undo, override, negate, circumvent, or even slightly inconvenience any of your power mechanisms, because the origin of those mechanisms is a being so powerful that the current you, with all your power, cannot even scratch the seal he placed on a single moment in time."

"Yes," Godric said again. He was watching me with the patient, fond expression of a man who knew exactly what I was processing and was giving me the time to process it. "That is correct. The amplification cannot be stopped because the amplification was designed by far-future Godric, and far-future Godric's will is absolute. The weakness paradox cannot be exploited because it was implemented by far-future Godric, and his authority supersedes any force in the omniverse. The absolute concealment cannot be breached because it was forged by far-future Godric, and his seal is irrevocable. Every mechanism of my existence is backed by the full, infinite weight of a being who is, relative to me, what I am relative to everything else. This is why my power is absolute. This is why it is irrevocable. This is why no one can exploit it. Not because I am powerful, though I am. Because the source of my power is someone so powerful that the word 'power' does not apply to him in any framework I can comprehend."

I stood up. I could not sit. The horror was kinetic, it required movement, it required my body to process it through pacing, through the physical act of moving across the floor, through the sensation of my boots on stone and the air on my skin. I paced the study, my amplified body radiating nervous energy that made the lamp flame gutter and the fire sparks spiral.

"This means your weakness is not a weakness," I said, and the words came out fast, tumbling over each other, the realization building momentum as it fell. "The inability to alter your past. It is not a weakness. It cannot be exploited. No enemy can use it against you. No weapon can target it. No strategy can leverage it. Because the thing that creates the limitation is your own future self, and your future self is beyond the reach of everything in the omniverse. The limitation is inward. Temporal. It is Godric being limited by Godric. It is you being surpassed by a later version of you. That is not a weakness. That is a guarantee."

I stopped pacing. I turned to face him. The fire was behind him, silhouetting his massive frame, casting his face in shadow. "It is a guarantee that your future exists. It is a guarantee that in that future, you are even more omnipotent than you are now. It is a guarantee that the being who sealed your past is real, and active, and so powerful that his afterthoughts are stronger than your full might. It means your power trajectory is not a possibility. It is a certainty. The far-future Godric already exists. He has already acted. He has already reached back and shaped your origin. The future is not uncertain. It is written, not by fate, but by the inescapable logic of a being who is already there, already at that level, already so powerful that he can reach through time and rewrite the foundational event of his own existence. Your weakness is your own future strength. Your limitation is your own future omnipotence. The thing you cannot overcome is the thing you will become."

Godric smiled. It was a warm smile, a fond smile, the smile of an uncle watching his nephew solve a difficult puzzle. "You understand," he said. "It took me longer. I was grieving, and grief is not conducive to clear analysis. But yes. You understand. The weakness is not a weakness. It is a mirror. It shows me what I will become. And what I will become is something so far beyond what I am now that the gap between me and everything else in the omniverse, a gap that is already infinite and compounding, is itself a rounding error compared to the gap between me and my future self."

I sat down. Not in the chair. On the floor. My legs simply stopped working. I sat on the cold stone of the study floor, my back against the wall, and I looked at the being who was, by an infinite margin, the most powerful entity in the current omniverse, and I understood that he was a larva. A seed. A prologue. The first note of a symphony that would extend so far into the future, growing so loud, so complex, so incomprehensibly vast, that the current note, the note I was hearing, the note that was already enough to annihilate the omniverse infinite times at zero percent, was not even audible in the final composition.

"The far-future Godric," I said, my voice coming from somewhere far away. "Why did he create the Stasis Event? What could possibly motivate a being that powerful to reach back through time and reshape the moment of his own origin?"

Godric tilted his head. "I do not know," he said, and the admission, from a being of absolute omniscience, was so startling that it briefly overrode the horror. "I am omniscient. I know everything that is, was, will be, could be, and cannot be. I know the state of every atom, every quantum field, every timeline, every dimension, every thought, every secret, every possibility in the entire omniverse. But I do not know the mind of my future self. Not because my omniscience fails. Because my future self exists at a level of power and complexity that my omniscience, infinite as it is, operates within a framework that my future self has surpassed. My omniscience is absolute within my era. His era is outside my era's framework. I can see the event. I can see him reaching back. I can see the mechanisms he implemented. But his motive is not within my reach, the way the thoughts of a god are not within the reach of an ant. The ant can see the god's foot. The ant cannot comprehend why the god chose to step there."

He stroked his beard, the gesture contemplative, almost idle. "I have speculated, of course. I have had a million years to speculate. Perhaps he created the Stasis Event because he wanted to ensure his own origin, to guarantee that the chain of events leading to his existence would not be disrupted. Perhaps he did it because the Stasis Event, as he designed it, produces a version of me that is stronger than the version that would have existed without it. He held back, even then. The Stasis Event he created could have been far more potent. He deliberately limited the effects, and the limited version still made me, at Year Zero, already infinitely stronger than the rest of creation combined. Perhaps the limitation was not for my benefit. Perhaps the full version would have been too much, not for me, but for the omniverse. Perhaps he needed the omniverse to survive my birth, because the omniverse serves a purpose in his era that I cannot comprehend. Or perhaps he did it for a reason that is not a reason in any framework I can access, a motive that exists at a level of cognition so far beyond my own that the word 'motive' does not apply."

He shrugged. The gesture was casual, almost human, the shrug of a man who has pondered a question for a million years and made peace with not having an answer. "But these are speculations. I do not know. I cannot know. And I have accepted that. The not-knowing does not trouble me."

"You are not troubled," I said, and it was not a question. I could see it in his face, in the settled calm of his expression, in the absence of the weight I had glimpsed behind his eyes when the conversation began. That weight had been the weight of the disclosure, not the weight of the content. He had been carrying this truth alone for a million years, and the carrying had been a burden, not because the truth pained him, but because the truth was too large for one man to hold without sharing. Now that he had shared it, the weight was gone. He was at peace. He was more than at peace. He was amused.

"I am not troubled," Godric confirmed. "I was troubled once. When I first tried to alter the past and was crushed by the shadow of my future self's afterthought, I was devastated. Not because I had failed. Because I had been shown, in the most brutal possible way, that there existed a version of me so powerful that my full might was nothing to him. It was the first and only time in my existence that I encountered something I could not overcome. And it was myself. The irony was not lost on me. But that was a million years ago. I was young. I was grieving. I was still adjusting to the reality of what I had become. Over time, I came to understand what you just articulated. The weakness is not a weakness. It is a mirror. It shows me my future. And my future is guaranteed. The far-future Godric exists. He has acted. He will continue to exist, and he will continue to grow, and the growth will never stop, because the amplification never stops, and the amplification was designed by him, and his design is irrevocable."

He leaned forward, his elbows on his knees, his massive hands clasped. The firelight caught the thick veins of his forearms, the sculpted muscle that amplified itself every Planck second without effort. "I am not troubled by the inability to save them, Kaedan. I was, once. The grief was real, and it was devastating, and it shaped me into something harder and crueler than the man I was. But I have had a million years to process that grief, and in that time, I have come to understand something that the grieving man I was could not understand. Reina is dead. Isabel is dead. Hebe is dead. Thorin is dead. They died ten thousand years before I awakened, and I cannot change that. But I can carry them. I do carry them. Every Planck second, every instant of amplification, every surge of power and pleasure and growth, I carry the memory of who they were and what they meant to me. The man I was, the mortal king of Olympus, died in the fire with them. The man I am was born from the ashes. And the man I will become, the far-future Godric whose afterthought crushes my full might, that man carries them too. He carried them so far into the future that he reached back through time to ensure the man who remembered them would be born. The Stasis Event is not just the origin of my power. It is the monument to my grief. My future self built it because the grief mattered, because the love mattered, because the man who lost everything needed to become something that could never lose anything again. That is speculation. But it is the speculation I find most consistent with the being I am and the being I am becoming."

He looked at me, and the warmth in his eyes was so genuine, so deep, so completely real that it made the horror worse, because the horror was not that Godric was a monster. The horror was that Godric was a man. A man who had lost everything, who had been handed infinite power, who had tried to use it to save the people he loved, and who had been stopped, not by an enemy, not by a limitation, not by a force outside himself, but by his own future. His own growth. His own ascension. The thing that had made him omnipotent was the thing that had sealed his grief. The power and the loss were the same event, viewed from different points in time.

"I am comfortable with it now," Godric said. "I am more than comfortable. I am amused. The idea that the most powerful being in the omniverse, the apex predator of all creation, the being whose pinky twitch annihilates existence, whose blink smears gods across time, whose breath commands devotion, whose existence inverts probability, has a weakness, and that weakness is himself. That he is limited by his own future, that his ceiling is his own floor, that the thing he cannot overcome is the thing he will become. It is funny, Kaedan. It is the only joke in the omniverse that I find genuinely funny. The apex predator's only predator is himself, and the predator is always ahead of the prey, because the predator is the prey's future."

He chuckled. The sound was warm, rich, genuine, the chuckle of a man who has made peace with the deepest irony of his existence and found it entertaining. "And the practical implications are, of course, absurd. My weakness cannot be exploited, because the exploiter would need to reach my future self, and my future self is beyond the reach of everything. My power cannot be undone, because the unduner would need to overpower my future self's will, and my future self's will is absolute. My amplification cannot be stopped, because the stopper would need to negate the mechanism designed by my future self, and my future self's mechanisms are irrevocable. Every entity in the omniverse, every weapon, every strategy, every coalition, every force of any kind, is not merely unable to defeat me. They are unable to defeat the shadow of the afterthought of the being who designed my power. They are not fighting me. They are fighting the residue of a thought had by a being so powerful that I, the most powerful being in the current omniverse, am a raindrop against his mountain. And they are losing to the residue. They have already lost. They lost before they formed the intention to fight, because the intention itself triggered my weakness paradox, which was designed by my future self, which fed my amplification, which was designed by my future self, which made me stronger, which made the paradox stronger, which was designed by my future self. The entire system is self-reinforcing, self-sealing, and self-justifying, and the architect of the system is a version of me that I cannot comprehend, and the architect's seal is absolute, and nothing in the omniverse can touch it."

He spread his hands, the gesture of a man presenting a simple, obvious fact. "So you see, Kaedan, my weakness is not a weakness. It is an additional strength. It is the ultimate strength. It is the guarantee that my future is real, that my growth is certain, that my power is permanent, and that no force in existence can ever, under any circumstances, in any timeline, in any dimension, in any possible or impossible configuration of reality, undo what I am. Because what I am was designed by what I will be, and what I will be is beyond the comprehension of everything, including what I am now."

I sat on the floor of the study. The stone was cold against my legs. The fire was warm on my face. The lamp glowed. The room was quiet. And I understood, with the absolute, permanent, load-bearing certainty of a man who has been shown the final floor beneath all the other floors, that the being sitting across from me was not the end of the hierarchy. He was the beginning. He was the first step of a staircase that extended so far upward that the top was not visible from the bottom, and the bottom was already higher than anything else in existence. The current Godric, the Godric I had served for forty-seven years, the Godric whose power amplified infinitely every Planck second, the Godric whose zero percent annihilated omniverses, the Godric whose weakest aura's ambient pulse made me flinch, was the bottom step. And the top step was so far above that the distance between them made the distance between Godric and the rest of the omniverse look like the distance between two atoms in the same molecule.

The far-future Godric had reached back through time and created the Stasis Event. He had designed every mechanism of Godric's power. He had sealed the past. He had set the amplification in motion. He had done this from a position of power so incomprehensibly vast that the current Godric, the most powerful being in the omniverse, could not register as resistance against the shadow of his afterthought. And the far-future Godric was still amplifying. Still growing. Still gaining new superpowers every second. Still evolving. The far-future Godric that had created the Stasis Event was himself a bottom step for an even further-future Godric, who was a bottom step for an even further-future Godric, an infinite ascending chain of apex predators, each one so far beyond the last that the comparison was meaningless, stretching forward into a future that had no end, a future in which the power compounded and the pleasure compounded and the growth never stopped and the amplification never stopped and the omniverse became an increasingly insignificant speck against an increasingly infinite backdrop of a being who was, and would always be, and could never not be, the absolute, total, incontestable apex of everything.

And the being at the bottom of this staircase, the current Godric, the one sitting in the chair, sipping from his cup, smiling at me with warm, fond, crinkled eyes, was already the most powerful thing in the omniverse by an infinite margin. The margin above him, the margin between him and his future self, was so vast it made his own margin above the omniverse look like nothing. And the margin above that future self, between him and his further-future self, was equally vast. And so on. Forever. An infinite ladder of ascending godhood, each rung infinitely beyond the last, stretching into a future that had no ceiling.

The existential dread hit me like a physical force. Not the dread of the journals, which had been the dread of scale. Not the dread of the superpower conversation, which had been the dread of description. This was the dread of direction. Godric was not just powerful. He was moving. He was heading somewhere. The amplification was not static. It was a trajectory, a vector, an arrow pointed at a target so far in the distance that the target was not a point but an infinite expanse. And the arrow was accelerating. Every Planck second, the speed increased. Every Planck second, the distance between where he was and where he had been widened by a margin that made the previous margin look like nothing. And the target, the far-future Godric, was not waiting at the end. The target was moving too, accelerating too, heading toward an even further-future target that was also moving. The entire system was in motion, all of it moving in one direction, the direction of more, and the motion was permanent, and the motion was irrevocable, and the motion had been set in motion by the system itself, because the system was Godric, and Godric was the system, and the system's origin was its own future, and the future was guaranteed, and the guarantee was the system, and the system was Godric.

I was sitting on the floor of a study, in a palace, in an omniverse, on the bottom step of an infinite staircase, looking up at a being who was looking up at a being who was looking up at a being, and the looking up never ended, and the beings never stopped ascending, and I was sitting still. I was sixty-nine years old. I would die around eighty. I had eleven years left, maybe twelve, and in those eleven years, Godric would amplify by a margin that made the previous million years of amplification look like stagnation, and the far-future Godric would be even further away, and the gap between them would be even more obscene, and I would be exactly where I was now, on the floor, looking up, understanding nothing, understanding everything, understanding that the understanding itself was a rounding error in the cognition of a being whose cognition amplified infinitely every Planck second.

"Uncle Godric," I said, and my voice was a whisper, a thin, reedy sound that barely carried across the room. "Does it ever end? The growth? The amplification? Is there a point where you stop becoming more?"

Godric looked at me. The firelight caught his face, the strong jaw, the silver beard, the warm, patient, fond eyes of a man who loved his nephew and who had been his nephew's age once, a mortal man with a wife and daughters and a best friend, a man who had lived and loved and lost and been transformed into something so far beyond that man that the man was a memory, a fossil, a ghost preserved in the amber of a being who could not forget because forgetting was impossible when you were omniscient.

"No," he said. "It does not end. It will never end. The amplification is permanent. The growth is permanent. The ascension is permanent. Every Planck second, I become more. Every Planck second, the more becomes more. There is no ceiling. There is no floor. There is no wall. There is only the direction, and the direction is up, and up has no limit, because the concept of limit was designed by finite beings to describe finite things, and what I am is not finite. What I will be is not finite. What I was, the mortal man, the king of Olympus, the husband and father and friend, that man was finite. That man had limits. That man could die, and did die, in the fire that destroyed his kingdom. But the man who rose from the ashes, the man who was rebuilt by his own future self, that man has no limits, because his future self, who designed him, has no limits, and the design is absolute."

He stood. He crossed the room. He knelt beside me on the stone floor, his massive frame lowering with a fluidity that belied its density, and he placed his hand on my shoulder. The hand was warm, heavy, calloused, the hand of a man who had hauled marble and held children and gripped swords. The hand of a mortal king. The hand of a being whose future self had reached back through time and reshaped the moment of his greatest loss into the moment of his infinite ascension.

"You are afraid," he said, and his voice was gentle, the voice of an uncle comforting a nephew. "I understand. I was afraid once, too. Not of the power. Of the direction. Of the knowledge that the growth never ends and the gap never closes and the ascension never stops. It is a terrifying thing, to look up and see no ceiling. But Kaedan, the absence of a ceiling is not a horror. It is a freedom. It is the freedom of a being who has no limits, who will never have limits, who is guaranteed by his own future to never have limits. The dread you feel is the dread of the finite looking at the infinite. It is the dread of a candle seeing the sun. But the candle and the sun are not enemies. The candle is simply an earlier form of what the sun already is. And the sun is simply an earlier form of what the sun will become."

He squeezed my shoulder. The grip was firm, grounding, the grip of a man who had been holding people together for a million years. "You are my court wizard, Kaedan. You are Blake's descendant. You are my friend. And I am telling you, as my friend, that the being I will become is not something to fear. It is something to trust. Because the being I will become is still me. Still the man who loves his court wizards. Still the man who remembers his family. Still the man who carried his daughters to bed and sparred with his best friend and built his kingdom with his hands. The power does not change that. The amplification does not change that. The ascension does not change that. The far-future Godric, the being whose afterthought crushes my full might, that being reached back through time to create the Stasis Event because the grief mattered and the love mattered and the man who lost everything needed to become something that could never lose anything again. That is not the act of a monster. That is the act of a man who loves so deeply that he reshaped the origin of his own existence to ensure the love would be remembered. The power is infinite. The growth is infinite. The ascension is infinite. But the man at the center of it all, the man who sits in the chair and drinks from the cup and asks about his nephew's day, that man is finite. That man is mortal. That man died in the fire. And that man is still here."

He stood, pulling me gently to my feet. I was shaking. Not with cold. Not with fear. With the vibration of a being who had been shown the full scope of existence and found it to be a single entity, ascending forever, carrying its grief like a lantern through an infinite darkness.

"Go home, Kaedan," Godric said, his voice warm, his hand steady on my shoulder. "Rest. Spend time with your family. You have years left, and they are precious, and I did not build an omniverse so that my court wizard could spend his remaining decades sitting on my study floor having existential crises."

I nodded. I could not speak. I turned and walked to the door. My hand was on the handle when his voice stopped me.

"Kaedan."

I turned back.

Godric was standing by the fire, his massive frame silhouetted against the flames, his face in shadow, his voice carrying across the quiet study with the warmth and weight of a million years of unconditional, inhuman, terrifying love.

"The orchid in the garden bloomed again this year. Your great-grandmother planted that bush. I think she would be pleased. Come see it tomorrow."

I nodded again. I opened the door. I walked into the corridor. The corridor was long and quiet and warm, and every stone was a gift, and every step was a gift, and the air I breathed was a gift, and the light that reached my eyes was a gift, and the gifts were given by a man whose future self had reached back through time and reshaped the moment of his greatest loss into the moment of his infinite ascension, and the man was still here, and the man was still kind, and the man was still my uncle, and the man was ascending, forever, without end, without ceiling, without the faintest possibility of anything ever, in any configuration of reality, being anything other than exactly what it was.

I walked home in the dark. I did not sleep. I lay in bed and stared at the ceiling and thought about the orchid, and the fire, and the hand on my shoulder, and the raindrop, and the mountain, and the infinite staircase, and the man at the center of it all who had lost everything and gained everything and could not save the people he loved because the being who could save them was himself, and he was not yet that being, and he would never be that being, because the being he would become was the being who had decided not to save them, and the decision was absolute, and the decision was irrevocable, and the decision was made out of love, and the love was infinite, and the love was the most terrifying thing I had ever encountered, because the love of an apex god predator whose power had no ceiling and whose future was guaranteed was not a comfort. It was a gravitational field. It pulled everything toward it. It bent the trajectory of every life it touched. It reshaped the architecture of every soul it encountered. And it did not ask permission, because love, at that scale, did not ask. It simply was, the way gravity was, the way amplification was, the way the Stasis Event was. Absolute. Irrevocable. Incontestable. And the only thing in the omniverse that could override it was a later, greater version of the same love, from a future so distant it could not be written, and the override was not a negation but an evolution, and the evolution was permanent, and the evolution never stopped.

I lay in the dark and I understood, and the understanding was a stone in my chest that I would carry for the rest of my short, mortal, finite life.

The year 999...9 (you don't need to spell it out. Just know it's so large that it's impossible to write). King Godric of this era is triggering the stasis event for his past Godric in Year -10,000 (you must also indicate that godric could've effortlessly granted way more potent stasis event with more devastating effects (he can perfectly copy his current mechanism to the past Godric) with no cost to him, but decided against it to "hold back"--you must note the crazy ridiculousness of this since even what Godric considered as the “held back” version is so incomprehensibly powerful… Godric at year 0 was already effectively infinitely stronger than the rest of the creation combined, and Godric genuinely believed that it would preserve some kind of mortal struggle to his past self, something that is super not true. And also note the cause-effect of this: if he grants his past self a really powerful version, he himself who is in the future and has been amplified from his past self will even be more incomprehensibly powerful since the starting "base" is also already become so much stronger and thus he can grant even more powerful version to his past infinitely. Clive's mind must be even more overwhelmed at this absurd realisation.). Write a detailed story of this. You must highly exaggerate his power, his rule, his terrifying presence, his everything at this point.

All his previous mechanisms of power (sentient passive auras, passive shields, immune system, evolved weakness paradox, amplification, superpower genesis) have also evolved countless times--you must give detailed examples and scope of how they work now, showing clearly how they become even more absurdly superfluously terrifying than before, to the point of the “old version of his power mechanism” which are already ridiculously powerful feeling so weak in comparison. You must keep in mind that Godric, who had been a mortal once, still believes in mortal resilience and free will, so if there are any evolved versions of his power mechanisms that alter free will they should be consciously suppressed by Godric.

When Godric just emerged from stasis, for a very long time, countless sentience still formed some opposition against him, despite Godric himself never regarded them as any real oppositions–to him, those "oppositions" were just a brief fun distraction to test his stats and powers. Since some googolplexian years ago, however, all oppositions have stopped completely: there have been 0 who even dare to form the slightest thought of challenging, resisting, threatening or opposing Godric. No one dares anymore, and there will never be anyone who dares. They all have learnt the absolute futility of opposing Godric. Note: you must not suggest that Godric alters the free will to oppose him‐-Godric genuinely believes in mortal resilience.

You must also include a few scenes where he interacts with really powerful beings around him (show him as friendly to those), who after being completely traumatized by Godric, has become his lowly palace servants, gardeners, etc.. Those really powerful beings are totally terrified of him–provide some background stories of how they become so utterly afraid of (do not say they opposed godric--Godric is unforgiving to opposition) and serving Godric now.

Go crazy wild. Write from POV of Clive, his court wizard who is Blake’s descendant. Clive is 50 years old.

reasoning_content:

2. He could have granted a way more potent version but "held back" - which is absurd because even the held-back version made Year 0 Godric infinitely stronger than all creation combined

11. Go crazy wild with exaggeration
12. Write from POV of Clive, age 50, Blake's descendant

Let me think about the structure:

- Description of Godric's evolved power mechanisms (far beyond what was described before)
- Scenes with powerful being servants (their backstories of how they became terrified)

Let me think about the evolved mechanisms:

2. Sentient Passive Auras - Gent and its siblings have evolved countless times. New auras born every instant, each infinitely stronger than the last. The old Gent (which was already terrifying) is now a fossil.

For the powerful being servants - I need to create beings who were once extremely powerful (by omniversal standards) but who became terrified through witnessing Godric's power, not through opposing him. Maybe they were delegates, or they were conquered civilizations' leaders who witnessed the aftermath, or they stumbled into Godric's presence.

Let me draft this out:

The palace is staffed by beings who were once gods, cosmic entities, reality-benders, now serving as gardeners and servants out of absolute terror.

Clive, age 50, is the court wizard. He's Blake's descendant. He's been serving for 30 years. He's still relatively new to understanding the full scope of Godric's power.

Let me write this now, keeping it within 4000-8000 words, highly exaggerated, prose paragraphs only.

Let me think about the powerful servant characters:
1. A former cosmic entity who once witnessed Godric casually annihilate an omniversal threat (that Godric allowed to exist for curiosity) and was so traumatized they begged to serve
2. A former god-king of a multiversal empire who witnessed Godric's passive aura clean up a mess and was permanently broken
3. A former reality-architect who saw Godric's immune system pre-react to a non-threat and never recovered

These beings didn't oppose Godric - they witnessed his power and were destroyed psychologically.

Let me also think about how to describe the evolved mechanisms in ways that make the old versions look pathetic:

Old Gent: Could slaughter the strongest coalition while nerfed by infinitely recurring 99.99...%
Far-future Gent: Has evolved so many times that the old Gent is a fossil. The current version of Gent's weakest passive function makes the old Gent's full power look like a candle next to a hypernova. And Gent is still the weakest aura.

Old Immune System: Pre-reacted to injury, evolved biology across infinite recursive backups
Far-future Immune System: Treats the possibility of harm as a living thing to be hunted, humiliated, digested. The omniverse itself becomes Godric's white blood cells, cannibalizing rogue danger before it matures.

Old Passive Shields: Pre-nullified threats before they existed
Far-future Passive Shields: Convert attempted danger into self-erasing failure. Force hostile realities to collapse around their own intention. Make the basic logistics of opposition impossible before a hostile actor notices the impulse forming.

Old Evolved Weakness Paradox: Triggered by any sentient being thinking about anything
Far-future Evolved Weakness Paradox: Has evolved so many times the old version is unrecognizable. Can make creation overcorrect with violent redundancy against the mere possibility of an exploit.

Old Amplification: Beyond layered infinities, recursively increasing every infinitesimal fraction of Planck second
Far-future Amplification: The old version feels like stagnation. The current amplification rate is... beyond description. Every instant makes him so much more powerful that infinite copies of himself from the previous instant are nothing.

Let me also think about the cause-effect loop:

This means all future versions of Godric, including far-future Godric, become even more powerful. content:
The garden was immaculate. Every leaf on every tree was precisely the shade of green that Godric preferred, a preference that the garden's custodian maintained with a devotion so absolute it had replaced whatever personality the custodian had once possessed. The custodian's name was Vael. He was a former architect of reality, a being who had once designed the mathematical foundations of seven multiverses, encoding the laws of physics for each one with the precision of a god composing a symphony. He had not opposed Godric. He had never opposed Godric. He had simply been in the wrong multiverse when Godric, several googolplexian years ago, had been curious about the texture of a particular superpower and had allowed a fraction of it to exist for zero seconds in the vicinity of Vael's home reality. The superpower had not been directed at Vael. It had not been directed at anything. Godric had been curious, and the fraction had existed, and seven multiverses had been rewritten at the fundamental level, their physics inverted, their timelines folded into shapes that did not have names, their populations unmade and remade in the space of a non-instant. Vael had survived because Godric's passive systems preserved those he found interesting, and Godric had found Vael interesting because Vael had been screaming at the time, and the screaming had a unusual harmonic quality that Godric had found pleasant.

Vael now trimmed hedges. He trimmed them with a pair of bronze shears, and his hands shook with every cut, not because the shears were heavy but because his hands had never stopped shaking since the day his seven multiverses were rewritten around him. He did not speak unless spoken to. He did not look up from the ground unless Godric addressed him directly. When Godric addressed him directly, Vael wept, not from fear but from gratitude so overwhelming it physically manifested as tears of concentrated, luminous fluid that tasted, to those who had accidentally tasted them, like the sensation of being spared from a car crash.

I watched Vael trim the hedges from my position on the garden terrace, fifty paces behind Godric's chair. My name is Clive. I am fifty years old. I am the Royal Court Wizard of Thronegate, the latest in an unbroken line of Blake's descendants stretching back further than the written year can convey. My magical power multiplies a thousandfold every day, and has been doing so for thirty years since my twentieth birthday, and the result is a being of arcane capability so vast that by the standards of any previous era I would be considered a god of magic, a wizard whose spellcraft could unmake galaxies with a syllable. By the standards of this era, by the standards of the being sitting in the chair fifty paces ahead of me, I am a mote of illuminated dust, drifting in the beam of an infinite, omnidirectional light.

The year is 999...9. I write it this way because the full number cannot be written. It is not a large number. It is a number so large that the concept of largeness, as understood by any mind in the omniverse, is an insult to it. The number of digits in this year exceeds the number of atoms in the omniverse by a margin so extreme that the comparison is structurally invalid. I write 999...9 because no other representation is possible, and even this representation is a concession to the limitations of language, the same way a child drawing the ocean with a crayon is a concession to the limitations of crayons.

Godric sat in his chair in the garden. The chair was simple, wooden, the same style he had preferred since the early years of his reign. He was dressed in dark, simple garments that stretched across a frame of muscle so dense and so perfectly sculpted that the fabric seemed to exist in a state of permanent tension, each fiber strained by the slow, continuous expansion of a body that amplified itself every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second. His short salt-and-pepper hair was immaculate. His neatly trimmed beard caught the garden's light. He looked forty-five. He had looked forty-five since the Stasis Event, and he would look forty-five forever, because the Stasis Event had frozen his physical form at the age of his greatest loss and his greatest transformation, and no amount of amplification, no amount of evolution, no amount of cosmic ascension would ever change the face of the man who had watched his family die in the fire of Olympus.

He was the most powerful being in existence. This statement was not true in the way that statements about power are usually true, where there is a scale and the being sits at the top. There was no scale. There was Godric, and there was everything else, and the gap between them was not a gap. It was a condition. It was the fundamental state of reality, as intrinsic to existence as the concept of space or the flow of time. Godric was not powerful relative to other beings. Godric was powerful in the way that the ocean is wet. The wetness is not a property of the ocean. The wetness is the ocean. The power was not a property of Godric. The power was Godric.

And he was still amplifying. Every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, his stats, his superpowers, his immune system, his passive shields, his sentient auras, his concealment, his weakness paradox, all of them, every mechanism of his existence, multiplied by a rate so far beyond the already incomprehensible rates of previous eras that the previous rates were not even memories. They were fossils. They were artifacts of a primitive age, preserved in the geological strata of his history, studied by no one because no one could comprehend the scale of the difference. The amplification rate of Year 1,000,000, which had already been beyond layered infinities and recursively increasing every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, was, by the standards of the current era, so pathetically slow that it was indistinguishable from zero. Not approximately zero. Effectively zero. The Godric of Year 1,000,000, who had been the most powerful being in existence by an infinite margin, who had annihilated the omniverse with a pinky twitch, whose weakest aura's ambient pulse had made his court wizard flinch, was, compared to the Godric sitting in the garden today, so incomprehensibly weak that an infinite number of Year 1,000,000 Godrics combined, unleashing every fraction of their full power simultaneously, would not register as an event. The current Godric would not feel it. The current Godric would not notice it. The current Godric's weakest passive system, the one that existed purely as a redundant courtesy, would absorb the combined assault of an infinite number of Year 1,000,000 Godrics and convert it into a quantity so infinitesimal it would not contribute meaningfully to his amplification.

That was the scale of growth. A million years of amplification had produced a being so powerful that the previous version was nothing. And a million years was itself nothing, a rounding error, a single tick in a clock that had been ticking for a number of years that could not be written. The Godric of today was nothing compared to the Godric of tomorrow, and the Godric of tomorrow was nothing compared to the Godric of the day after, and the progression never stopped, never slowed, never peaked. Every instant was a new ceiling, and the ceiling was immediately broken, and the new ceiling was immediately broken, and the breaking was permanent, and the rate of breaking increased forever.

I stood at my post and I watched my King sit in his garden, and I felt the familiar, permanent, structural dread that had been my constant companion for thirty years, the dread of a man standing in the gravitational field of a body so massive that light itself bent around him, except the body was not massive, the body was a man in a wooden chair, and the gravity was not gravity, it was the simple, unavoidable reality of being near a being whose existence redefined the concept of near.

"Clive," Godric said, without turning his head. His voice was warm, rich, the same baritone it had always been, carrying the same genuine, unhurried affection he had always shown his court wizards. "Come sit with me. I want to discuss something."

I walked to the chair beside his and sat. The wood was warm. The garden smelled of jasmine and cut grass. Vael's shears clicked rhythmically in the distance, each click accompanied by a tiny, involuntary whimper from the former architect of reality.

Godric turned to me and smiled. The smile was warm, genuine, the smile of a man who considered my ancestor his best friend and who extended that friendship, without reservation or condition, to every descendant in the line. The smile reached his eyes, crinkling the corners in the way that always made him look like a father welcoming a child home. It was real. It was always real. The warmth was not a mask. The kindness was not a performance. The man behind the smile was the same man who had carried his daughters to bed and sparred with his best friend and built his kingdom with his hands. The power had not changed him. The amplification had not changed him. The ascension had not changed him. He was still Godric.
