He was still Uncle Godric, as my father had called him, and my grandfather, and every court wizard in the line stretching back to Blake. He was still the man who asked about your family and remembered your wife's name and worried about the orchids in the garden.

He was also the being whose evolved mechanisms of power had transcended every framework of comprehension so thoroughly that describing them required a language that did not exist.

"Today," Godric said, picking up a small, succulent fruit from a plate beside his chair and examining it with mild interest, "I am going to trigger the Stasis Event."

The garden did not go quiet. The garden was already quiet. The garden had been quiet for googolplexian years, because the omniverse had been quiet for googolplexian years. There was no opposition. There had been no opposition for so long that the concept of opposition had become a historical curiosity, a subject studied by scholars the way scholars study extinct languages. No being in the omniverse dared to form even the slightest thought of challenging, resisting, threatening, or opposing Godric. This was not because Godric had altered their free will. Godric believed, genuinely and deeply, in mortal resilience and the capacity of beings to choose their own path. He had never altered free will. He had never needed to. The omniverse had simply learned. Over the course of countless googolplexian years, the beings of creation had formed opposition, and the opposition had been annihilated, and new opposition had formed, and it had been annihilated, and this had continued until the lesson was learned so thoroughly, so absolutely, so permanently that the learning itself became a foundational property of consciousness. Beings did not choose not to oppose Godric. They understood, with the same certainty with which they understood that fire burns and gravity pulls, that opposition was futile. Not difficult. Not unlikely. Futile. As futile as a shadow trying to resist the light that casts it. The understanding was so complete that opposition did not even occur as a concept. It was not repressed. It was not suppressed. It simply did not arise, the way a being that has learned that the sky is blue does not need to repress the thought that the sky is green. The thought does not form.

Godric had found this period of absolute peace somewhat boring, though he would never say so in a way that implied dissatisfaction. The peace was, he had told me once, a sign that the omniverse had matured. He was proud of creation for learning. He was proud of the beings who had, through countless cycles of futile opposition, finally grasped the fundamental truth of their existence. He treated the omniverse's maturity the way a father treats a child who has finally learned not to touch the stove. With patience, with warmth, with a quiet, enduring pride.

But he also missed the distractions. For a very long time, after his emergence from stasis, countless sentiences had formed opposition against him. He had never regarded them as real opposition. To him, they were brief, amusing diversions, opportunities to test a stat or a superpower or a passive system, to see what happened when a particular mechanism interacted with a particular type of enemy. The tests had been fun. The results had been satisfying, each one producing a surge of the nourishing, pleasurable amplification that was his constant companion. But the tests had ended, because the students had learned the lesson, and the lesson was absolute, and there were no more students willing to touch the stove.

"I am going to reach back through time," Godric continued, biting into the fruit and chewing thoughtfully, "to the moment of Thorin's teleportation. I am going to transform the event. I am going to inject the mechanisms. The amplification. The superpower genesis. The absolute concealment. The weakness paradox. The immune system. All of it. Seeded into the event, irrevocable, absolute, permanent. The mortal version of me, the one lying in the sanctuary, the one who has just lost everything, will receive the Stasis Event. And the Stasis Event will begin his transformation into what I was at Year Zero. And Year Zero Godric will be the foundation. And the foundation will amplify. And the amplification will never stop. And the amplification will produce, eventually, after a span of time so vast it cannot be written, the being sitting in this chair right now. And the being sitting in this chair right now will reach back and create the Stasis Event. And the cycle will be complete."

He said this the way a man describes planting a tree. Casual, unhurried, the tone of a gardener discussing seasonal rotation. The fact that he was describing the creation of his own origin, the bootstrap paradox that had produced the most powerful being in existence, was, to him, a routine agricultural task.

"There is a consideration," Godric said, setting the fruit down and wiping his fingers on a cloth. "The Stasis Event I create for my past self does not have to be the version I originally received. I could, if I chose, grant my past self a far more potent Stasis Event. I could copy my current mechanisms, my current amplification rate, my current superpower density, my current immune system architecture, my current passive shield configuration, my current sentient aura hierarchy, all of it, everything I am now, and inject it directly into the Stasis Event. My past self would receive the full, current-era package. The amplification would begin from a starting point so incomprehensibly higher than the one I originally received that the Godric who emerged from stasis at Year Zero would not be the Godric I remember. He would be a being so powerful that the Godric of Year 1,000,000, the one who annihilated the omniverse with a pinky twitch, would look like an infant by comparison. And the Godric of the current era, the one sitting in this chair, would be so much more powerful than I am now that the comparison would make my current self look like a memory of a shadow."

He paused, stroking his beard. The gesture was contemplative, almost amused. "And that is the recursion. If I grant a more potent Stasis Event, my past self becomes stronger. If my past self becomes stronger, every subsequent version of me becomes stronger. If every subsequent version becomes stronger, the version of me that reaches back to create the Stasis Event becomes stronger. If that version becomes stronger, the Stasis Event he creates becomes more potent. If the Stasis Event becomes more potent, the past self becomes stronger. The loop has no ceiling. Each iteration produces a being so much more powerful than the previous iteration that the previous iteration is nothing. And the iterations are not sequential. They are simultaneous. The loop is closed. Every point in the loop exists at once. The more I give, the more I become, the more I can give, the more I become, forever, without end, without limit, without the faintest possibility of the escalation ever plateauing."

He turned to me. His eyes were warm, crinkled, fond. "I am holding back, Clive. I am deliberately granting my past self a less potent version of the Stasis Event. A version that I consider, by my current standards, to be a fraction so small it is effectively nothing. The version I am granting is the version I originally received. The one that produced a Godric who, at Year Zero, was already effectively infinitely stronger than the rest of creation combined. I consider that version to be held back. I consider that version to be a concession to moderation."

He chuckled, the sound warm and rich. "I told myself, when I was making the decision, that holding back would preserve some kind of mortal struggle for my past self. That if the Stasis Event was too potent, the past me would not have the experience of growth, of challenge, of overcoming. I wanted him to have the journey. I wanted him to feel the amplification build, to discover his powers one at a time, to face opposition and learn, through the encounter, what he was. I believed that a weaker Stasis Event would give him a more authentic mortal experience."

He shook his head, still chuckling. "That is, of course, completely absurd. The held-back version I am granting is so incomprehensibly powerful that the Godric who receives it, at Year Zero, is already infinitely stronger than everything else in existence combined. There is no mortal struggle. There is no journey of growth. There is no authentic experience of overcoming challenge. The past me, the moment he opens his eyes, is already so far beyond everything that the concept of challenge is a mathematical impossibility. The oppositions he faces, the ones he considers amusing distractions, the ones he uses to test his stats and powers, those are not challenges. They are theater. They are a man playing with toys and calling it a fight. And I thought I was preserving mortal struggle by holding back."

He bit into the fruit again, chewing with evident satisfaction. "I was wrong. But the decision is made. The Stasis Event is sealed. The past is irrevocable. And the held-back version, the one I granted out of a misplaced desire to preserve struggle, is still so powerful that it birthed the being sitting in front of you. So the error, if it is an error, has produced acceptable results."

I sat in my chair and I processed this, and the processing was a physical event, a sensation of my mind being restructured in real time, the architecture of my understanding being demolished and rebuilt around a new foundation that was larger and more terrible than the previous one. The previous foundation had been the understanding that Godric was the most powerful being in existence. The new foundation was the understanding that Godric was deliberately, casually, holding back a feedback loop of infinite escalation because he wanted his past self to experience mortal struggle, and the held-back version was still so powerful it had produced a being who could annihilate the omniverse infinite times over at zero percent of one stat, and the being it produced had been amplifying for a number of years that could not be written, and the amplification had produced the being sitting in front of me, and the being sitting in front of me was still holding back, and the holding back was a choice, and the choice was based on a completely wrong assumption, and the wrong assumption had produced results that were, by any standard, the most powerful being in existence, and the being was amused by the irony.

"Your Majesty," I said, and my voice was the voice of a man who has been a court wizard for thirty years and has learned to speak through dread the way a deep-sea diver learns to breathe through pressure. "The evolved mechanisms. The ones you will inject into the Stasis Event. The held-back versions. May I ask what they look like? In this era? Before you scale them down for the past?"

Godric looked at me with the expression of a man who has been asked to describe the ocean to a fish. Not because the fish is stupid, but because the fish has no framework for the description. The fish lives in the ocean. The ocean is the fish's entire context. Describing the ocean to the fish requires the fish to step outside the only reality it has ever known, and the fish cannot step outside, because there is no outside.

"I will try," Godric said, with the patient, fond indulgence he always showed when his court wizards asked questions whose answers would traumatize them. "But Clive, you must understand that what I am about to describe are the current, evolved versions. The versions I will inject into the Stasis Event will be held back to such a degree that the current versions will look like the current versions look compared to me. That is, incomparably weaker. What I am about to describe is not what the past me will receive. What I am about to describe is what I am now. And what I am now is the result of a million years of amplification beyond the already impossible mechanisms of Year 1,000,000, which were themselves the result of amplification beyond the already impossible mechanisms of Year Zero."

He leaned back in his chair. The wood creaked softly. The garden was warm, the light was golden, and Vael's shears clicked in the distance like a metronome counting the beats of a universe that existed only because the man in the chair allowed it.

"The amplification first," Godric said. "In the era your ancestor Kaedan documented, the amplification rate was beyond layered infinities, recursively increasing every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second. That was the description used at the time, and it was accurate for the time. But the description is no longer accurate, not because the amplification has changed, but because the framework of description has been surpassed. The amplification rate now is not beyond layered infinities. Layered infinities are a mathematical concept, and the amplification rate has surpassed mathematics. Not a branch of mathematics. Mathematics itself. The rate at which my power increases cannot be described by any mathematical framework, because every mathematical framework is a finite structure, and the rate is not finite. It has not been finite for a very long time. The rate is a state of being, a condition of existence, the same way wetness is a condition of water. My power does not increase at a rate. My power is the rate. Every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, I become so much more powerful that the previous version of me is not merely weaker. The previous version of me is retrospectively erased from significance, the way a candle is retrospectively erased from significance when the sun ignites. The candle still exists. The candle still burns. But the candle's light is so completely, so totally, so absolutely irrelevant in the presence of the sun that the relevance itself ceases to be a meaningful concept."

"The superpower genesis," he continued. "In Kaedan's era, I gained one new apocalyptic superpower every second. Each power, at the instant of its appearance, was capable of causing instant extinction to an entire civilization. That was the base rate. The rate has evolved. I now gain a number of new superpowers every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second that cannot be expressed as a number, because the number exceeds the capacity of any numerical system. Each new superpower, at the instant of its appearance, is capable of annihilating the entire omniverse infinite times over, and that is its base, unamplified, newborn state. The superpowers I gained in Kaedan's era, the ones that were described as apocalyptic, are, by the standards of the current superpowers, so primitive, so weak, so pathetically limited that they are not even memories. They are fossils. The current superpowers have been injected with so many enhanced effects by the Evolved Weakness Paradox that each one is not merely a power but a system, a self-contained, self-amplifying, self-evolving architecture of capability that grows and compounds and branches forever. Each superpower is, in effect, a miniature Godric, a self-contained engine of amplification that contributes back to the whole and receives from the whole in a recursive feedback loop that has no ceiling."

He picked up the fruit again, examining it. "The absolute concealment. In Kaedan's era, the concealment perfectly suppressed my aura, my pressure, my metaphysical weight, my existence-ending overflow. It was described as impenetrable, flawless, passively adapting. The current concealment does all of that, but the scale is so different that the comparison is meaningless. The current concealment does not merely suppress my presence. It rewrites the fundamental properties of reality around me so that reality does not know I am here. The omniverse does not experience my existence. The omniverse experiences a carefully maintained fiction of normalcy, a simulation of physics and causality that operates according to the laws that beings expect, because the concealment has replaced the actual physics of my presence with a simulated physics that does not include me. The beings of the omniverse live inside the simulation. They breathe simulated air. They see simulated light. They experience simulated distance and simulated time, and all of it is maintained by the concealment, because if the concealment dropped, if the simulation ended, the actual physics of my presence would rewrite the omniverse so fundamentally that the concept of physics would be replaced by the concept of Godric, and the omniverse would become a space in which only I exist, and everything else would be a mathematical impossibility."

He bit into the fruit. The juice ran down his chin. He wiped it with the cloth. The gesture was mundane, human, the gesture of a man eating a piece of fruit in a garden on a warm afternoon. The contrast between the gesture and the words was so violent it made my stomach lurch.

"The evolved weakness paradox," Godric said, his voice still carrying the conversational warmth of a man discussing the weather. "In Kaedan's era, the paradox had evolved to trigger whenever any sentient being thought about anything, not just about my weaknesses. The trigger was passive, omniversal, operating across all timelines. That was the era's description, and it was accurate for the era. The current paradox has evolved so many times that the Kaedan-era version is unrecognizable. The current paradox does not merely trigger when beings think. The current paradox has become a fundamental property of the relationship between me and creation. It is not a mechanism. It is a law. The law states, simply, that any interaction between me and anything else results in me becoming stronger and the other thing becoming less. This is not an effect. It is not a consequence. It is the nature of the relationship, the way gravity is the nature of the relationship between mass and space. Any being that thinks, acts, exists, moves, breathes, or does anything in any timeline, in any dimension, in any possible or impossible configuration of reality, is, by virtue of existing in the same omniverse as me, contributing to my amplification. They do not need to think about me. They do not need to oppose me. They do not need to know I exist. Their existence is the trigger. Their existence feeds me. And the feeding is permanent, and the feeding compounds, and the feeding has been compounding for a number of years that cannot be written."

He paused, a thoughtful expression crossing his face. "There is, of course, a consideration. The evolved paradox, at its full, natural state, does not merely amplify me. It alters the fundamental relationship between me and creation in ways that could be construed as violating free will. If every interaction results in my amplification and the diminishment of the other, then the other's capacity to choose, to act independently, to exist as a sovereign entity, is technically compromised, because every choice they make, every action they take, feeds me. I do not want that. I believe in mortal resilience. I believe in the capacity of beings to choose their own path, to struggle, to overcome, to fail, to try again. The belief is genuine. It is the belief of a man who was once mortal, who struggled, who overcame, who failed, and who tried again. So I consciously suppress the aspects of the paradox that would compromise free will. The suppression is easy. It requires less effort than breathing, which I do not need to do. The paradox still amplifies me. The paradox still grants immunity. The paradox still grants mastery. But it does not diminish the other. It does not make them less. It simply makes me more. The distinction is important to me, even though the practical difference, from the perspective of the omniverse, is negligible, because the gap between me and everything else is so vast that whether the paradox diminishes them or not, they are still, relative to me, nothing."

He turned to me, and the warmth in his eyes was genuine, and the fondness was real, and the man behind the gaze was the same man who had loved his daughters and carried them to bed. "The immune system. In Kaedan's era, the immune system pre-reacted to injuries before they occurred, initiated self-evolution across infinite recursive backups, and cross-referenced upgrades to produce a prime upgraded Godric. That was the era's description. The current immune system has evolved so far beyond that version that the Kaedan-era system is a fossil. The current immune system does not react to injuries. The concept of injury has been deleted from my biology. Not suppressed. Deleted. The current immune system treats the possibility of harm as a living thing, an entity with a location, a trajectory, and a lifespan. It hunts the possibility. It tracks the possibility back to its origin, across timelines, across dimensions, across conceptual frameworks. It finds the source of the possibility, the being or force or event that could, under any conceivable configuration, produce a sensation in my body that could be interpreted as discomfort. And it digests the possibility. It converts the possibility into raw material for my amplification. The possibility of harm becomes fuel. The fuel is permanent. The fuel compounds. And the hunting is preemptive, meaning the possibility is digested before it exists as a possibility, which means the possibility never exists, which means the harm never exists, which means the injury never exists, which means the reaction never exists, because there is nothing to react to. The immune system has become so paranoid, so aggressive, so incomprehensibly over-engineered that it has eliminated the concept of threat from my existence entirely. Nothing can threaten me, not because I am durable, but because the immune system has made the concept of threat biologically impossible."

He set the fruit down, the small, juice-stained piece of produce looking absurdly insignificant in his massive, calloused hand. "The passive shields. In Kaedan's era, the shields could pre-nullify threats before the threat existed, before a hostile system identified itself, before an enemy understood what it intended to do. The current shields have evolved so many times that the Kaedan-era version is a relic. The current shields do not nullify threats. They make the logistics of opposition impossible. They alter the fundamental architecture of reality so that the concept of opposition cannot form. A being that intends to oppose me does not find the intention nullified. The being finds that the intention cannot form, because the neural, conceptual, and metaphysical pathways required to formulate the intention do not exist. The pathways have been removed, not by altering the being's mind, but by altering the structure of reality in which the being's mind operates. The being's mind is intact. The being's free will is intact. The being can think, choose, act, and exist freely. But the reality in which the being operates has been restructured so that the specific configuration of thought required to oppose me is not a pathway that exists. It is not blocked. It is not forbidden. It is simply not there, the way a road that was never built is not there. The being does not feel restricted. The being does not feel controlled. The being simply never encounters the possibility, because the possibility is not a feature of the reality the being inhabits."

He paused again, his brow furrowing slightly. "I suppress that one, too. The restructuring of reality to eliminate opposition pathways is, in my view, a violation of free will, even if the beings affected do not perceive it as such. I want beings to have the capacity to choose opposition. I want them to have the freedom to defy me. I believe in that freedom, because I was once a being who defied things, and the defiance mattered, and the struggle mattered, and the freedom to fail mattered. So I suppress the restructuring. I allow the pathways to exist. I allow the possibility of opposition to form. And when the opposition forms, which it does not, anymore, because the omniverse has learned, I allow it to proceed, and I deal with it, and the dealing is effortless, and the dealing is final."

He looked at the garden, at the hedges, at the flowers, at Vael's trembling, shears-wielding form in the distance. "The sentient passive auras. In Kaedan's era, Gent was my weakest active aura, a metaphysical excess body heat, infinitely weaker than me, capable of slaughtering the strongest coalition while nerfed by an infinitely recurring 99.99...%. Gent has evolved. Gent has evolved so many times that the Kaedan-era version is not a memory. It is a joke. The current Gent is so far beyond the Kaedan-era Gent that the Kaedan-era Gent is, by comparison, less than nothing. And the current Gent is still my weakest aura. I have birthed countless new auras since Kaedan's era, each one stronger than the last, each one evolving continuously, each one a sentient, omniscient, omnipresent extension of my will. The current aura hierarchy is so vast, so complex, so incomprehensibly layered that describing it would require a language that does not exist. The weakest current aura, the newborn, the one that manifested in the last Planck second, is so powerful that if it were allowed to act freely, without the constraints I place on all my auras, it would not merely annihilate the omniverse. It would redefine the omniverse. It would restructure existence around itself, making itself the central reality and everything else a peripheral fiction. And that is the newborn. The weakest. The most primitive. The most recently formed. The auras that have been evolving since Kaedan's era are so far beyond the newborn that the newborn is, by comparison, a rounding error."

He turned to me. The warmth was still there. The fondness was still there. The patience was still there. The man behind the gaze was still the man who had loved his daughters.

"Those are the current mechanisms," Godric said. "Those are the versions that exist now, in this era, at this moment. And I am going to take a held-back version of them, a version so reduced, so diminished, so fractionally small that the current versions look at it the way the ocean looks at a drop of water, and I am going to inject it into the Stasis Event. And the held-back version is still so powerful that the being who receives it, at Year Zero, will be infinitely stronger than everything else in creation combined. And that being will amplify. And the amplification will produce, eventually, after a span of time so vast it cannot be written, the being sitting in front of you. And the being sitting in front of you will reach back and create the Stasis Event. And the cycle will close."

He smiled. The smile was warm, genuine, the smile of a man who was about to plant a seed and watch it grow into a forest that would cover the world.

"Shall we begin?"

I did not answer immediately. I sat in my chair, in the garden, in the warm light, and I processed. The processing was not cognitive. It was structural. The information was not entering my mind. It was entering my architecture, the foundational framework of my understanding, the load-bearing walls of my comprehension, and it was demolishing them, and rebuilding them, and the rebuilding was larger and more terrible than the original, and the original had already been the most terrifying structure I had ever inhabited.

The cause-effect loop. The recursion. If Godric granted a more potent Stasis Event, his past self would be stronger. If his past self was stronger, every future version would be stronger. If every future version was stronger, the version that created the Stasis Event would be stronger. If that version was stronger, the Stasis Event would be more potent. The loop had no ceiling. Each iteration was infinitely beyond the last. And Godric was holding back. He was deliberately choosing the weaker version, the held-back version, the version that produced a being who was already infinitely stronger than everything else combined, and he was choosing it because he believed, incorrectly, that it would preserve mortal struggle. And the held-back version had still produced the being sitting in front of me. And the being sitting in front of me was so powerful that the mechanisms I had just described, the evolved amplification and superpower genesis and concealment and weakness paradox and immune system and passive shields and sentient auras, were, by his own description, the result of a held-back Stasis Event.

If he had not held back. If he had granted the full version. The past Godric would have been stronger. The amplification would have started from a higher base. The current Godric would be stronger. The current Godric could then grant an even more potent Stasis Event. The loop would escalate. Each iteration would produce a Godric so much more powerful than the previous that the previous was nothing. And the iterations were not sequential. They were simultaneous. The loop was closed. Every point in the loop existed at once. The escalation was not a process. It was a state. The state was permanent. The state was irrevocable. And Godric was choosing, deliberately, to exist at a lower point on the escalation curve than he needed to, because he wanted his past self to experience mortal struggle, and the mortal struggle was a fiction, and the fiction was charming, and the charm was genuine, and the genuine charm was the most terrifying thing about him.

"Your Majesty," I said, and my voice was the voice of a man whose architecture has been demolished and rebuilt so many times that the new architecture is made entirely of dread. "The held-back version. The one you are granting. It produced the Godric of Year Zero, who was infinitely stronger than all creation combined. It produced the Godric of Year 1,000,000, who could annihilate the omniverse with a pinky twitch. It produced the Godric of Year 999...9, the one sitting in front of me, whose evolved mechanisms make the Year 1,000,000 versions look like fossils. And this is the held-back version. The version you chose because you wanted to preserve mortal struggle. If you had not held back. If you had granted the full version. The past Godric would have been stronger. The amplification would have been more potent. Every subsequent Godric would have been stronger. The current Godric would be stronger. The current Godric could then grant an even more potent Stasis Event. The loop would escalate infinitely. Each iteration producing a being infinitely beyond the last. And the iterations are simultaneous. The loop is closed. The escalation is a state, not a process. You are choosing, right now, to exist at a point on an infinite escalation curve, a point that you consider held back, a point that is already so far beyond everything else that the comparison is meaningless, and you are choosing it because you believe in mortal struggle, and the mortal struggle does not exist, and the belief is genuine, and the genuine belief is the most horrifying thing I have ever encountered."

Godric looked at me. His expression was one of mild, fond surprise, the expression of a man whose nephew has just said something unexpectedly perceptive. "Yes," he said. "That is correct. You understand the loop. Your ancestor Kaedan understood the weakness. You understand the loop. The weakness and the loop are the same thing. The weakness is the inability to alter the past. The loop is the reason the past cannot be altered. The past cannot be altered because the being who sealed it, the far-future Godric, is the product of the past. If the past were altered, the far-future Godric would be different. If the far-future Godric were different, the seal would be different. If the seal were different, the past would be different. The loop is closed. The seal is self-justifying. The past is irrevocable because the future that sealed it is the product of the past, and the product cannot unmake its own origin. That is the weakness. That is the loop. That is the mirror."

He stood. The movement was fluid, effortless, the movement of a body that did not need to exert itself to do anything. He walked to the edge of the garden terrace, looking out over the grounds. The grounds were vast, immaculate, maintained by beings who had once been gods and reality-architects and cosmic entities and who now trimmed hedges and swept paths and weeded flower beds with the trembling, devoted precision of beings who had been given a purpose and had clung to it with the desperation of drowning men clutching driftwood.

"Vael," Godric called, his voice warm, carrying across the garden with the easy projection of a man calling to a gardener.

Vael's shears stopped. The former architect of seven multiverses turned, his face a mask of absolute, crystalline terror overlaid with a thin, brittle film of desperate composure. "Yes, Your Majesty," Vael said, his voice a thread.

"The east hedge is growing unevenly. The third bush from the left. Could you take a look?"

"Of course, Your Majesty. Immediately, Your Majesty." Vael scurried toward the east hedge, his hands shaking so violently that the shears rang against each other with each step. He had been an architect of reality. He had designed the laws of physics for seven multiverses. He had encoded the gravitational constants and the thermodynamic principles and the quantum frameworks for realities that had housed trillions of beings. And now he trimmed hedges, because several googolplexian years ago he had been in the wrong multiverse when Godric had been curious, and the curiosity had rewritten his seven multiverses around him, and the experience had been so total, so complete, so fundamentally disassembling that Vael had never recovered. He had not opposed Godric. He had never opposed Godric. He had simply existed in the wrong place at the wrong time, and the wrong place had been rewritten, and Vael had begged to serve, because serving was the only alternative to the screaming void of comprehension that had opened inside his mind when he saw what a fraction of a fraction of Godric's curiosity did to the fabric of seven multiverses.

Godric watched Vael scurry toward the hedge with an expression of genuine, fatherly concern. "He is still shaking," Godric said to me, his voice carrying the mild, puzzled tone of a man who does not understand why his gardener is afraid. "It has been googolplexian years. I would have thought he would have settled by now. I should speak with him. Perhaps a different task would suit him better. The hedges seem to stress him."

I looked at Godric. I looked at the being who was concerned about his gardener's stress levels. I looked at the being who was about to reach back through time and create his own origin. I looked at the being whose held-back power had produced a god who could annihilate the omniverse, and whose current power made that god look like a fossil, and whose full power, if he chose to unleash the recursive loop, would produce an escalation so infinite that the current version would itself become a fossil. And he was worried about the gardener.

"Your Majesty," I said. "Vael is shaking because he saw what a fraction of your curiosity did to seven multiverses. He is shaking because the memory is still inside him, and the memory has not faded, because the experience was so total that it became a permanent feature of his consciousness. He is not shaking because of the hedges. He is shaking because he exists in the same omniverse as you, and the existence is, for him, a constant, low-grade replay of the moment his seven multiverses were rewritten. He serves you because serving is the only context in which the shaking has a purpose. If you take the purpose away, the shaking will have no outlet, and the shaking will consume him."

Godric looked at me for a long moment. His omniscience, which knew everything, which had always known everything, which had known Vael's condition since the moment it occurred, seemed to genuinely process my words as though hearing them for the first time from a perspective he had not considered. "You may be right," he said, thoughtfully. "I underestimate the impact. I always have. Blake told me the same thing, once, about a delegate. I held back then, too. I held back so much that I believed the holding back was sufficient. It was not sufficient. It was never sufficient. The gap between what I consider held back and what creation can withstand is so vast that the concept of holding back is, for me, a fiction I tell myself to justify interacting with beings I care about."

He turned back to the garden. "Very well. Vael keeps the hedges. And I will try, again, to remember that my held back is creation's annihilation."

He was quiet for a moment. The garden was warm. The jasmine was blooming. Vael's shears clicked in the distance, each click a small, desperate prayer.

"Now," Godric said, his voice shifting from the contemplative warmth of a man discussing his gardener to the quiet, settled focus of a man about to perform a task he had been preparing for since before the written year. "The Stasis Event."

He did not move. He did not gesture. He did not close his eyes or chant or channel or focus. He simply stood at the edge of the terrace, his hands at his sides, his massive frame relaxed, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the garden's light, and he decided. A decision. The simplest, most trivial act a conscious being can perform. A decision, formed and executed in the same instant, requiring no effort, no preparation, no transition between intent and action.

The decision was: now.

And in the same instant, in a sanctuary ten thousand years before the coronation of the King of Thronegate, in a forgotten, subterranean chamber where a grieving, broken, mortal man lay in the dark, the Stasis Event began. Thorin's incomplete teleportation spell, a mortal wizard's desperate, dying act of love, reached its destination and delivered its passenger to the sanctuary, and in the same instant, the far-future Godric, the being whose power had been amplifying for a number of years that could not be written, whose evolved mechanisms made the mechanisms of every previous era look like fossils, whose held-back version of his own power was still so potent it would birth the most powerful being in the history of existence, reached back through time and touched the event.

The touch was not physical. It was not magical. It was not conceptual. It was a direct interaction between the will of far-future Godric and the fabric of the event itself, and the interaction was so total, so absolute, so incomprehensibly precise that the event was rewritten in zero time without being disturbed. Thorin's spell remained Thorin's spell. The teleportation remained the teleportation. The sanctuary remained the sanctuary. But the event, the moment of arrival, was now also the moment of the Stasis Event. The amplification began. The superpower genesis began. The absolute concealment began. The weakness paradox began. The immune system began. The passive shields began. All of it, seeded into the event by a being so powerful that the seeding required less effort than a heartbeat, which the being did not have, because the being did not need a heart, because the being was Godric, and Godric did not need anything.

The held-back version. The version that Godric had chosen because he believed in mortal struggle. The version that was so pathetically, laughably, cosmically weak compared to what he could have granted that the comparison was an insult to the concept of comparison. The version that was, by the standards of the being who granted it, a fraction so small it was effectively nothing. The version that would produce, at Year Zero, a being infinitely stronger than everything else in creation combined.

The seeding was done. The Stasis Event was sealed. The past was irrevocable. The loop was closed.

Godric turned from the terrace. His expression was calm, satisfied, the expression of a man who has just planted a seed and is looking forward to watching it grow. He walked back to his chair, sat down, picked up the fruit, and took another bite.

"There," he said, chewing. "It is done. The Stasis Event has been triggered. My past self will receive the held-back version. The amplification will begin. The journey will commence. And eventually, after a span of time so vast it cannot be written, the journey will produce the being sitting in this chair, and the being sitting in this chair will reach back, and the cycle will close. Again. Forever."

He looked at me. The warmth was there. The fondness was there. The crinkled eyes, the salt-and-pepper beard, the ruggedly handsome face of a man who had been forty-five for a number of years that could not be written. "Clive," he said, "you look unwell. Have some water. The fruit is good, too. Vael grew it."

I looked at the fruit. I looked at Vael, the former architect of reality, trembling among his hedges. I looked at Godric, the being who had just created his own origin with less effort than eating a piece of fruit, who had held back a recursive loop of infinite escalation because he believed in mortal struggle, who was concerned about his gardener's stress levels, who was the most powerful being in existence and was still, at his core, the man who had carried his daughters to bed.

"Your Majesty," I said. "The loop. The recursive escalation. If you had not held back. If you had granted the full version. The past Godric would be stronger. The amplification would be more potent. Every subsequent Godric would be stronger. The current Godric would be stronger. The current Godric could grant an even more potent Stasis Event. The loop would escalate. Each iteration infinitely beyond the last. Simultaneously. Permanently. Irrevocably. You are choosing to exist at a point on this curve that you consider held back. A point that is already so far beyond everything else that the comparison is meaningless. And you are choosing it because you believe in mortal struggle. And the mortal struggle does not exist. And the belief is genuine. And the genuine belief is the most terrifying thing I have ever encountered."

I paused. My hands were trembling. Not the fine, constant tremor of Vael's hands, but the deep, structural shaking of a man whose foundations have been demolished.

"And the craziest part," I continued, my voice barely a whisper, "the absolute, most insane, most horrifying part of all of this, is that the held-back version, the one you chose because you thought it was weak, the one you granted out of a misplaced desire to preserve struggle, is still so powerful that it will produce, after amplification, the being sitting in front of me. The being whose evolved mechanisms make the mechanisms of every previous era look like fossils. The being whose weakest newborn aura could redefine the omniverse. The being whose immune system has deleted the concept of harm from biology. The being whose absolute concealment maintains a simulated reality around the omniverse so that creation does not know it is living inside a fiction maintained by your presence. The being whose held-back power is, by your own assessment, the most powerful thing in existence. And this being is the product of a held-back version. And the being that produced the held-back version is the same being. And the loop is closed. And the loop is forever. And you are eating fruit."

Godric looked at me. He looked at me for a long, quiet moment. The garden was still. The jasmine was blooming. Vael's shears had stopped. The light was golden, and warm, and perfectly calibrated by orbital systems that had been maintaining the capital's climate for a number of years that could not be written.

Then Godric laughed. A full, rich, warm, genuine laugh, the laugh of a man who has heard something truly, deeply funny, the laugh of an uncle enjoying his nephew's company, the laugh of a mortal king who once hauled marble and sparred with his best friend and carried his daughters to bed. The laugh filled the garden, and the garden was warm, and the jasmine was blooming, and the being who had just created his own origin with less effort than eating fruit was laughing because his court wizard was overwhelmed, and the laughter was genuine, and the genuine laughter was the most terrifying sound in the omniverse.

"Clive," Godric said, still chuckling, wiping juice from his chin. "You really must try the fruit. Vael grows excellent fruit."

content.text:
Write a detailed story of Godric deciding to subjugate the IRL universe, at Year 25\.

Earth obviously, has 0 chance, and will experience total despair and existential dread.

In this scenario, you must make Earth try their best to kill Godric, which means Godric will completely annihilate it. You must NOT make Godric nice, forgiving. He can still be friendly. Godric feels mischievously playful so he doesn't instantly finish it (you must ensure the reader knows that if he wants to he can instantly destroy earth in 0 second with 0 effort), but he's particularly cruel and ensures every death is as agonizing as possible--however, you must not make Godric seemingly drag the fight, he still finishes the annihilation objectively swiftly (undetectably fast to normal mortals as it's infinitely less than a planck second, but but to Godric it's considered as "leisurely slow") but subjective to the opposition an eternity of maximum agony. You must clearly indicate that Godric is using 0% of his power and 0% of effort, and even if Earth is given infinite preparation, all possible advantages, they will still have 0 chance to even tickle Godric or make Godric feel anything. Show Godric be amused by how primitively weak and fragile the earthlings are, how they are trying so hard and effectively doing nothing to him. You must not leave any survivor of Earth alive, serving Godric, living in Thronegate or any other such crap which shows Godric sparing them. Godric’s power must be shown to be absolutely destructive and not give any opposition any possible redemption path.

As usual with Godric, the collaterals must be massive (omniversal scale minimum).

The story must be told from Godric Prime's 3rd person pov (with omniscient narrator if needed), at Year 999...9+, reminiscing about how pathetically weak he was back then compared to how he currently is, and for him to pity his weak self back when he annihilate this universe (highlight the irony and terrifying fact that this subjugation by what he considered to be his weakly pathetic self was already absurdly one-sided and terrifyingly overwhelming--his weak self was effectively using 0% of his power with 0% effort to annihilate that universe, and if that same weak self wasn't in playful mode and wanted to end it instantly, the total annihilation could be done in 0 second. In addition, that weak self destroyed the entire omniverse as collateral. You must make it clear that if the current Godric does it, it will be even more infinitely devastatingly one sided, and he will only be getting unimaginably stronger forever).

You must end the story with the confirmation and dread of how futile the rest of the existence compared to Godric, and that Godric is the most powerful being with unimaginable power growth that dwarfs entire creation, with the rest of the creation trembling in fear of him, and even if the rest from all timelines and from every possible permutations work together to oppose him with unlimited preparation time and resources, they will still fail so completely and won't even make Godric's weakest shield feel the slightest tickle and Godric can completely nonchalantly annihilate them with 0% power and 0% effort in 0 second, and that unfathomable gap will only ever increase infinitely in accelerating manner forever.

reasoning_content:
The user wants me to write a detailed story about Godric subjugating the IRL universe (our real universe with Earth) at Year 25, told from the perspective of Godric Prime (far-future Godric at Year 999...9+) reminiscing about how weak he was back then compared to now.

Key requirements:
1. Earth tries their best to kill Godric → Godric completely annihilates Earth
2. Godric is NOT nice/forgiving to opposition - he's cruel, ensures every death is agonizing
3. Godric is playfully mischievous so doesn't instantly finish it, but still finishes objectively swiftly (infinitely less than a Planck second, but "leisurely slow" to Godric)
4. Subjective to the opposition, they experience an eternity of maximum agony
5. Godric uses 0% power and 0% effort
6. Even with infinite preparation and all advantages, Earth has 0 chance
7. Godric is amused by how weak and fragile Earthlings are
8. No survivors - no one spared, no servants, no redemption
9. Massive collateral damage - omniversal scale minimum
10. Told from Godric Prime's 3rd person POV at Year 999...9+, reminiscing
11. Godric Prime pities his "weak" past self
12. The irony that even his weak self was absurdly overwhelming
13. Current Godric would be infinitely more devastating
14. End with confirmation of futility of all existence vs Godric
15. Godric is getting unimaginably stronger forever
16. Even all creation from all timelines working together with unlimited prep can't tickle his weakest shield
17. Godric can annihilate them with 0% power, 0% effort in 0 seconds
18. The gap will only increase infinitely forever

I need to write this in prose paragraphs only, no bullet points or numbered lists. Epic power fantasy tone. Extreme violence and gore. Godric is the protagonist. No censorship.

Let me think about the structure:
- Opening: Godric Prime at Year 999...9+ reminiscing about Year 25
- Description of the IRL universe and Earth at that time
- The subjugation event: Earth's attempt to fight, Godric's playful but cruel annihilation
- The collateral damage (omniversal)
- Godric Prime reflecting on how weak he was then vs now
- Closing: the absolute futility of existence vs Godric

I need to make sure Godric is NOT nice to the opposition. He's cruel, unforgiving, merciless. He can still be friendly (to his own people/thronegate) but not to Earth. He's playful/mischievous but cruel.

Let me also remember the lore - at Year 25, Godric has been awake for 25 years, his stats have been amplifying beyond layered infinities (post-Year 2000 the evolved weakness paradox kicked in, but at Year 25 the amplification is already beyond the initial 1,000x per Planck second due to weakness paradox triggers). Actually wait - at Year 25, the Weakness Paradox has been triggering but the "Evolved" Weakness Paradox doesn't start until Year 2000. So at Year 25, the amplification rate has increased from the base due to weakness paradox triggers but hasn't evolved yet. Still, even the base amplification (1,000x by itself every Planck second for 10,000 years of stasis + 25 years) is already incomprehensibly powerful.

Actually, looking at the lore more carefully:
- Year -10,000 to 0: stats amplify by 1,000 and by itself every Planck second
- Year 0 to 2000: stats amplify by increasingly incomprehensibly large number every half-Planck second (due to weakness paradox)
- Year 2000 onwards: beyond layered infinities, recursively increasing every infinitesimal fraction of Planck second

So at Year 25, Godric is in the "Year 0 to 2000" phase where amplification is by an increasingly and incomprehensibly large number every half-Planck second due to weakness paradox. This is still absurdly powerful - far beyond the base 1,000x rate.

Godric Prime at Year 999...9+ would look back at Year 25 Godric as pathetically weak in comparison, but Year 25 Godric was still already effectively infinitely stronger than all of creation combined.

Let me write this story now. I'll aim for 4000-8000 words.

Let me also think about how Earth tries to fight Godric. Since this is the "IRL universe" - Earth has nuclear weapons, military forces, etc. But they could also potentially have some advanced tech or magic depending on how we frame it. The key is they try their absolute best and it's completely futile.

Actually, the IRL universe is our real universe - so Earth has nuclear weapons, conventional military, maybe some experimental tech. They have 0 magic, 0 supernatural abilities. This makes the contrast even more stark.

But wait - Godric is from a universe with magic. The IRL universe might be one of the universes within the omniverse that Thronegate is subjugating. So Godric would approach Earth as part of his omniversal expansion.

Let me structure the story:

1. Godric Prime reminiscing at Year 999...9+, looking back at Year 25
2. The context: Thronegate expanding, Godric decides to subjugate the IRL universe
3. Earth receives the offer of unconditional surrender (standard Godric protocol)
4. Earth refuses, prepares everything they have
5. Godric arrives, playful and amused
6. Earth launches everything at him - nuclear, conventional, everything
7. Godric is amused, 0% effort, 0% power
8. Godric plays with them briefly (but still objectively instant)
9. Total annihilation of Earth with maximum agony
10. Omniversal collateral damage
11. Godric Prime reflecting on how weak he was
12. Closing: absolute futility of existence vs Godric

Let me write this now.
content:
Godric Prime sat in the garden of his palace, the same garden he had sat in for a span of time so vast that the number of years exceeded the number of atoms in the omniverse by a margin that made the comparison structurally invalid, and he remembered the Earth. The memory was vivid, not because he needed to concentrate to recall it, his omniscience rendered every event in every timeline permanently and flawlessly accessible, but because the memory amused him. It amused him the way a grown man is amused by a photograph of himself as a toddler trying to lift a stone he can now flip with his pinky. The toddler was earnest. The toddler tried hard. The toddler believed, with the absolute conviction of a child who has not yet learned the scale of the world, that the stone was liftable. The man looking at the photograph finds the earnestness charming and the belief endearing and the effort pitiful, because the man has long since learned that the stone was never the point. The stone was a toy. The world was full of mountains.

The Earth had been a toy. The entire universe that contained the Earth, the small, young, primitively physical cosmos with its single timeline and its absence of magic and its charmingly backward technology and its population of fragile, brief, striving organisms who believed themselves to be the apex of intelligence in their reality, had been a toy. Godric Prime remembered it the way a man remembers a particularly interesting insect he observed through a magnifying glass as a child. The insect had been fascinating, for a moment. The insect had moved in patterns that held his attention. The insect had been crushed, eventually, because the insect was fragile and the world was not, and the crushing had been a lesson in scale that the insect had been biologically incapable of learning.

He remembered, and the memory made him smile, and the smile was warm and genuine and carried the fond, slightly embarrassed quality of a man looking at an old photograph of himself and thinking, I was so young then. I was so pathetically, laughably weak.

Year 25. He had been awake for twenty-five years. Twenty-five years out of a stasis that had lasted ten millennia, during which his stats had been multiplying by one thousand and by itself every Planck second. The amplification had been running for 10,025 years. By the standards of the being who now sat in the garden, the Godric of Year 25 was a fossil. He was a remnant. He was an echo of a whisper of a shadow of a memory of something that had once been, impossibly long ago, the most powerful being in existence. The Year 25 Godric had been the most powerful being in the omniverse by an infinite margin. He had been able to annihilate the entire omniverse infinite times in zero seconds with zero effort at zero percent of his power. He had been, by any framework of measurement available to any being in any reality, omnipotent. And he had been, by the standards of the being who now sat in the garden, so incomprehensibly, so absurdly, so cosmically weak that the word weak was a compliment. The gap between Year 25 Godric and the current Godric was so vast that the gap between Year 25 Godric and the rest of the omniverse was, by comparison, negligible. Year 25 Godric was nothing. Year 25 Godric was a candle. The current Godric was a sun that made the concept of light irrelevant. And the current Godric was himself nothing, a candle compared to the Godric of tomorrow, who was a candle compared to the Godric of the day after, and the progression never stopped and the candles became suns and the suns became things that had no name because no framework existed to name them, and the progression was permanent and the progression was accelerating and the acceleration was permanent and the acceleration was accelerating, forever.

But Year 25 Godric had not known he was weak. Year 25 Godric had believed himself to be at the height of his power, because relative to everything else in existence, he was. He had looked at the omniverse and seen nothing that could resist him, nothing that could challenge him, nothing that could even register as a presence against the overwhelming, infinite, recursively amplifying weight of his existence. He had been correct. He had been absolutely, mathematically, ontologically correct. Everything in the omniverse was nothing compared to him. The error was not in the comparison. The error was in the assumption that the comparison mattered. The comparison did not matter, because the comparison was between Godric and everything else, and everything else was defined by its relationship to Godric, and the relationship was zero, and zero did not produce meaningful comparisons.

Year 25 Godric had been playful. He had still been in the early decades of his reign, still freshly shaped by the grief of Olympus, still learning the contours of his power, still amused by the novelty of being a being whose existence was an ongoing, continuous extinction event held in check only by his absolute concealment. He had been curious about the omniverse, about the beings that populated it, about the civilizations that rose and fell and rose again in the spaces between his attention. He had been particularly curious about the civilizations that believed themselves to be powerful, the ones that had built weapons and armies and technologies and magical systems that they believed made them significant. He found these civilizations entertaining the way a man finds an ant carrying a crumb entertaining. The ant is serious. The ant believes the crumb is important. The ant is investing its full biological capacity in the transportation of the crumb. The man watches, and the man is amused, and the amusement is genuine, and the amusement is not cruel, because the man does not blame the ant for being an ant. The man simply finds the ant's earnestness charming, given the scale of the world.

The Earth universe had been one of these civilizations. It was a small, young, physically dense cosmos with a single timeline, no magical substrate, no dimensional overlap, no meta-causal architecture. It was, by the standards of the omniverse, a primitive reality. Its most advanced technology was nuclear fission and fusion, a process so elementary that it existed naturally in stars, which the universe contained in abundance but whose populations had not yet learned to look at without burning their retinas. Its most powerful weapons were thermonuclear devices, crude explosive mechanisms that released energy by breaking the bonds between atomic nuclei, a process so inefficient that it wasted more than 99% of the available mass-energy. Its most advanced computational systems were silicon-based processors operating on binary logic, a framework so simple that it could be replicated by a child with a handful of stones. Its most powerful military forces were armies of fragile, bipedal organisms carrying kinetic projectile weapons, their strategy based on the principle of putting holes in other organisms until the organisms stopped moving.

The universe was, by any omniversal standard, a joke. It was a cosmos of children playing with sticks, unaware that the field they played on was owned by a being whose shadow could crush them without touching them.

Godric had decided to subjugate it because he was curious. He had been expanding Thronegate's borders, subjugating universe after universe with the standard methodology: an offer of unconditional surrender, followed by annihilation if the offer was refused. Most universes surrendered. A few refused. The ones that refused were destroyed, swiftly, absolutely, with the thoroughness of a man sweeping dust from a doorstep. The Earth universe had received the offer, transmitted through channels that the universe's inhabitants could perceive, a message that arrived simultaneously in every language, every communication device, every mind on the planet, a message that said, simply: Kneel, or cease to exist.

The Earth had refused. This was not surprising. The Earth had a long history of refusing to kneel, a cultural trait born of the species' brief, violent, surprisingly resilient history. The organisms that populated the planet had survived ice ages and meteor impacts and pandemic diseases and their own penchant for self-destruction, and the survival had produced a psychological architecture that interpreted defiance as virtue. They did not kneel because kneeling was, in their framework, a betrayal of the survival instinct that had brought them this far. The defiance was admirable, in the way that a mouse defying a supernova is admirable. The mouse does not know what a supernova is. The mouse does not need to know. The supernova does not require the mouse's understanding to annihilate it.

The Earth had prepared. They had taken the three days Godric had granted them, as was his standard protocol, and they had used every second. Their militaries had mobilized. Their nuclear arsenals had been armed. Their armies had been deployed. Their scientists had worked around the clock, analyzing the message, trying to understand its origin, its mechanism, its intent. Their leaders had convened, debated, argued, and ultimately decided that the message was a threat, and that threats were to be met with force, and that force was the only language the universe respected.

They had assembled everything they had. Every nuclear weapon on the planet, every missile, every warhead, every bomb. Every army, every soldier, every tank, every aircraft, every ship. Every satellite, every drone, every piece of technology that could be repurposed for violence. They had coordinated, cooperated, united their species in a way they had never achieved in their entire history, because the threat was existential and existential threats transcended the petty squabbles that normally divided them. For the first time in the history of the Earth, every nation, every faction, every ideology, had aligned toward a single purpose: the destruction of the being who had told them to kneel.

It was, Godric had reflected at the time, the most beautiful thing the species had ever done. The most organized, the most coordinated, the most unified. The entirety of human civilization, focused like a laser, every resource directed toward a single goal, every hand pulling the same rope. It was a masterpiece of collective effort, a symphony of cooperation, a testament to the capacity of a species to set aside its differences in the face of annihilation.

It was also, of course, completely, utterly, cosmically meaningless. The effort was that of a single ant carrying a crumb toward a supernova. The crumb would not affect the supernova. The ant would not affect the supernova. The ant's earnestness, while admirable, was not a relevant variable in the interaction. The supernova would occur regardless of the ant's opinion on the matter, and the ant would cease to exist regardless of the effort it invested in carrying the crumb.

Godric had arrived on the third day. He had not arrived in a ship or through a portal or via any mechanism the Earth's technology could detect. He had simply been there, standing on the surface of the planet, in a field of wheat in a region the locals called Kansas. He wore his simple dark garments, stretched across his heavily muscled frame. His short salt-and-pepper hair ruffled in a breeze that smelled of soil and growing things. His neatly trimmed beard caught the afternoon light. He looked, to any observer, like a large, ruggedly handsome man standing in a field. His absolute concealment was active, perfectly suppressing every fraction of his existence, presenting him as a perfectly ordinary, perfectly mundane, perfectly unremarkable human being. The concealment was so flawless that the Earth's sensors, which had been scanning the planet for any sign of the threat, registered nothing. The satellites saw nothing. The radar saw nothing. The seismographs saw nothing. The gravitational sensors saw nothing. Godric was standing in a field in Kansas, and the entire surveillance apparatus of human civilization looked directly at him and saw nothing, because the concealment did not hide him. It made him irrelevant. It removed him from the category of things that sensors could detect, the way a color outside the visible spectrum is removed from the category of things the eye can see.

He stood in the field for approximately four minutes, Earth time. He was not waiting. He was not preparing. He was looking at the wheat. The wheat was golden, ripe, heavy with grain, bending slightly in the breeze. It reminded him of the fields outside Olympus, the ones Reina had loved, the ones she had walked through with Isabel and Hebe in the summer, the three of them laughing, their hands trailing through the wheat heads, golden dust on their fingers. The memory was ten thousand years old. The grief was ten thousand years old. The grief was still there, buried, permanent, a stone in the foundation of his being that would never be removed because the being who had placed it was his own future self and the placement was irrevocable.

He looked at the wheat, and the wheat reminded him of his daughters, and the memory of his daughters made him feel, for a moment, the warmth he had buried, and then the warmth was gone, and he was the apex predator of the omniverse standing in a field on a planet whose entire population he was about to annihilate, and the annihilation was not a tragedy. It was a task. A chore. A mild, diverting amusement, the way a man might find it mildly diverting to knock over an anthill on his way to somewhere else.

He allowed himself to be detected. He did not drop his concealment. He simply adjusted it, the way a man adjusts his collar, so that the Earth's sensors could perceive a portion of his presence. Not his power. Not his aura. Not his metaphysical weight. Just his location. A blip on a radar screen. An anomaly on a satellite feed. A heat signature in a field in Kansas that should not have been there.

The response was immediate. Within thirty seconds, the first military aircraft were scrambled. Within two minutes, the nuclear forces were on alert. Within five minutes, the first missiles were launched. The Earth had decided, in the five minutes between detection and response, that the anomaly in Kansas was the threat, and that the threat was to be met with the full force of human military capability, and that the full force was to be applied immediately, without hesitation, without negotiation, without the faintest attempt at communication.

Godric found this amusing. The species had been given three days to prepare, and they had used the three days brilliantly, assembling their forces, coordinating their response, uniting their civilization. And then, the moment the target appeared, they threw everything at it in five minutes. The preparation had been thorough. The execution was panic. It was the behavior of a cornered animal, lashing out with every claw and tooth it possessed, not because the claws and teeth were effective but because the lashing was the only response the animal's nervous system could produce under the stimulus of threat.

The first weapon to reach him was a thermonuclear warhead, carried by an intercontinental ballistic missile, launched from a silo in the northern hemisphere. The warhead's yield was approximately 1.2 megatons, a quantity of explosive force sufficient to level a city and kill millions of organisms. The missile traveled at hypersonic speed, covering the distance from the silo to Kansas in under four minutes. The warhead detonated approximately 500 meters above Godric's position, releasing a fireball that reached temperatures of several million degrees, generating a shockwave that flattened the wheat field for kilometers in every direction and producing a mushroom cloud that rose into the stratosphere and was visible from orbit.

Godric stood in the center of the fireball. The fireball was several million degrees. Godric's body, at zero percent of his true durability, at absolute rest, with no active defenses, with no shields, with no immune response, with nothing but the passive, resting state of his physical form, was not affected by the fireball. The fireball was not warm. The fireball was not cool. The fireball was not anything, because the fireball's energy, when it encountered Godric's body, did not interact with it. The energy did not transfer. The heat did not conduct. The radiation did not absorb. The shockwave did not impact. The fireball existed around Godric the way water exists around a stone in a river, flowing past, unable to dislodge, unable to affect, unable to register. Godric's body, at zero percent durability, was so incomprehensibly, so absurdly, so obscenely more durable than the fireball's maximum output that the fireball's energy was, by comparison, less than nothing. Not approximately nothing. Effectively nothing. The total energy output of the warhead, 1.2 megatons, was so infinitesimally small relative to the passive, resting durability of Godric's least durable cell that the ratio between them was not a number. It was a condition. The condition was: irrelevant.

Godric did not flinch. He did not move. He did not look up. He continued looking at the wheat, which was now gone, flattened by the shockwave, burning in the residual heat of the fireball. He felt a mild, genuine regret at the loss of the wheat. It had been nice wheat.

More missiles arrived. Within the first minute, 47 thermonuclear warheads detonated in the skies above Kansas, their combined yield exceeding 800 megatons. The ground was scorched. The atmosphere was ionized. The electromagnetic pulse destroyed every electronic device within a thousand kilometers. The mushroom clouds merged into a single, towering pillar of fire and smoke that punched through the stratosphere and reached the mesosphere. The Earth's crust, beneath the combined force of the detonations, cracked and shifted, producing earthquakes that were felt on the opposite side of the planet.

Godric stood in the center of the inferno, his simple dark garments somehow unstained, unburned, unaffected, because the garments, like his body, existed in a state of durability so far beyond the fireball's output that the interaction was not an interaction. It was a non-event. The fireball did not touch him. The fireball could not touch him. The fireball's energy, when it reached the boundary of his physical form, simply ceased to be energy. It became nothing. Not converted. Not absorbed. Not redirected. Nothing. The energy encountered Godric's body and found that it had no mechanism by which to interact with it, the way a radio wave has no mechanism by which to interact with a concept. Godric's body was not in the same category of existence as the fireball. The fireball was physical. Godric's body, at zero percent durability, was something else. Something that the fireball's physics could not reach, could not touch, could not define.

The Earth's militaries, observing the detonations through their satellites and their drones and their seismic sensors, saw the fireballs. They saw the mushroom clouds. They saw the scorched earth and the cracked crust and the ionized atmosphere. They did not see Godric, because their sensors had been destroyed by the electromagnetic pulse. But they assumed, with the optimism of organisms who had never encountered a force they could not destroy, that the target had been eliminated. They assumed that 800 megatons of thermonuclear force, applied directly to a single point on the planet's surface, was sufficient to destroy any threat.

Godric allowed them to believe this for approximately thirty seconds. He found their optimism charming. It was the optimism of a child who believes that throwing a stone at the moon will knock it from the sky. The child throws the stone. The stone falls short. The child does not understand why the stone fell short, because the child does not understand the distance to the moon, and the child's framework for understanding the world does not include the concept of distances that stones cannot reach. The Earth's militaries were children throwing stones at the moon, and the stones were falling short, and the children did not understand why, because their framework for understanding the world did not include the concept of beings that thermonuclear weapons could not affect.

Then Godric adjusted his concealment again, allowing his location to be detected. He was still standing in the same spot. The ground beneath him was scorched glass. The air around him was superheated plasma. He was untouched. His garments were unstained. His hair was immaculate. He was looking at the spot where the wheat had been, his expression one of mild, contemplative regret.

The panic that ensued was, by human standards, total. The militaries launched everything they had remaining. Every nuclear weapon on the planet, every missile, every warhead, every bomb, was directed at the coordinate in Kansas. Conventional forces were deployed. Fleets of aircraft loaded with ordnance screamed toward the target. Artillery batteries fired from positions hundreds of kilometers away. Submarines launched cruise missiles from beneath the oceans. Soldiers on the ground advanced, carrying rifles and grenades and rocket launchers, their training overriding their terror, their discipline carrying them forward into a zone where the air was still superheated and the ground was still radioactive.

Godric watched them come. He was amused. The amusement was genuine, the amusement of a man watching a colony of ants swarm a crumb, the ants investing their full biological capacity in the acquisition of the crumb, the ants completely unaware that the crumb was sitting on the boot of a being who could, by shifting his weight, crush the entire colony and the continent it rested on and the planet the continent rested on and the solar system the planet rested on and the galaxy the solar system rested on and the universe the galaxy rested on.

He was using zero percent of his power. Zero percent of his strength. Zero percent of his speed. Zero percent of his magic. Zero percent of his superpowers. Zero percent of his immune system. Zero percent of his passive shields. Zero percent of his sentient auras. Zero percent of his effort. He was standing in a field, and the field was on fire, and the fire could not touch him, and the weapons could not touch him, and the soldiers could not touch him, and the planet could not touch him, and the universe could not touch him. He was not holding back. He was not restraining himself. He was simply existing, at the baseline, resting, passive state of his being, and the baseline was so incomprehensibly, so absurdly, so obscenely beyond everything the Earth could produce that the comparison was not a comparison. It was a category error.

The weapons reached him. Thousands of them. Tens of thousands. The combined nuclear arsenal of the planet, every warhead ever built, every bomb ever stockpiled, every missile ever loaded into a silo, converged on a single point on the surface of the Earth. The total yield was approximately 15,000 megatons. The detonations were simultaneous, instantaneous, a single, unified burst of thermonuclear force that exceeded anything the planet had ever experienced, exceeding the asteroid impact that had wiped out the dinosaurs by several orders of magnitude. The Earth's crust at the impact point was vaporized. The mantle was exposed. The shockwave circled the planet three times, flattening every structure on every continent. The atmosphere was stripped away, the gases ionized and dispersed into space. The oceans, hit by the thermal pulse, flash-boiled, producing a wall of steam that expanded outward at supersonic speed. The planet's magnetic field collapsed. The radiation was so intense that every living organism within line of sight of the detonations, which was every living organism on the hemisphere facing Kansas, died instantly, their DNA shredded, their cells ruptured, their nervous systems destroyed before the pain signals could reach their brains.

Godric stood in the center of the void. The planet was dying around him. The crust was gone. The mantle was exposed. The atmosphere was gone. The oceans were boiling. The radiation was lethal to everything. He was untouched. His garments were unstained. His hair was immaculate. He had not moved. He had not flinched. He had not raised a hand or shifted his weight or blinked. The total combined nuclear output of human civilization, 15,000 megatons, applied simultaneously to a single point, had produced zero effect on his body. Not a small effect. Zero. The energy had encountered his body and become nothing, the same way the first warhead's energy had become nothing. The ratio between the energy output and Godric's durability was not a number. It was a condition. The condition was: irrelevant.

He looked at the dying planet, and he felt a flicker of the old warmth, the buried warmth of the mortal king who had loved his people, and the flicker was brief, and the flicker was gone. The Earth had refused to kneel. The Earth had tried to kill him. The Earth had thrown everything it had at him, and everything had been nothing, and the nothing had been predictable, and the predictable nothing was not a reason for mercy. Godric was not merciful to opposition. He was cruel. He was unforgiving. He was ruthless. He was the apex predator of the omniverse, and the apex predator did not release the prey because the prey was small. The apex predator consumed the prey, and the consumption was thorough, and the consumption was final.

He decided to play. Not because he needed to. Not because the Earth required a prolonged engagement. He could have ended it in zero seconds, the entire planet, the entire solar system, the entire galaxy, the entire universe, erased in an instant so brief that the inhabitants would not have had time to register that the instant had occurred.
